name: HMS Unrivalled (P 45)
events: The history of HMS P 45 / Unrivalled as compiled on this page is extracted from this submarines patrol reports and logbooks. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in February 2018.

2 May 1942 Around 1230 B/2, P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and P 222 (Lt. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) departed their builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)
3 May 1942 Around 1515 A/3, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a work-up period.
She had been turned over to the Navy after successful completion of her acceptance trails a little over an hour before. She had also conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh. (2)
5 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted speed trials on the measured half mile and also independent exercises in the Clyde area. (2)
6 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted speed trials conducted speed trials, surfaced and submerged, on the measured half mile and also deep dive trials in the Clyde area. (2)
7 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (2)
8 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to the torpedo firing range at Arrochar. (2)
9 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (2)
10 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (2)
11 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) proceeded from Arrochar to Holy Loch. (2)
15 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) proceeded to the Ardrossan area for night exercises with special forces. During these exercises she was escorted by HMS La Flore (?). (2)
16 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS La Flore (?). (2)
17 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS La Flore (?) as target. (2)
18 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted gunnery exercises in the Clyde area. (2)
19 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil (2)
20 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted D/G trails off Helensburgh and trials (tests) in lower Loch Long. (2)
21 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Kames Bay where she was docked in AFD 7 for a propeller change. (2)

22 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) is undocked at Kames Bay. She then conducted gunnery exercises in the Clyde area on a target that was towed by HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR). These were followed by practice attacks during which HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) served as target and finally night exercises with special forces. (2)
23 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS La Flore (?) acted as target. (2)
25 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh and speed trials / log calibration trials on the measured mile in Loch Long. (2)
26 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) acted as target. (2)
27 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Campbeltown. (2)
28 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Spaniel (?) and HMS Bedlington (Skr. J.H.D. Dansie, RNR). (2)

29 May 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS L'Incomprise (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) F.S. Deveson, RNR) and HMS Carina (Lt. R.A.H. Lord, RNR). Upon completion of these exercises HMS P 45 proceeded to Holy Loch. (2)
31 May 1942 At 1400 B/31, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. She was escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (2)
2 Jun 1942 Shortly before 0800 B/2, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow from Holy Loch.

Late in the afternoon and early in the evening, P 45 participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. C.D. Maud, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Escapade (Lt.Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN). (3)

3 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Lewes (A/Cdr.(Retd.) L.H. Phillips, RN) and HMS Wheatland (Lt. R.deL. Brooke, DSC, RN) and later with two ML's (not identified). (3)

4 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. J.J. Youngs, OBE, RD, RNR) and HMS Castleton (Lt.Cdr. R.H. Bristowe, DSO, RN) and later with three ML's (not identified). Also P 45 also carried out a practice attack on HMS Castleton. (3)

5 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow. During the morning with HMAS Nepal (Cdr. F.B. Morris, RAN) and HrMs Tjerk Hiddes (Lt.Cdr. W.J. Kruys, RNethN) and in the afternoon with and in the afternoon with HMS Oakley (Lt.Cdr. T.A. Pack-Beresford, RN) and ORP Slazak (Kmdr ppor. (Lt.Cdr.) R. Nalecz-Tyminski, ORP). (3)

6 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. J.J. Youngs, OBE, RD, RNR) and HMS Ledbury (Lt.Cdr. R.P. Hill, RN) and later with HMAS Nepal (Cdr. F.B. Morris, RAN) and HrMs Tjerk Hiddes (Lt.Cdr. W.J. Kruys, RNethN). (3)
8 Jun 1942 The King inspected the naval base and four ratings from P 45 took part in the parade. (3)

9 Jun 1942 In the morning and afternoon, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Nigeria (Capt. J.G.L. Dundas, CBE, RN), later with HMAS Nepal (Cdr. F.B. Morris, RAN) and HrMs Tjerk Hiddes (Lt.Cdr. W.J. Kruys, RNethN) and finally with two A/S trawlers (not identified). P 45 also carried out a practice attack on HMAS Nepal. (3)

10 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS London (Capt. R.M. Servaes, CBE, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral L.H.K. Hamilton, DSO and Bar, RN). Later P 45 made rendezvous with HMS Stoke City (Lt. D.P. Piggford, RNR) and HMS Preston North End (Lt. K.A. Vasey, MBE, RNR) which escorted her during HMS P 45's gunnery exercises which were conducted off Scapa Flow. (3)

11 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with USS Mayrant (Cdr. C.C. Hartman, USN) and USS Rowan (Lt.Cdr. B.R. Harrison, Jr., USN). HMS P 45 also made a practice attack on one of these two destroyers. (3)

12 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. J.J. Youngs, OBE, RD, RNR) and ORP Slazak (Lt.Cdr. R. Nalecz-Tyminski, ORP) and later HMS Preston North End (Lt. K.A. Vasey, MBE, RNR) and HMS Davy (Skr. W.J. Salisbury, RNR). HMS P 45 also carried out a practice attacks on HMS Speedwell and HMS Preston North End. (3)

13 Jun 1942 In the morning and early afternoon, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMAS Nepal (Cdr. F.B. Morris, RAN) and HrMs Tjerk Hiddes (Lt.Cdr. W.J. Kruys, RNethN). HMS P 45 also carried out a practice attack on HMAS Nepal. (3)
15 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Scapa Flow with HMS Preston North End (Lt. K.A. Vasey, MBE, RNR) and aircraft. (3)

16 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Salamander (Lt. W.R. Muttram, RN) and HrMs Tjerk Hiddes (Lt.Cdr. W.J. Kruys, RNethN) and later with HMS Offa (Cdr. R.A. Ewing, DSC, RN) and HMAS Nepal (Cdr. F.B. Morris, RAN). HMS P 45 also carried out a practice attack on HMS Salamander. (3)

17 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Somali (Capt. J.W.M. Eaton, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Wilton (Lt. A.P. Northey, DSC, RN) and later with two local A/S craft (not identified). (3)

18 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted exercises with the local boom defence and in the afternoon participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS Stella Pegasi (T/Lt. F.S. Howell, RNR) and HMS Northman (Capt.(Retd.) N.M.F. Corbett, RN). (3)

19 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises at Scapa Flow, first with HMS Catterick (Lt. A. Tyson, RN) and HNoMS Glaisdale (?) and later with HMS Halcyon (T/A/Lt.Cdr. H. Harding, RNR) and HMS Salamander (Lt. W.R. Muttram, RN). (3)

20 Jun 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Scapa Flow, first with HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.K. Scott-Moncrieff, RN) and HMS Britomart (Lt.Cdr. S.S. Stammwitz, RN) and later with HMS Catterick (Lt. A. Tyson, RN) and HNoMS Glaisdale (?). (3)
24 Jun 1942 Around 0600 B/24, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Lerwick where she arrived at 1850 B/24. She was escorted by HMS Preston North End (Lt. K.A. Vasey, MBE, RNR). (3)
25 Jun 1942 Around 0900 B/25, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Lerwick for her 1st war patrol.
HMS P 45 was to patrol in zone 'K 43' to provide cover for convoy PQ 17. En route to that zone she was to pass through position 68°59'N, 08°01'E.

Patrol zones 'K 35' to 'K 44' were in an area 25 miles either side of a line joining positions 69.01'N, 07.58'E and 71.40'N, 17.20'E. This area was divided into ten zones each 25 miles long, numbered from south to north 'K 35' to 'K 44'.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 45 during this patrol see the map below.

26 Jun 1942 In the Norwegian Sea, in position 65°03'N, 03°27'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) fired a torpedo at what is thought to be a submerged enemy submarine that was proceeding on a course of 290°. Only the periscope was sighted and the torpedo was fired in the direction of the hydrophone effect.

The attack was most likely against a non-sub target (perhaps a whale). There is a remote possibility that the target was U-456 which was in the general area. [We have so far been unable to pinpoint the exact position of this submarine at the time of the attack as her KTB (log) is not in our collection. It should be available at the German Military Archives at Freiburg so if we ever end up there (which we hope), we will try to photograph it.]

2154 B/26 - Sighted a periscope bearing green 90° 1000 yards away. P 45 dived immediately. Hydrophone Effect (H.E.) was heard. As there was doubt about the identity of the submarine attempts were made to make contact by S.S./T. but there was no response.
2247 B/26 - Fired one torpedo at the contact. The torpedo was heard to explode after running its full range. Contact was lost soon after. (5)
27 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1154 B/27, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) ordered HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to patrol in area 'K 59'. (6)
28 Jun 1942 At 0405 B/28, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) passed through the ordered position, 68°59'N, 08°01'E. Course was then altered to 049° to proceed to area 'K 59'. (5)

30 Jun 1942 At 0440 B/30, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived in her assigned patrol area, 'K 59'. She commenced a diving patrol at 70 feet coming up of W/T routines and every half hour to check the visibility. (5)

4 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1704 B/4, the Flag officer submarines sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; At 2000 B/4, HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) move to positions 72°14'N, 24°59'E and 72°14'N, 23°32'E respectively. FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville), HMS Sturgeon (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) move to zones 'K 60', 'K 59', and 'K 58' respectively. Submarines are to make good approx. 25 miles every 5 hours and are to endeavour to remain unseen. (7)

5 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 0302 B/5, the Flag Officer Submarines the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; Cancel my signal timed 1704 B/4. HMS Sturgeon (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) leave patrol at 0600 B/5 and return to Lerwick by reverse of outward route. HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) establish new patrol positions as follows; P 54 in 71°28'N, 29°55'E, P 45 in 71°44'N, 28°45'E and P 212 in 71°52'N, 27°30'E. P 54 and P 45 should reach new positions ordered by 0200 B/6. P 212 as soon as practicable thereafter. Submarines are to endeavour to remain unseen. (7)

5 Jul 1942 At 2002 B/5, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the enemy report timed 1700 B/6 in which the Russian submarine K-21 reported Tirpitz, Admiral Scheer and eight destroyers in position 71°25'N, 23°40'E, steering course 045°. She claimed to have hit the Tirpitz with two torpedoes.
At 2026 B/5, an enemy report timed 1816 B/6 was received from an aircraft which reported eleven strange ships in position 71°31’N, 27°10’E steering 065°, speed 10 knots.

At 2151 B/5, HMS P 45 received HMS P 54's (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) contact report timed 2029 B/5 which contained the following; Tirpitz, Admiral Scheer and six destroyers sighted bearing 130° at a range of 10 miles. My estimated position is 71°41'N, 28°16'E, course and speed of enemy were 060° at 22 knots.
[The destroyers / torpedo boats with Tirpitz were were Z 4 / Richard Beitzen, Z 14 / Friedrich Ihn, Z 24, Z 27, Z 28, Z 29, Z 30 and the torpedo boats T 7 and T 15.] (8)

7 Jul 1942 At 1050 B/7, in position 71°54'N, 29°00'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 330° on a course of 110°. Range was 6 miles. It is thought this may have been either the Soviet submarine K-21 or a U-boat. P 45 closed to 4.5 miles before the submarine turned away and contact was lost.
At 1118 B/7, visual and Asdic contact was lost with the other submarine.
At 1804 B/7, course was altered to 346° to proceed in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0641 B/7. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 7 July 1942.]
At 2130 B/7, a submarine was sighted bearing Red 40° at a range of 6 miles. It was thought to be a British 'S'-class submarine.
At 2135 B/7, HMS P 45 dived and attempted to close submerged.
At 2148 B/7, HMS P 45 surfaced when the submarine was definitely recognised as a British 'S'-class.
At 2210 B/7, recognition signals were exchanged with HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN).
At 2350 B/7, HMS P 45 arrived in position 'B' (72°40'N, 28°01'E). Course was altered to 291° to proceed to position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E). (5)

7 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 0645 B/7, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to submarines on patrol covering convoys PQ 17 and QP 13; 1) HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR), HMS Seawolf (Lt. R.P. Raikes, RN) and HMS Trident (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, DSC, RN) leave patrol 1200 B/7 for position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E).

2) HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) leave patrol at 1800 B/7 through position 'B' (72°40'N, 28°01'E) thence to position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E).

3) From position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E), HMS Ursula, HMS Seawolf and HMS P 45 proceed through positions 'C' (71°31'N, 17°40'E), 'D' (68°49'N, 08°19'E to 'E' (089° - Outer Skerries - 6 miles), thence Lerwick.

4) From position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E), HMS Tribune, HMS Trudent, HMS P 212 and HMS P 54 proceed through positions 'F' (71°46'N, 16°59'E), 'G' (69°08'N, 07°38'E) thence through position 'E' ((089° - Outer Skerries - 6 miles) to Lerwick.
5) Submarines are to proceed on surface at Commanding Officers discretion at best speed compatible with fuel remaining and are to report their E.T.A. 36 hours in advance.
6) For the last 24 hours of her passage HMS Trident is to carry out endurance trials at 8 knots. (7)
8 Jul 1942 At 0218 B/8, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) was in position 'A' (72°50'N, 26°35'E). Course was now altered to 245°.
At 1940 B/8, HMS P 45 was in position 'C' (71°31'N, 17°40'E). Course was now altered to 230°. (5)

9 Jul 1942 At 0625 B/9, in position 70°30'N, 14°03'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted an unknown vessel bearing 220° at a range of 10 miles. P 45 closed submerged but lost visual touch at 0645 B/9 as visibility decreased. P 45 made an enemy report (timed 0625 B/9).
However at 0901 B/9, contact was made with HMS Seawolf ((Lt. R.P. Raikes, RN) which proved to be the vessel sighted at 0625 hours.
At 2335 B/9, HMS P 45 was in position 'D' (68°49'N, 08°19'E). Course was altered to 204° to proceed to position 'E' (089° - Outer Skerries - 6 miles). (5)
11 Jul 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) made two attacks on German U-boats.
In first attack four torpedoes were fired at U-510 but they all missed. The U-boat had observed the British submarine from a distance of 10000 metres and later sighted one of the torpedo tracks.
In second attack four rounds of 3" were fired at U-658 which had just been missed by four torpedoes from HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN).
1005 B/11 - Sighted submarine bearing 155°, range about 6 nautical miles. altered course to 175° too close.
1010 B/11 - Made the challenge. but received not the correct reply. The submarine subsequently identified as a German U-Boat.
1018 B/11 - In position 63°36'N, 02°20'E fired four torpedoes from about 5000 yards. The enemy dived and all torpedoes missed.
[U-510 reported the attack as taking place in Grid AF 4958 which is centred in position 63°57'N, 01°37.5'E.]
1020 B/11 - Dived.
1027 B/11 - Altered course to 130°. Went to 90 feet to reload.
1313 B/11 - Altered course to 290° and surfaced. Then altered course to 204°.
1338 B/11 - Sighted the conning tower of a submarine bearing 190°. Dived and attempted to close on course 220°. Could not see the submarine so at ......
1357 B/11 - Surfaced.

1359 B/11 - Sighted a U-boat bearing 275°. Full speed ahead. In position 63°25'N, 02°05'E fired 4 rounds of 3" the enemy from a range of 7000 yards. The enemy then dived. 1402 B/11 - Dived. 1508 B/11 - Surfaced. Sighted the U-boat at a distance of about 4.5 miles. Altered course to close. The enemy altered course away and increased speed.

[U-658 reported the position of this encounter as being in grid AF 4991 which is centred in position 63°51'N, 02°07'30"E. U-658 reported having surfaced at 1513 B/11 and on doing so the British submarine was sighted again but quite some distance away astern proceeding to the south-west.]
1542 B/11 - Sighted a second U-Boat bearing 175° which was soon lost to sight steering westwards.
1545 B/11 - Made the challenge but received no reply.
1555 B/11 - The enemy was outside gun and torpedo range. P 45 was unable to close.
1558 B/11 - Changed course to 200°. (9)
12 Jul 1942 Around 1515 B/12, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick. (5)
21 Jul 1942 At 0730 B/21, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Lerwick for Scapa Flow where she arrived around 2130 B/21. She was escorted by HMS Stoke City (Lt. D.P. Piggford, RNR). (10)
22 Jul 1942 At 0651 B/22, HMS Douglas (Lt.Cdr. R.B.S. Tennant, RN) collided with HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) causing some damage.
P 45 was then docked in AFD 12 later the same day to effect repairs. (10)
25 Jul 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) is undocked. She then made a trial dive in Scapa Flow. The trial dive was satisfactory. (10)
28 Jul 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) participate in a boom defence exercise at Scapa Flow. (10)
30 Jul 1942 Around 0600 B/30, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Stella Pegasi (T/Lt. F.S. Howell, RNR). (10)
31 Jul 1942 Around 2015 B/31, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Scapa Flow. (10)
3 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted special noise trials in the Clyde area. (11)
4 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted special noise trials in the Clyde area. (11)
5 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted Folbot exercises with special forces and special noise trials in the Clyde area. (11)
8 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) is docked at Ardrossan. (11)
22 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. A.G. Prideaux, RN) is undocked. (11)
29 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. A.G. Prideaux, RN) proceeded from Ardrossan to Holy Loch. (11)
30 Aug 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. A.G. Prideaux, RN) conducted deep diving trials in the Clyde area. (11)
1 Sep 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS La Flore ( T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.C. Hopkins, DSC, RNR) acted as target. (12)

4 Sep 1942 Around 0700 A/4, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick. They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (13)
6 Sep 1942 Around 0930 A/6, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Holy Loch. (13)
7 Sep 1942 At 1600 A/7, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Lerwick for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to provide cover for convoy operations (PQ 18 and QP 14) to and from Northern Russia.
P 45 was to patrol in zone 'K 62'. En-route to the patrol area she was to proceed through position 71°01'N, 10°01'E.
For the daily positions of HMS P 45 during this patrol see the map below.

8 Sep 1942 At 0427 A/8, north-east of the Shetland Islands, in position 61°48'N, 00°34'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted two merchant vessels bearing 340° at a range of 6 miles. Course of these merchant vessels, which were burning their navigation lights, was 115°. Lt. Turner closed to investigate.

At 0500 A/8, HMS P 45 dived. The second ship was identified as the Swedish Vingaren (3154 GRT, built 1936). The first ship appeared to be similar to the Swedish Annie Johnson (4896 GRT, built 1925). The ships passed ahead at a range of 4 miles. No further attempt was made to close them as the ships appeared to be those referred to in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2146 A/7. [This signal mentions the identify of the ship thought to be the Annie Johnson as the Margaret Johnson (5143 GRT, built 1928).] (5)
11 Sep 1942 Around noon, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived in her patrol area, zone 'K 62'. (5)

12 Sep 1942 At 1600 A/12, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 090° and she surfaced and proceeded at 6 knots in accordance with the orders in the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 1117 A/12. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 12 September 1942.]
At 1830 A/12, a submarine was sighted bearing 000° at a range of 8 miles.
Five minutes later the other submarine was identified as a British S-class submarine.
At 1935 A/12, HMS P 45 altered course to close the other submarine at 20 minutes later recognition signals were exchanged with HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN). (5)

12 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1117 A/12, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned in the signal; At 1600 A/12 submarines are to commence a move to occupy undermentioned zones at 0800 A/13. HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) to zone 'K 74'. HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) to zone 'K 73'. HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren) to zone 'K 72'. HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. 13 Sep 1942 At 0315 A/13, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 100 at a range of 4 miles. This was thought to be HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) again.
Which was indeed correct and at 0345 A/13, recognition was exchanged.
At 0610 A/13, HMS P 45 entered zone 'K 69'. (5)

G.R. Colvin, RN) to zone 'K 78'. HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) to zone 'K 77'. HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren) zone 'K 76' HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) to zone 'K 75'. HMS P 221 (Lt. 14 Sep 1942 At 0001 A/14, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 090° to comply with the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1114 A/13. Speed was set to 6 knots.
At 2245 A/14, course was altered to 107° to comply with the F.O.S. signal timed 1541 A/14. Speed was set to 6 knots. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 14 September 1942.] (5)

14 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1541 A/14, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following information / orders to the submarine mentioned in the signal; Estimated position of convoy PQ 18 at 0800 A/15 will be 75°55'N, 31°30'E. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) to zone 'K 78'. HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to zone 'K 77'. HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) to zone 'K 76'. (15)
15 Sep 1942 At 1030 A/15, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) entered zone 'K 77'. (5)

15 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1633 A/15, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren), HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) and HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. 16 Sep 1942 At 0800 A/16, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived in her new patrol zone as had been ordered in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1633 A/15. (5)

17 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 0036 A/17, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned in the signal; At 0800 A/17 submarines are to commence a move to occupy undermentioned zones at 0900 A/17. They were to be in position by 2100 A/17. HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) to zone 'K 82'. HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) to zone 'K 81'. HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren) to zone 'K 80'. HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) to zone 'K 79'. HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) to zone 'K 78'. HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to zone 'K 77'. HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) to zone 'K 76'.

In a signal timed 0039 A/17, the F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; Convoys crossed over about 2000 A/16. Estimated position of convoys at 0800 A/17, QP 14 in position 74°33'N, 48°30'E and PQ 18 in position 72°18'N, 46°50'E.

In a signal 2011 A/17, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; 0200 A/18 you are to commence a move so as to be in undermentioned zones by 1400 A/19. HMS Tigris to zone 'K 70'. HMS Tribune to zone 'K 69'. HMoMS Uredd to zone 'K 68'. HMS P 54 to zone 'K 67'. HMS P 221 to zone 'K 66'. HMS P 45 to zone 'K 65'. HMS Unique to zone 'K 64'. (15)
17 Sep 1942 At 0800 A/17, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 300° to comply with the orders in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0036 A/17.
At 2100 A/17, HMS P 45 commenced to patrol in zone 'K 77'. (5)
18 Sep 1942 At 0200 A/18, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 276° to comply with the orders in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2011 A/17. (5)

18 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 2327 A/18, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren), HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) and HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN); At 0001 A/20 you are to leave patrol and proceed to Lerwick as routed by the Capt. S.9.

In a signal timed 1155 A/19 the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to the submarine listed above; F.O.S. 2327 A/18. Submarines are to leave patrol for Lerwick through position 91° - Out Skerries - 5 miles through undermentioned positions as follows: 1) At 0001 A/20th; HMS UNIQUE through position 'A', 71°01'N, 08°59'E. HMS P 45 through position 'B', 70°59'N, 11°01'E. HMS P 221 through position 'C', 69°02'N, 08°01'E. HMS Tribune through position 'D', 71°39'N, 17°20'E thence through position 'C'. HMS Tigris through position 'E', 71°10'N, 17°58'E thence through position 'F', 68°40'N, 08°40'. 2) At 0400 A/20; HMS P 54 through position 'G', 71°55'N, 16°35'E, thence through position 'H', 69°20'N, 07°05'E. HNoMS Uredd through position 'D', thence through position 'F'. 3) Total bombing restriction will be arranged. 4) Submarines should proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion. (15)
19 Sep 1942 At 1550 A/19, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a British S-class submarine on the starboard beam at a range of 5.5 miles. Course was altered to close.
At 1556 A/19, the submarine was identified as HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and recognition signals were exchanged and both submarine proceeded on their way again. (5)
20 Sep 1942 At 0345 A/20, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted and identified HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) on her starboard beam.
At 1816 A/20, HMS P 45 was in position 'B' as had been ordered in the Capt. S.9's signal timed 1155 A/19. (5)
23 Sep 1942 At 2230 A/23, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Lerwick. (5)

24 Sep 1942 Around 0900 A/24, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Lerwick for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN). They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (12)
26 Sep 1942 Around 1245 A/26, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick.
[As no logs are available for this submarine from October 1942 up to November 1943, some details for these months may be missing.] (12)
11 Oct 1942 At 1700 A/11, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Holy Loch for passage to Gibraltar.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS P 511 (Lt. C.R. Pelly, RN). They were escorted by HMS Northern Pride (T/Lt. A.L.F. Bell, RNR).
P 221 and P 45 were to part company with the escort in position 'R', 240° - Wolf Rock - 3 miles at 1930 A/13.

P 45 was further routed as follows; Position 'D', 49°40'N, 07°05'W. Position 'E', 49°40'N, 12°31'W. She was to pass latitude 43°00'N, at Longitude 12°31'W at 2300 A/18. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.).
For the daily positions of HMS P 45 during this passage see the map below.

16 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0936 A/16 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); Onward routes as follows; 1) P 221 through position 'K', 39°04'N, 11°31'E at 0200 A/19, position 'L', 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 15 miles at 1930 A/19, position 'M', 36°32'N, 06°54'E at 0630 A/20, position 'N', 35°58'N, 05°59'E at 1200 A/20 to arrive at Gibraltar 1550 A/20. 2) P 45 through position 'K' at 0930 A/20, position 'L' at 1030 A/21, position 'M' at 0200 A/22, position 'N' at 0930 A/22 to arrive at Gibraltar 1350 A/22. 3) Both submarines are to proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion. Proceed unescorted from position 'M' to Gibraltar and report if unable to comply within 2 hours of these times. 4) Speed of advance, 10 knots for P 221 and 7 knots for P 45. 5) Total bombing restrictions arranged 20 miles either side route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. (16)

17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1324 A/17, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); Estimated positions of P 45 and P 221 at 1500 A/17, 45°25'N, 12°30'W and 44°34'N, 12°30'E respectively. Remain on patrol in your present position. (16)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1324 A/17, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); Patrol cancelled, P 45 and P 221 proceed forthwith on the surface to Gibraltar through position 43°00'N, 12°30'W, thence as previously ordered. (16)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0306 A/18, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); 1) Onward routes as in my signal timed 0936 A/16. 2) P 221 at average speed 8.5 knots passes position 'K' (39°04'N, 11°31'E) at 1600 A/19, position 'L' (225° - Cape St. Vincent - 15 miles) at 1300 A/20, position 'M' (36°32'N, 06°54'E) at 0200 A/21, position 'N' (35°58'N, 05°59'E) at 0830 A/21 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1200 A/21. 3) P 45 at average speed 7 knots passes position 'K' at 0500 A/20, position 'L' at 0700 A/21, position 'M' at 0001 A/22, position 'N' at 0830 A/22 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1230 A/22. (16)
22 Oct 1942 At 1350 A/22, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Holy Loch. (5)
This convoy was assembled off Oversay on 23 October 1942.

It was made up of the following transports; Alcinous (Dutch, 6189 GRT, built 1925), Alphard (Dutch, 5483 GRT, built 1937), Ardeola (British, 2609 GRT, built 1912), Benalbanach (British, 7153 GRT, built 1940), Charles H. Cramp (American, 6220 GRT, built 1920), Chattanooga City (American, 5687 GRT, built 1921), City of Worcester (British, 5469 GRT, built 1927), Clan MacTaggart (British, 7622 GRT, built 1920), Delilian (British, 6423 GRT, built 1923), Edward Ruthledge (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Empire Confidence (British, 5023 GRT, built 1925), Empire Mordred (British, 7024 GRT, built 1942), Fort McLoughlin (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942), Glenfinlas (British, 7479 GRT, built 1917), Havildar (British, 5401 GRT, built 1940), Hopecrown (British, 5180 GRT, built 1937), Jean Jadot (Belgian, 5859 GRT, built 1929), Lalande (British, 7453 GRT, built 1920), Lochmonar (British, 9412 GRT, built 1924), Lycaon (British, 7350 GRT, built 1913), Macharda (British, 7998 GRT, built 1938), Manchester Port (British, 7071 GRT, built 1935), Mark Twain (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Maron (British, 6487 GRT, built 1930), Mary Slessor (British, 5027 GRT, built 1930), Ocean Rider (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Viceroy (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Volga (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Wanderer (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Pacific Exporter (British, 6734 GRT, built 1928), Recorder (British, 5981 GRT, built 1930), Salacia (British, 5495 GRT, built 1937), Sobo (British, 5353 GRT, built 1937), St. Essylt (British, 5634 GRT, built 1941), Stanhill (British, 5969 GRT, built 1942), Tadorna (British, 1947 GRT, built 1928), Theseus (British, 6527 GRT, built 1908), Tiba (Dutch, 5239 GRT, built 1938), Urlana (British, 6852 GRT, built 1941), Walt Whitman (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), William M. Floyd (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), William Wirt (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942) and Zebulon B. Vance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942).
Also part of the convoy were the landing ships Derwentdale (8390 GRT, built 1941), Dewdale (8265 GRT, built 1941) and Ennerdale (8280 GRT, built 1941).

On assembly the convoy was escorted by the escort carrier HMS Avenger (Cdr. A.P. Colthurst, RN), AA ship HMS Alynbank (A/Capt.(Retd.) H.F. Nash, RN), destroyer HMS Vansittart (Lt.Cdr. T. Johnston, RN), sloops HMS Deptford (Lt.Cdr. H.R. White, RN), HMS Stork (Cdr. G.N. Brewer, RN), corvettes HMS Convolvulus (A/Lt.Cdr. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR), HMS Gardenia (T/Lt. M.M. Firth, RNVR), HMS Marigold (Lt. J.A.S. Halcrow, RD, RNR), HMS Pentstemon (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J. Byron, DSC, RNR), HMS Rhododendron (Lt.Cdr. L.A. Sayers, RNR), HMS Samphire (Lt.Cdr. F.T. Renny, DSC, RNR), HMS Vetch (T/A/Lt.Cdr. H.J. Beverley, DSO, DSC, RNR), HMS Violet (Lt. C.N. Stewart, RNR) and the minesweepers HMS Acute (Lt.Cdr. D. Lampen, DSO, RN), HMS Alarm (T/Lt.Cdr. R. Patterson, SANF(V)), HMS Albacore (Lt.Cdr. J.D.L. Williams, RN) and HMS Cadmus (Lt.Cdr. J.B.G. Temple, DSC, RN).

Around 1000 A/4, the convoy was split up into two sections KMS A1 and KMS O1. KMS A1 was destined for Algiers and KMS O1 was destined for Oran. KMS O1 then proceeded to the westwards so as to pass the Straits of Gibraltar later.

Convoy KMS A 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 2345 A/5; it was made up of the transports; City of Worcester, Glenfinlas, Jean Jadot, Lalande, Lochmonar, Macharda, Manchester Port, Maron, Ocean Rider, Ocean Viceroy, Ocean Volga, Ocean Wanderer, Sobo, Stanhill, Tiba and Urlana.
The landing ships Dewdale and Ennerdale were also part of the convoy.

The convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Stork, corvettes HMS Convolvulus, HMS Marigold, HMS Pentstemon, HMS Samphire [this corvette might have already parted company though, see below] and the minesweepers HMS Acute, HMS Alarm, HMS Albacore and HMS Cadmus.
Around 0700 A/5, the corvette HMS Samphire arrived at Gibraltar with defects from convoy KMS A1.

Around 0800 A/5, the minesweepers HMS Algerine (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Cooke, RN), HMS Hussar (Lt. R.C. Biggs, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Around 1830 A/5, the M/S trawlers HMS Cava (T/Lt. R.L. Petty-Major, RNVR), HMS Juliet (Lt. L.B. Moffatt, RNR), HMS Othello (T/Lt. S.C. Dickinson, RNVR), HMS Stroma (Skr. J.S. Harper, RNR), HMS Hoy (T/Lt. G.H. McNair, MBE, RNVR), HMS Inchcolm (Skr. A.C. Whitcombe, RNR), HMS Mull (Lt. J. Plomer, RCNVR), HMS Rysa (T/Lt. J.H. Cooper, RNVR) and the motor launches ML 238, ML 273, ML 283, ML 295, ML 307, ML 336, ML 338, ML 444 departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Around 2230 A/5, the monitor HMS Roberts (Capt. J.G.Y. Loveband, RN), escort destroyers HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN), HMS Bramham (Lt. E.F. Baines, DSO, RN), HMS Cowdray (Lt.Cdr. C.W. North, RN), HMS Zetland (Lt. J.V. Wilkinson, RN) and the corvette HMS Samphire (with her repairs completed) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Convoy KMS O 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 1630 A/6; it was made up of the transports; Alcinous, Alphard, Benalbanach, Charles H. Cramp, Chattanooga City, Clan Mactaggart, Delinlian, Edward Rutledge, Empire Confidence, Empire Mordred, Havildar, Lycaon, Mark Twain, Mary Slessor, Pacific Exporter, Recorder, Salacia, St. Essylt, Thesues, Walt Whitman, William Floyd, William Wirt and Zebulon B. Vance.
The landing ship Derwentdale was also part of this convoy.
The convoy was escorted by the AA ship HMS Alynbank, sloop HMS Deptford, corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Rhododendron, HMS Vetch and HMS Violet.

Around 1500 A/6, the minesweepers HMS Brixham (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR), HMS Bude (Lt. F.A.J. Andrew, RN), HMS Clacton (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) L.S. Shaw, RNR) and HMS Felixstowe (T/Lt. C.G. Powney, RNVR) departed Gibraltar to join the convoy KMS O1.

After dark on the 6th, the M/S trawlers HMS Coriolanus (T/Lt. N. Hunt, RNVR), HMS Eday (T/Lt. W.Y. Surtees, RNR), HMS Inchmarnock (T/Lt. C.G.V. Corneby, RNR), HMS Kerrera (Skr. R.W. Slater, RNR) and the motor launches ML 280, ML 458, ML 463, ML 469, ML 471, ML 480, ML 483 and HDML 1127, HDML 1128 and HDML 1139 departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS O1.

Two ships from Convoy KMS 1, the Ardeola and Tadorna formed part of Convoy KMS 1A after the convoy had split up. They were to proceed to Malta unescorted. The Admiralty had decided to make use of the expected confusion of the landings in North Africa to run two 'small' merchant ships with important cargo to Malta. These ships were considered expendable. They parted company with convoy KMS 1A on 8 November. They did not reach Malta however. When off Cape Bon on 9 November, they were taken under fire by Vichy French coastal batteries, despite the darkness, and then captured by motor torpedo boats. They were brought into Bizerta where their cargo was unloaded. The ships were later taken over by the Italians.
This convoy was assembled off Oversay on 26 October 1942.

It was made up of the following (troop) transports; Awatea (British, 13482 GRT, built 1936), Batory (Polish, 14287 GRT, built 1936), Cathay (British, 15225 GRT, built 1925), Dempo (Dutch, 17024 GRT, built 1931), Derbyshire (British, 11660 GRT, built 1935), Duchess of Bedford (British, 20123 GRT, built 1928), Durban Castle (British, 17388 GRT, built 1938), Ettrick (British, 11279 GRT, built 1938), Exceller (American, 6597 GRT, built 1941), Leinster (British, 4302 GRT, built 1937) Letitia (British, 13595 GRT, built 1925), Llangibby Castle (British, 11951 GRT, built 1929), Marnix van St. Aldegonde (Dutch, 19355 GRT, built 1930), Monarch of Bermuda (British, 22424 GRT, built 1931), Mooltan (British, 20952 GRT, built 1923), Nieuw Zeeland (Dutch, 11069 GRT, built 1928), Orbita (British, 15495 GRT, built 1915), Otranto (British, 20026 GRT, built 1925), Reina del Pacifico (British, 17702 GRT, built 1931), Sobieski (Polish, 11030 GRT, built 1939), Strathnaver (British, 22283 GRT, built 1931), Tegelberg (Dutch, 14150 GRT, built 1937), Viceroy of India (British, 19627 GRT, built 1929), Warwick Castle (British, 20107 GRT, built 1930) and Winchester Castle (British, 20012 GRT, built 1930).

The headquarters ships HMS Bulolo (Capt.(Retd.) R.L. Hamer, RN), HMS Largs (Cdr. E.A. Divers, OBE, RNR), the landing ships HMS Glengyle (Capt.(Retd.) D.S. McGrath, RN), HMS Karanja (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) D.S. Hore-Lacy, RN), HMS Keren (A/Cdr. S.E. Crewe-Read, RN), HMS Princess Beatrix (Cdr.(Retd.) T.B. Brunton, DSC, RN), HMS Queen Emma (Capt.(Retd.) G.L.D. Gibbs, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Royal Scotsman (Lt.Cdr. J.D. Armstrong, DSC, RD, RNR), HMS Royal Ulsterman (A/Lt.Cdr. W.R.K. Clark, DSC, RD RNR) and HMS Ulster Monarch (Lt.Cdr. N.A.F. Kingscote, RNR) and the attack transports USS Almaack (T/Capt. C.L. Nichols, USN), USS Leedstown (Cdr. D. Cook, USNR), USS Samuel Chase (Capt. R.C. Heimer, USCG) and USS Thomas Stone (Capt. O.R. Bennehoff, USN) were also part of the convoy.

On assembly off Oversay on the 27th the convoy was escorted by the light cruiser HMS Sheffield (Capt. A.W. Clarke, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral C.H.J. Harcourt, CBE, RN), escort carrier HMS Biter (Capt. E.M.C. Abel Smith, RN), destroyer HMS Clare (Lt.Cdr. L.H. Landman, RN), sloops HMS Aberdeen (Lt.Cdr. H. Day, RN), HMS Enchantress (Lt.Cdr. A.E.T. Christie, OBE, RN), HMS Ibis (Lt.Cdr. H.M. Darell-Brown, RN), cutters HMS Hartland (Lt.Cdr. G.P. Billot, RNR), HMS Walney (Lt.Cdr. P.C. Meyrick, RN), frigates HMS Exe (A/Cdr. M.A.O. Biddulph, DSC, RN), HMS Rother (Lt.Cdr. R.V.E. Case, DSC and Bar, RD, RNR), HMS Spey (Cdr. H.G. Boys-Smith, DSO and Bar, RD, RNR), HMS Swale (Lt.Cdr. J. Jackson, RNR) and HMS Tay (Lt.Cdr. R.E. Sherwood, RNR).
Around 1120 A/2, the destroyers HrMs Isaac Sweers (Capt. W. Harmsen, RNN) and HMS Escapade (Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN) joined coming from the Azores.

Around 0200 A/3, the AA ships HMS Palomares (A/Capt.(Retd.) J.H. Jauncey, RN), HMS Pozarica (Capt.(Retd.) L.B. Hill, DSO, OBE, RN) and the destroyers HMS Achates (Lt.Cdr. A.H.T. Johns, RN), HMS Antelope (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Sinclair, RN), HMS Amazon (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) Lord Teynham, RN), HMS Velox (Lt. G.B. Barstow, RN), HMS Verity, (Lt.Cdr. R. Horncastle, RN), HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN) and HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN) departed Gibraltar to join the convoy. At 1045 A/3, the destroyer HMS Wivern (Cdr. M.D.C. Meyrick, RN) also departed to join the convoy. She had been unable to depart earlier due to defects.
Around 0800 A/3, the destroyer HMS Marne (Lt.Cdr. H.N.A. Richardson, DSO, DSC, RN) joined the convoy coming from the Azores.
Around 1300 A/3, the light cruiser HMS Jamaica (Capt. J.L. Storey, RN) also departed Gibraltar to join the convoy.
Around 1830 Z/3, HMS Sheffield parted company with the convoy to proceed to Gibraltar where she arrived at 0815 A/3, she was to fuel and then join ' Force O '.

Around noon on 4 November 1942, the convoy was split up into two sections KMF A1 and KMF O1. KMF A1 was destined for Algiers and KMF O1 was destined for Oran. KMF O1 then proceeded to the westwards so as to pass the Straits of Gibraltar later.

Convoy KMF A 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 0100 A/6; it was made up of the (troop) transports; Almaack, Awatea, Cathay, Dempo, Ettrick, Exceller, Leedstown, Marnix van St. Aldegonde, Otranto, Sobieski, Strathnaver, Viceroy of India and Winchester Castle.

The headquarters ship HMS Bulolo and the landing ships HMS Karanja, HMS Keren, HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and Ulster Monarch and the attack transports USS Samuel Chase and USS Thomas Stone were also part of the convoy.
[exactly which ships of the escort went on with this part of the convoy will have to be researched further.]

In the morning of 5 November, HrMs Isaac Sweers parted company with the convoy to join ' Force H '. HMS Escapade and HMS Marne were apparently detached to Gibraltar on the convoy passing the Strait of Gibraltar.
Also on 5 November, the corvettes HMS Spiraea (Lt.Cdr. R.S. Miller, DSC, RNR) and HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF A1.

Around 0200 A/6, the destroyers HMS Broke (Lt.Cdr. A.F.C. Layard, RN), HMS Malcolm (A/Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN), HMS Vanoc ( A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN) and HMS Wrestler (Lt. R.W.B. Lacon, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF A1 and relieve HMS Achates, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon and HMS Wivern. After having been relieved these destroyers arrived at Gibraltar around 0545 A/6. Also arriving at Gibraltar were the Leinster, HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and Ulster Monarch.

Around 1000 A/6, HMS Broke, HMS Malcolm, HMS Vanoc and HMS Wrestler joined ' Force O ' while the screen on ' Force O ' joined the convoy, the destroyers / escort destroyers involved were ORP Blyskawica (Lt.Cdr. L. Lichodziejewski, ORP), HMS Lamerton (Lt.Cdr. C.R. Purse, DSC, RN), HMS Wheatland (Lt.Cdr. R. de L. Brooke, DSC, RN) and HMS Wilton (Lt. A.P. Northey, DSC, RN). The AA ship HMS Tynwald (Capt.(Retd.) P.G. Wodehouse, DSO, RN) also joined the convoy from ' Force O ' at the same time.

Around 0535 A/7, in position 37°34'N, 00°01'W, the attack transport USS Thomas Stone was torpedoed and damaged by an enemy aircraft. HMS Spey remained with the damaged ship. At 2040 A/7, the destroyers HMS Wishart and HMS Velox joined and the ship was taken in tow by HMS Wishart. HMS Spey by that time had departed with the ships 24 landing craft in which the ships troops had embarked. She was to escort them to Algiers but all had to be scuttled and the troops were taken on board HMS Spey. At 0535 A/8 the tug St. Day joined which also passed a tow. The damaged ship anchored off Algiers around 1030 A/11 being towed there by HMS Wishart and HMS St. Day.
Around 0725 Z/7, HMS Clare parted company to join ' Force O ' which she did around 0913 Z/7.

Around 1815 A/7, the section destined for ' C Sector ' (Charlie Sector) parted company with the convoy. It was made up of the USS Almaack, USS Leedstown, USS Samuel Chase, Exceller and Dempo. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Tynwald, escort destroyers HMS Cowdray, HMS Zetland, sloop HMS Enchantress, minesweepers HMS Algerine, HMS Hussar, HMS Speedwell, corvettes HMS Pentstemon, HMS Samphire, MS trawlers HMS Cava, HMS Othello and the motor launches HMS ML 273 and HMS ML 295. At 2135 A/7, the beacon submarine HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) made contact with the force and the ships were guided to their positions for the landings. From convoy KMS A1 the transports Macharda and Maron were destined for Charlie sector. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Stork and the corvettes HMS Pentstemon and HMS Samphire.
Around 1900 A/7, The remainder of convoy KMF A1 split into two sections, one for ' A Sector ' (Apple Sector) and one for ' B Sector ' (Beer Sector).

The force for ' A Sector ' was made up of HMS Karanja and the Marnix van St. Aldegonde and Viceroy of India. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Pozarica, escort destroyers HMS Bicester, HMS Bramham, frigate HMS Rother, minesweeper HMS Cadmus, MS trawlers HMS Juliet, HMS Rysa, HMS Stroma and the motor launches HMS ML 283, HMS ML 336 and HMS ML 338. At 2214 A/7, the made contact with their beacon submarine HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN). A few minutes later they stopped and the landings commenced. From convoy KMS A1 the following ships were assigned to ' A Sector '; Dewdale, Lalande, Manchester Port, Ocean Viceroy and Ocean Wanderer. They were escorted by the corvettes HMS Convolvulus and HMS Marigold.

The force for ' B Sector ' was made up of HMS Bulolo, HMS Keren and the Awatea, Cathay, Otranto, Sobieski, Strathnaver and Winchester Castle. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Palomeres, destroyer ORP Blyskawica, escort destroyers HMS Lamerton, HMS Wheatland, HMS Wilton, minesweepers HMS Acute, HMS Alarm, HMS Albacore, MS trawlers HMS Hoy, HMS Incholm, HMS Mull and the motor launches HMS ML 238, HMS ML 307 and HMS ML 444. They made contact with their beacon submarine HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) around 2220 A/7 hours and landing operation commenced shortly afterwards. From convoy KMS A1 the following ships were assigned to ' A Sector '; City of Worcester, Ennerdale, Glenfinlas, Jean Jadot, Lochmonar, Ocean Rider, Ocean Volga, Sobo, Stanhill, Tiba and Urlana. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Stork and the corvettes HMS Pentstemon and HMS Samphire which then went on with the ships for the ' Charlie sector '.
On 9 November the ships involved in the landings anchored in Algiers Bay.

Convoy KMF O 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 2230 A/6; it was made up of the (troop) transports; Batory, Duchess of Bedford, Durban Castle, Letitia, Llangibby Castle, Monarch of Bermuda, Mooltan, Nieuw Zeeland, Orbita, Reina del Pacifico, Tegelberg and Warwick Castle.
The headquarters ship HMS Largs and the landing ships HMS Glengyle, HMS Princess Beatrix and HMS Queen Emma were also part of the convoy.
Around 1950 A/4, the light cruiser HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF O1.
For the landings at Oran three main beaches were selected. ' X ', ' Y ' and ' Z ' beach. There was also one subsidiary beach, ' R '.
The fast convoy, KMF O1, would, after passing through the Straits of Gibraltar make rendezvous with the slow convoy, KMS O1 in position 36°26'N, 01°15'W.

The convoys would then be diverted into nine groups, these were; For ' X ' beach Group I, 1st Division; Batory, HMS Princess Beatrix, Queen Emma, 2nd Division; Benalbenach, Mark Twain, Mary Slessor and Walt Whitman. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Aurora, destroyer HMS Wivern, corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Vetch and the motor launch HMS HDML 1139. Group VIII, LST HMS Bachaquero (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) A.W. McMullan, RNR) escorted by the M/S trawler HMS Horatio (T/Lt. C.A. Lemkey, RNR).

For ' Y ' beach Group II; HMS Glengyle, Monarch of Bermuda, Llangibby Castle, Clan Mactaggart and Salacia. They were escorted by the destroyers Brilliant, HMS Verity, M/S trawlers HMS Coriolanus, HMS Eday, HMS Inchmarnock, HMS Kerrera and the motor launches HMS ML 458, HMS ML 463, HMS ML 469, HMS ML 471 and HMS HDML 1128.

For ' Z ' beach Group III, 1st Division; Duchess of Bedford, Durban Castle, Ettrick, Warwick Castle. 2nd Division; Derwentdale, Reina del Pacifico and Tegelberg. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Jamaica, escort destroyers HMS Calpe (Lt.Cdr. H. Kirkwood, DSC, RN), HMS Farndale (Cdr. D.P. Trentham, RN), minesweepers HMS Brixham, HMS Bude, HMS Clacton, HMS Felixtowe, HMS Polruan (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.S. Landers, RNR), HMS Rothesay (Cdr. A.A. Martin, DSC, RD, RNR), HMS Rhyl (Cdr. L.J.S. Ede, DSO, RN), HMS Stornoway (T/A/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Fraser, RNR) and the motor launches HMS ML 280, HMS HDML 1127.

Group V; Alcinous, Alphard, Charles H. Cramp, Chatanooga City, Delilian, Recorder and Zebulon B. Vance. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Deptford, cutters HMS Hartland, HMS Walney, corvettes HMS Rhododendron, HMS Violet and the motor launches HMS ML 480 and HMS ML 483.
Group VI, 1st division; Derbyshire, Letitia, Mooltan and Nieuw Zeeland. 2nd division, Empire Confidence, Lycaon and Theseus.

Group VII, 1st division, Empire Mordred, Havildar, Pacific Exporter and St. Essylt. 2nd division; Edward Rutledge, William Floyd and William Wirt. Groups VI and VII were escorted by the light (AA) cruiser HMS Delhi (Capt. A.T.G.C. Peachey, RN), destroyer HMS Vansittart, sloop HMS Aberdeen and the frigates HMS Exe and HMS Swale.

Group IX; LST's HMS Misoa (T/Lt. K.G. Graham, RNR) and HMS Tasajera (Lt.Cdr. W.E. Gelling, DSC, RD, RNR). They were escorted by the M/S trawlers HMS Fluellen (T/Lt. B.J. Hampson, RNR), HMS Ronaldsay (T/Lt. A. Stirling, RNR) and HMS Shiant (T/Lt. A.C. Elton, RNR).
For ' R ' beach Group IV; HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and HMS Ulster Monarch. They had the same escort as Group III.
Two submarines were stationed off the beaches as beacons, these were HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN).

31 Oct 1942 At 0700 Z/31, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (during operation Torch, landing beach marking off Algiers). She carried only six torpedoes to make room for two folbots. She was to proceed to Malta at the end of this patrol to join the 10th Submarine Flotilla based there.
1 Nov 1942 At 2240 Z/1, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1506 A/1, in which it was stated that Operation Torch would go ahead and that 'D'-day was 8 November. (5)
1 Nov 1942 At 0800 Z/1, at the eastern end of the Alboran Sea, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a westbound tanker to the north which passed at a range of 3 miles.
At 2240 Z/1, HMS P 45 received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1506 A/1, in which it was stated that Operation Torch would go ahead and that 'D'-day was 8 November. (5)
2 Nov 1942 At 2025 Z/2, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0940 A/2, in which it was she was ordered to carry out preliminary reconnaissance by Folbot.
At 2120 Z/2, a westbound merchant ship burning navigation lights was seen 3 miles from the shore off Cape Tenes. (5)
3 Nov 1942 At 0430 Z/3, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived in her assigned area off Algiers.
At 1808 Z/3, HMS P 45 surfaced and set course and speed to close Bordelaise Rock for a night reconnaissance.
At 1935 Z/3, the reconnaissance was completed. (5)
4 Nov 1942 At 0510 Z/4, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived.
At 0700 Z/4, course was set to 220° to close Bordelaise Rock for a periscope reconnaissance.

At 0830 Z/4, a Vichy-French merchant vessel, thought to be the Criton (4564 GRT, built 1927) was seen leaving Algiers eastbound on course 080°. [The Criton had been sunk in 1941 so this could not have been correct.]

At 1223 Z/4, the periscope reconnaissance was completed. A dummy attack was then carried out on what was thought to be the Vichy-French merchant vessel Bougainville which was leaving Algiers on a course of 080°. [There were two French ships named Bougainville but one had been taken over by the Japanese and the other, a naval auxiliary had been sunk at Diego Suarez in May 1942 so this must have been another vessel.]
At 1308 Z/4, course was set to 020° to retire to the northward.
At 1803 Z/4, HMS P 45 surfaced. Course was set to 200° to close Bordelaise Rock for a preliminary Folbot reconnaissance of 'C' beaches.
At 2101 Z/4, a Folbot with Lt.Cdr. Willmott and Lt. Cooper was launched. P 45 withdrew to seaward to charge the batteries.
At 2325 Z/4, Bordelaise Rock was closed once more to re-embark the Folbot.
At 2340 Z/4, having done so, P 45 withdrew to seaward. (5)
5 Nov 1942 At 1900 Z/5, just to the east of Algiers Bay, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted an eastbound merchant vessel burning navigation lights proceeding on course 080°. (5)

6 Nov 1942 At 0630 Z/6, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) carried out a dummy attack on a westbound Vichy-French merchant vessel thought to be the Cap Falcon (1727 GRT, built 1924). The ship was proceeding towards Algiers on course 250°.

At 0751 Z/6, HMS P 45 sighted two small trawlers bearing 140° at a range of 4 miles. The trawlers were seen to stop and were seen fishing in the vicinity of Aguelli Rocks so these were not naval trawlers.
At 1853 Z/6, HMS P 45 evaded a merchant vessel buring navigation lights which was eastbound on course 070° coming from Algiers. (5)

7 Nov 1942 During the night of 7/8 November 1942, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), assisted in the landing at Algiers Bay. She launched the folbot and her party and also guided the convoy in, acting as beacon.
0115 Z/7 - Sighted a merchant vessel burning navigation lights proceeding westwards on course 270°. Course was altered to open out to avoid detection.
0400 Z/7 - Dived and set course to 200° to close the coast.
0600 Z/7 - Altered course to 020°.
0900 Z/7 - Altered course to 200° to close Bordelaise Rock to obtain a final bearing before sunset.
1644 Z/7 - Altered course to 020°.
1734 Z/7 - Sighted an eastbound merchant vessel coming from Algiers proceeding on course 070°.
1805 Z/7 - Surfaced.
1830 Z/7 - Altered course to 220° to close Bordelaise Rock.
1900 Z/7 - Altered course to keep clear of a merchant vessel burning navigation lights proceeding on course 080°.
1907 Z/7 - Altered course to 180°.
2015 Z/7 - Launched a Folbot near Bordelaise Rock and set course to proceed to the release position of the convoy.
2130 Z/7 - Commenced transmitting radar transmissions and signalling the sector letter with a white light 60° either side of bearing 320°.

2145 Z/7 - Sighted two destroyers bearing 045°. Turned towards and brought No.3 and No.4 torpedo tube to the ready. Later sighted four merchant vessels. Transmitted the sector letting in their direction. The convoy commenced to close the submarine.
2200 Z/7 - Transferred Lt.Cdr. Willmott and Lt.'s Cooper and Leadsman to a landing craft from the USS Samuel Chase (Capt. R.C. Heimer, USCG). P 45 then proceeded to the 'inner mark position'.
2305 Z/7 - Arrived at the 'inner mark position' and commenced transmitting the sector letter to seaward.
0001 Z/8 - Sighted the Folbot's light transmitting the sector letter. P 45 adjusted her position.
0049 Z/8 - Sighted the first flight of landing craft passing the stern.
0220 Z/8 - Destroyers, which were to seaward, started firing tracer. Searchlights were shining to seaward. Shore batteries opened fire.
0250 Z/8 - Ceased transmitting the sector letter. Switched on dimmed side lights and proceeded clear of the convoy, which had by then closed the mark position.
0304 Z/8 - Altered course to 070° for the eastern end of the submerged bombing restriction area at 11.5 knots.
0509 Z/8 - Dived. Intermittent distant underwater explosions were heard throughout the day.
0858 Z/8 - Sighted two cruiser, thought to be a County-class and Dido-class, two aircraft carriers and three destroyers bearing 310° at a range of 7 miles.
0915 Z/8 - Altered course to 090°. (5)

8 Nov 1942 At 2104 Z/8, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 082° to proceed as ordered in the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1342 A/7 to proceed to the patrol area that had been ordered by the Capt. S.10. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but the Capt. S.10 had ordered P 45 to patrol off the Gulf of Tunis and Bizerta upon completion of her duties at Algiers.]

At 2210 Z/8, a submarine was sighted bearing 300° proceeding on a course of 080°. P 45 turned to a firing course but the other submarine was recognised as a British S-class just before firing. A challenge was then made and the submarine was identified as HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN). (5)
9 Nov 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) completed her special operation off Algiers. (5)

10 Nov 1942 At 0020 Z/10, off the Gulf of Philippeville, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), off the Gulf of Philippeville sighted an unknown submarine on course 240°. The Officer of the Watch altered to a firing course and when Lt. Turner arrived on the bridge the submarine was identified as a large Italian one. The enemy then sighted P 45 and dived. P 45 also dived owing to the possibility of the enemy firing a stern torpedo. Asdic contact could not be gained, no HE was picked up. HMS P 45 surfaced at 0103 Z/10.
The submarine encountered was the Italian Bronzo (T.V. Cesare Buldrini). The Italians were under the impression that the other submarine was also Italian.

At 0430 Z/10, P 45 altered course to 360° to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0821 A/9 which ordered her to proceed through positions 38°16'N, 07°56'E and 38°30'N, 10°00'E. Further orders would follow.
At 1135 Z/10, course was altered to 015° to comply with the orders.
At 2119 Z/10, course was altered to 081°. (5)

10 Nov 1942 In a signal from the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2233 A/10, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) and HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) received the following orders; Following orders apply to submarines operating east of 08°00'E. 1) No submarine is to be attacked unless its hostile identity is established beyond reasonable doubt. 2) Submarines are not mutually to establish identity by flashing recognition signals. If in pursuit of enemy and another submarine is sighted which is not definitely identified as enemy it is to be watched and course adjusted to draw clear. If not in pursuit of enemy the submarine making the sighting is to dive and use super sonic telegraphy (S.S./T.) to endeavour to establish identity. 3) Italy bound merchant ships are not to be attacked. Salvoes are limited to four torpedoes except during an attack on battleships. 4) Submarines on patrol lines should communicate and exchange positions by S.S./T. once daily. (17)
11 Nov 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) was ordered to patrol off Cape San Vito, Sicily. (5)

11 Nov 1942 At 1858 A/11, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) surfaced and set course to 075° to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 A/10 which ordered her to patrol off Cape San Vito in position 'G'.

A patrol line, laid out off Cape San Vito in positions 'F', 'G', 'H', 'J' and 'K' is to be relieved. The patrol line was currently occupied by the following submarines, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) was in position 'F' (315° - Cape San Vito - 3 miles), the other positions were on the same bearing 10 miles between them. Position 'G' was occupied by HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), 'H' by HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN), 'J' by HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN). Position 'K' was vacant.
HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS P 45, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) approaching from the west were to take up position 'K', 'G' and 'H' respectively. Further orders would follow.
At 1904 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1729 A/11 was received which ordered her to patrol off the Gulf of Tunis. (5)
12 Nov 1942 At 1603 A/12, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1509 A/12 in which she was ordered to move 10 miles, 320° from her current position with all despatch.
At 1619 A/12, having decoded the signal course was altered to 285° to proceed to the position ordered.
At 1830 A/12, HMS P 45 surfaced and patrolled up and down moon in the vicinity of position 37°06'N, 10°22'E.

At 2040 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1002 A/12 was received which ordered P 45 to patrol in position 090° - Cani Rock - 8 miles. This signal had been missed in the earlier W/T routines. Course was set to proceed to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°28.5'N, 10°18'E which was the position ordered in the signal timed 1002 A/12 amended by the instructions in the signal timed 1509 A/12.
At 2300 A/12, HMS P 45 commenced to patrol on courses 090° - 270° in the vicinity of position 37°28.5'N, 10°18'E. (5)
13 Nov 1942 At 0300 A/13, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) set course to proceed to position 110° - Cani Rock - 24 miles as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1916 A/12.
At 0445 A/13, HMS P 45 commenced to patrol on courses 090° - 270° in the vicinity of the new patrol position.
At 0549 A/13, HMS P 45 dived and commenced to patrol on courses 180° - 360° in the vicinity of the patrol position.
At 1820 A/13, HMS P 45 surfaced and patrolled up and down moon in the vicinity of the patrol position. (5)

14 Nov 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with four torpedoes north-east of Bizerta, Tunisia in position 37°25'N, 10°11'E. No hits were obtained. This was the Italian transport Sivigliano (1270 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boat Sirtori on a transport mission from Palermo to Bizerta.

Later on the same day another escorted merchant vessel was attacked with one torpedo north-east of Bizerta in position 37°26'N, 10°12'E. The torpedo missed. [This vessel has not yet (July 2016) been identified.]
0300 A/14 - Set course to patrol in position 013° - Cani Rock - 9 miles as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2325 A/13.
0500 A/14 - Arrived in the new patrol position. Commenced to patrol on courses 090° - 270° through the patrol position.
0725 A/14 - Sighted smoke bearing 110°. Altered course to get on track. Sighed a destroyer and the later masts of merchant ship with air escort. Enemy course 240°. Commenced attack.

0754 A/14 - In position 37°25'N, 10°11'E fired four torpedoes from 5500 yards at the merchant vessel thought to be of about 3500 tons. On firing broke surface. The aircraft was seen to come towards. Quickly dived again and took evasive action.
0800 A/14 to 0930 A/14 - Took avoiding action at 120 feet to get clear of the HE. Meanwhile reloaded torpedo tubes No.3 and No.4.
1045 A/14 - Surfaced to transmit an enemy report, timed 0745 A/14.
1053 A/14 - Dived and altered course to 195°.
1402 A/14 - Sighted an aircraft ahead. Picked up HE on bearing 135°. Proceeded at full submerged speed to close.
1424 A/14 - Sighted two Maestrale-class destroyers bearing 145° at a distance of 5 miles in approximately 37°25'N, 10°11'E. The submarine closed to about 3.5 miles before giving up the attack.
This were two destroyers out of a group of four, Maestrale, Grecale, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani, which were carrying troops from Palermo to Bizerta.
1430 A/14 - Set course to patrol closer to Cani Rock as traffic appeared to pass about two miles north of the lighthouse.
1731 A/14 - Surfaced to make an enemy report, timed 1415 A/14, on the destroyers.
1736 A/14 - Dived after passing the signal.
1823 A/14 - Surfaced and set course 075° for new patrol position 37°30'N, 10°30'E as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1341 A/14.
1828 A/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel escorted by three destroyers on the port quarter. Enemy course 060°. Started attack.
1831 A/14 - In position 37°26'N, 10°12'E fired one torpedo at the merchant vessel from 1000 yards. The torpedo missed.
1832 A/14 - Dived and altered course to 060°.
1855 A/14 - Surfaced after HE had been lost. Set course for the new patrol position.
1930 A/14 - Passed an enemy report timed 1830 A/14.
2100 A/14 - Arrived in the new patrol position. Patrolled on courses 090° - 270° through this position. (5)

15 Nov 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with one torpedo east-north-east of Bizerta, Tunisia in position 37°30'N, 10°25'E. The torpedo missed. This was P 45's last torpedo as she had carried only six torpedoes on this patrol so as to make room for the Folbots that had been used on the special operation off Algiers during the initial landings there.

The target was probably a ship from the convoy made up of the Italian transports Città di Tunisi (5419 GRT, built 1930) and Caterina Costa (8060 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Uragano. This convoy had sailed from Bizerta for Palermo and Naples.
1935 A/15 - Sighted one merchant vessel, one unidentified ship and two destroyers bearing 320°. Enemy course 050°. Altered course to attack. Visibility was patchy.
1939 A/15 - In position 37°30'N, 10°25'E fired one torpedo at the merchant vessel from about 1200 yards.
1940 A/15 - Dived and altered course to the south.
1950 A/15 - Surfaced to make an enemy report timed 2002 A/15. Also reported that all torpedoes had been expended and that P 45 would retire to seaward.
2035 A/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1815 A/15 in which submarines were ordered to maintain W/T silence until all torpedoes had been expended.
2330 A/15 - Altered course withdraw from patrol in the area to the west of the Skerki Bank. (5)

16 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0032 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); Your 2002 A/15. Pass through following position at times quoted, since presumed you have withdrawn west of Skerki Bank. Position 38°00'N, 10°00'E at 0600/16, position 'A' at 0500 A/17, Position 'B' at 0600/17 thence dived at 120 feet to position 'D'. You will be 10 miles astern of HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) and should sound range occasionally. HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) will pass you on opposite course at 80 feet about midway. After arrival at position 'D' at 1900/17tthence through positions 'E' and 'F' at 0700/18 continuing on the surface to the end of the swept channel where a minesweeper will meet you and P 48 at 1325 A/18. Whilst approaching Malta in daylight on 18th set watch on local patrol wave 465 k/cs and dive for blue air raid warning. Your attention is drawn to Q.B.B. 298 (mined area). (18)
18 Nov 1942 At 1630 A/18, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN), were swept in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1330 A/18. (5)
26 Nov 1942 At 1300 A/26, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Tunis / Bizerta.
She, and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN), were escorted out until 1520 A/26-1530 A/26 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).
Both submarines were to proceed via the usual route through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) and then through positions 38°14'N, 11°21'E and 38°05'N, 10°15'E to their patrol area's.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (19)

27 Nov 1942 At 2215 A/27, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1647 A/27 in which she was ordered to be part of a patrol line which was established in direction 120° from position 040° - Cani Rock - 4 miles. HMS P 45 was ordered to take up position 'Z' in the line at dawn on the 28th.
At 2228 A/27, after decoding the signal, course was altered to 245° to comply with the orders.

At 2338 A/27, aircraft flares were sighted bearing 325°, probably over an eastbound enemy convoy of three merchant vessels and a destroyer previously reported by aircraft. [Three motor vessels and one destroyer, west-north-west of Marettimo, in position 38°16'N, 11°15'E, course 100°, speed 15 knots at 2305 A/27] P 45 did not attempt to close as it seemed impossible to intercept. Also the battery had not been fully charges and other submarines were thought to be closer. Besides that if P 45 would chase the convoy she would not be able to be in position at dawn the following morning. (20)

28 Nov 1942 At 0334 A/28, well to the west of Marettimo Island, in position 37°51'N, 11°19'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted two large and one medium sized merchant vessels and three destroyers bearing 025° proceeding on course 225° at an estimated speed of 10 knots. Course was altered to close.
[Citta di Tunisi and Brindisi convoy ???]
At 0355 A/28, one of the merchant vessels opened fire with tracer AA. No aircraft were seen or heard. Also no flares were dropped.
At 0403 A/28, an explosion was heard, thought to be a bomb. No visible damage was seen to any of the ships in the convoy.

At 0413 A/28, when about 2 miles distant from P 45 the ships were seen to turn away to the north-westward and two destroyers made smoke screens. The ships also increased speed and P 45 was unable to keep in contact so course was set to proceed to the ordered patrol position. It is not thought the submarine had been seen and no counter attack was made by the escorts.
Between 0630 A/28 and 0730 A/28, P 45 remained on the surface after daylight as the battery was still low and P 45 was still 25 miles from the ordered patrol position.
At 0730 A/28, P 45 dived after a transport aircraft was seen overhead. The aircraft may have sighted the submarine.
At 1200 A/28, P 45 arrived in her patrol area.
At 1827 A/28, P 45 surfaced. (5)
29 Nov 1942 At 1305 A/29, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a mast and funnel bearing 250°. Course of this vessel was 190°. The submarine closed submerged at full speed.

At 1325 A/29, the vessel was identified as an armed trawler. Course was now altered to 030° to get back in position. The trawler was seen to alter course to port, apparently to pass between Zambretta Island and the mainland keeping close inshore.
At 1830 A/29, HE was picked up followed by the sound of tanks being blown.

At 1910 A/29, HMS P 45 surfaced. A ship was sighted bearing 020° proceeding on course 020°. It was thought this was HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN). Contact was not established. [Most likely this was indeed HMS P 217 which was in the area and surfaced at 1839 A/29.] (5)
1 Dec 1942 At 1250 A/1, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) picked up distant HE on bearing 175°.
At 1256 A/1, the masts and funnel top of a merchant vessel were sighted on bearing 180°. Enemy course was 260°. HMS P 45 closed at full submerged speed on a 120° track to attack.

The vessel had entered the Gulf of Tunis from the southward of Zembra Island. P 45 was unable to close within 5 miles. The vessel was seen to alter course to the southwards and proceed to Tunis. The attack was broken off at 1412 A/1.
[Possibly Valdirosa ???]

At 2132 A/1, HE was picked up doing 160 revs. This is considered to have been a patrol vessel fitted with a reciprocating engine. P 45 first attempted to close but the HE stopped and started at intervals which gave the impression of the target being an A/S patrol vessel which was never on a steady course for long. Lt. Turner decided to draw clear to the southward. (5)

2 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked an Italian torpedo boat with one torpedo north of the Gulf of Tunis. The torpedo missed. This was most probably the damaged Italian torpedo boat Procione on her way to Tunis after the Aventino-convoy had been decimated by Force Q.

0143 A/2 - Heard HE from a reciprocating engine doing 160 revs. It was considered to be the patrol vessel also encountered the previous day (evening) again. Lt. Turner again drew clear to the southward.
0312 A/2 - Sighted ship astern. It was first thought to be a torpedo boat, a destroyer and then a submarine. Finally, visually confirmed as torpedo boat.
0325 A/2 - In position 37°12'N, 10°48'E fired one torpedo. Dived and altered course to get clear.
0459 A/2 - Surfaced after having reloaded the torpedo tube. Commenced charging as the battery was still rather low.
0608 A/2 - Dived.
0700 A/2 - Sighted an Orsa-class torpedo boat.

1200 A/2 - Sighted two merchant vessels thought to be on a course of 330° at a range of 8 miles. Only the masts and funnel tops were seen. Commenced to close at full submerged speed but the enemy turned south-westwards into Tunis Bay and the attack was broken off.
1835 A/2 - Surfaced. (5)

3 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked but missed an Italian schooner with one torpedo. This may have been the Italian sailing vessel Eleonora Rosa (54 GRT, built 1901) which arrived at Tunis in the morning between 1000 and 1100 hours.
Later, HMS P 45 damaged the Italian sailing vessel Cesira Curreri (81 GRT) with gunfire in the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°09'N, 10°29'E.

And even later HMS P 45 attacked the Italian torpedo boat Antonio Mosto in the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°16'N, 10°22'E. The Italian warship had sailed to reinforce the Arlesiana-convoy. One torpedo hit the port side and failed to explode but caused a leak in no.4 fuel tank and unhinged the port propeller guard.

Even later on this day, HMS P 45 damaged the Italian sailing vessel Cesira Curreri (81 GRT) with gunfire in the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°09'N, 10°29'E. The sailing vessel arrived at Tunis (La Galouette) in the afternoon. Four of the crew had been killed.
0256 A/3 - Sighted three destroyers, one motor torpedo boat and one large schooner to the northward. Turned to close.
0312 A/3 - In position 37°11'N, 10°33'E fired one torpedo at the schooner from 1800 yards. It missed.
0320 A/3 - Dived. The ships seem the have altered course and to be moving slowly.
0325 A/3 - In position 37°09'N, 10°29'E fired another torpedo at the schooner which had turned back to her original course. Range on firing was 1500 yards. The torpedo missed yet again.
0730 A/3 - Altered course to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0451 A/3 which ordered HMS P 45 to patrol in position 37°13'N, 10°25'E.

0830 A/3 - Surfaced in position 37°09'N, 10°29'E and engaged a small two-masted schooner with gunfire from a range of 1000 yards. A total of 16 rounds were fired for 1 or 2 hits. The crew was seen to jump overboard. Dived. Unable to see the schooner through the periscope. Range was only 1000 yards on diving.
1034 A/3 - Sighted another schooner at a range of 7 miles proceeding on course 290°.
1200 A/3 - Sighted an Odero-class torpedo boat.
1221 A/3 - In position 37°14'N, 10°27'E fired one torpedo. The enemy sighted the tracks and no hits were obtained.
1231 A/3 - Heard seven depth charges exploding. A second destroyer or torpedo boat arrived and the enemy carried out a hunt.
1430 A/3 - The enemy made a fairly accurate attack in which six depth charges were dropped quite close causing minor damage.
1545 A/3 - Now clear of the enemy, returned to periscope depth.

1835 A/3 - Surfaced. Altered course to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0438 A/3 in which submarines were ordered to withdraw towards the Tyrrhenian Sea or to the south-east of Pantelleria. Lt. Turner decided to do the last and course was set towards position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).
2227 A/3 - Heard HE which was thought to be a patrol vessel on the port quarter. Course was altered to draw clear.
2308 A/3 - Sighted a patrol vessel, possibly a torpedo boat in position 37°23'N, 11°09'E. Dived and opened out. The vessel could not be positively identified through the periscope.
2346 A/3 - Surfaced and continued passage towards position 'K'. (5)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2359 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); P 217 and P 219 proceed to the Naples area with despatch by proceeding on surface by day at the Commanding Officers discretion to patrol within 8 miles of the following positions; P 217, 40°47'N, 13°48'E and P 219, 40°35'N, 14°08'E. HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) is expected to join you shortly.

P 37, P 43 and P 48 proceed with despatch but submerged by day to form a patrol line (operation B.S.). 3 - 350° -Cape San Vito, with the first submarine 4 miles from Cape San Vito and the other submarine spaced 5 miles apart.
