P 45 return to establish patrol approximately 40° from Plane Island lighthouse in position selected by you for interception of Bizerta traffic. (21)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0438 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); On completion of attacks [on an expected convoy] you are to withdraw either the the north-east to the Tyrrhenian Sea or via the route of HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) as in my 0005 A/1 to the south-east of Pantelleria.

[HMS Trooper's route had been through the following positions according the signal timed 0005 A/1 from the Capt. S.10; 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).]

In a signal timed 0451 A/3, these submarines were sent the following orders; Assume the following dispositions forthwith; P 219, 37°28'N, 11°00'E. P 37, 37°24'N, 10°51'E. P 45, 37°13'N, 10°25'E. P 48 and P 43, remain in your present ordered positions. Position, course and speed of the enemy convoy at 0205 A/3, 37°50'N, 11°38'E, 235°, 8 knots. Convoy of four merchant ships and five destroyers.

In a signal timed 0845 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37, HMS P 43 and HMS P 48; Move with utmost despatch from ordered position as follows; P 37, 10 miles 305°. P 48, 5 miles 305°. P 43, 5 miles 340°. (21)
4 Dec 1942 At 0428 A/4, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) reversed course to patrol on a line 40° from Plane Island lighthouse as ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2359 A/3.
At 0600 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0501 A/4 was received which ordered HMS P 45 to patrol in position 37°16'N, 10°40'E (north-west of Zembra Island).
At 0607 A/4, HMS P 45 dived in position 37°30'N, 11°15'E.

At 1258 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 A/4 was received which ordered HMS P 45 to move 3 miles 270° from the patrol position previously ordered. It was not possible to establish patrol in the position ordered before dark today.
At 2221 A/4, HMS P 45 arrived in the patrol position as ordered by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1145 A/4. (5)
5 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked an escorted small tanker with two torpedoes north of the Gulf of Tunis. No hits were obtained.

This was the small German tanker Sudest (former French, 641 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the Italian destroyer Saetta and the Italian torpedo boat Procione. This convoy was en-route from Tunis to Trapani. A fourth vessel which had been part of the convoy, the small tanker Noroit (former French, 453 GRT, built 1935), had just been mined and sunk.
0230 A/5 - Altered course to close a white light sighted bearing 070°.
Identified the source of the white light as an Italian hospital ship.
0355 A/5 - Dived and carried out a dummy moonlight attack on this hospital ship.
[Perhaps the German Graz en-route from Naples to Bizerta. She was mined and sunk off Cani Rocks about two hours later ???]
0430 A/5 - Surfaced and returned to the patrol position.
0710 A/5 - Altered course to close smoke sighted on bearing 320°. Closed at full submerged speed.
0734 A/5 - The bearing of the smoke drew first to port and then to starboard. Suspected the source of the smoke were patrolling destroyers / torpedo boats.
0810 A/5 - Abandoned the chase.
0832 A/5 - Altered course to 180° to return to the patrol position.
1120 A/5 - Sighted smoke bearing 225°. Went to full submerged speed to close.
1145 A/5 - The range could not be closed so abandoned the chase.
1205 A/5 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 A/5 which ordered all submarines on patrol to withdraw 50 miles from the nearest coast.
1250 A/5 - Sighted a Maestrale-class destroyer bearing 135°. Enemy course was 070°.
1255 A/5 - Sighted a barrage balloon bearing 340°. Turned to close at full speed.
1330 A/5 - Sighted the mast of a ship underneath the barrage balloon. Later lost sight the mast. The range could not be closed further. Enemy course was about 270°. Abandoned the case.
1340 A/5 - Sighted two Maestrale-class destroyers escorting a small tanker in ballast on a northerly course. Closed to attack.
1433 A/5 - In position 37°23'N, 10°41'E fired two torpedoes at the tanker from 1200 yards. No hits were obtained.
1453 A/5 - Altered course to the northward to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 A/5.
1600 A/5 to 1800 A/5 - Heard distant explosions.

1836 A/5 - Surfaced and continued to retire to the northward, west of the Skerki Bank and then altered course to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1744 A/5 which orders P 45 to return to Malta by the route to the south-west of Pantelleria. (5)

5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1058 A/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Withdraw 50 miles from the coast, orders will follow. (21)

7 Dec 1942 At 0200 A/7, in position 36°19'N, 12°36'E, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a vessel bearing 110° proceeding on an opposite course. Lt. Turner dived. The vessel was thought to be HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) but the HE indicated it was probably a motor torpedo boat (E-boat). The vessel was sighted through the periscope but it could not be positively identified.
[It is quite likely the vessel sighted was indeed HMS Rorqual which reported nothing though.]
At 1545 A/7, HMS P 45 ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by ML 168 (?) which had joined at 0900 A/7. (22)
22 Dec 1942 At 1300 B/22, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah and Kuriat.
She was escorted out until around 1510 B/22 by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (22)
24 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) arrived in the Kerkennah area.

At 0232 A/24, while closing Kerkennah No.4 buoy a ship was seen bearing 320° inside the buoy line. Dived as P 45 was silhouetted against the lighter horizon and the ship appeared to be closing. No HE was picked up. The ship could not be seen through the periscope.
At 0334 A/24, HMS P 45 surfaced. The ship was now thought to be a wreck.
At 0630 A/24, HMS P 45 dived 2.5 miles, 190° from Kerkennah No.4 buoy and patrolled north and south through this position.
At 1200 A/24, course was altered to close land at Cape Africa (near Mahdia) by nightfall. (5)

25 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked a convoy made up of the Italian sailing vessel Pasquale Costa (Italian, 198 GRT, built 1910) and the German landing barges (F-boats) F 479 and F 546 off Mahdia, Tunisia. Following the attack F 479 dropped four depth charges.

0515 A/25 - Closed land. Sighted a large schooner and two small craft in line ahead steering 180 degrees in position 35°28'N, 11°08'E. The small craft were thought to be lighters. Engaged the larger of these two lighters with the 3" gun from 1000 yards. The vessel opened fire with tracer and probably Oerlikon. These lighters were probably landing craft. The schooner turned away. After a burst of Vickers gun the enemy ceased fire temporarily but soon re-opened an accurate fire. P 45 turned away and dived. Two light depth charges were dropped close astern. P 45 then drew clear and returned to periscope depth but the enemy could no longer be seen. One of the gun crew of P 45 had been hit by the enemy and was seriously wounded. Course was then set to return to Malta to put him ashore for treatment but he died at 1130 A/25. The submarine itself had sustained some minor damage by the enemy gunfire.
1215 A/25 - Course was set to patrol to the north of Cape Africa.
1845 A/25 - The body of Able Seaman J. Sim (P/SSX 32496) was committed to the deep.
2130 A/25 - East of Mahdia, in position 35°30'N, 11°16'E, two unidentified ships were seen. This were thought to be destroyers. P 45 turned to close and identify the enemy.

2155 A/25 - Dived and continued to close but now submerged. The ships could not be definitely identified owing to the varying visibility and occasional rain squalls. Their appearance was like small warships. Both ships were proceeding on course 350°. It is possible the ships encountered were HMS Paladin (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN) which were on an anti-shipping sweep off the east coast of Tunisia this night. (5)

26 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary submarine chaser O 97 / Margherita (69 GRT) with gunfire about 5 nautical miles north of Mehedia, Tunisia in position 35°32'N, 11°05'E. There were no casualties.
0502 A/26 - Dived off Ras Kapudia. Proceeded northwards along the coast.
1410 A/26 - Surfaced and engaged a small southbound schooner with the 3" gun from a range of 2500 yards. The enemy abandoned ship. Closed. Obtained 3 hits.
1415 A/26 - Dived for aircraft, flying low over the eastern horizon on course 350°. Meanwhile closed the schooner.
1420 A/26 - Surfaced and resumed the gun action now from a range of 1200 yards closing to 800 yards.
1425 A/26 - Dived after 12 hits. The schooner was seen to sink in 15 fathoms in approximate position 35°32'N, 11°05'E.
1834 A/26 - Surfaced and patrolled east of Kuriat Island. (5)

29 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked an unescorted southbound merchant vessel with four torpedoes east-south-east of Kuriat. No hits were obtained. Possibly this was Sivigliano (1270 GRT, built 1921) on passage from Tunis to Sousse without escort.
0515 A/29 - Sighted ship bearing 340°.
0521 A/29 - Dived and turned to attack.
0531 A/29 - In position 35°44'N, 11°10'E fired four torpedoes at an unescorted merchant vessel of about 3500 tons on a course of 270°. All torpedoes missed.

0800 A/29 - In position 35°41'N, 11°15'E, sighted the mast and funnel top of a single-funnel torpedo boat with a large merchant vessel astern bearing 290°. Enemy course was 185°, estimated speed 12 knots. Altered course to close at full submerged speed but the range could not be closed under 6000 yards. Two aircraft were escorting this enemy convoy. Eventually broke off the attack due to the long range.

0842 A/29 - Lost sight of the convoy to the southward. The escorting torpedo boat was dropping depth charges. Later the mast and funnel top of the torpedo boat were seen again after it turned back to the north in the vicinity of Cape Africa. Later it turned south again presumably to rejoin the merchant ship. Possibly it had a doubtful A/S contact or report from the air escort.
[Zenobia Martini escorted by Orione ???].
1020 A/29 - Heard more distant explosions. Doubtful HE was heard but not confirmed. Nothing further was sighted.
1045 A/29 - Heard faint A/S transmissions on bearing 270° but the faded and eventually ceased. Again nothing was sighted.
1650 A/29 - Picked up HE. Sighted the torpedo boat returning alone on course 320° from No.1 Kerkennah buoy to position 270° - Cape Africa - 5.5 miles.
1720 A/29 - Lost the torpedo boat from sight.
1830 A/29 - Surfaced and patrolled north and south in the area as the attack from this morning had certainly not been detected so there was no need to vacate the area for 24 hours. (5)

31 Dec 1942 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian sailing vessel Maddalena (345 GRT, built 1920) south-east of Mehedia, Tunisia in position 35°18'N, 11°23'E. The Italian vessel was on passage from Sousse to Tripoli.
0453 A/31 - Sighted a southbound vessel on the starboard beam. Altered course to close at full speed.
0509 A/31 - Dived when the range was 1500 yards and closed submerged to 1200 yards.
0517 A/31 - Fired one torpedo by moonlight at the three-masted auxiliary Maddalena. The torpedo hit and the ship blew up with of column of black smoke and debris.
0521 A/31 - Surfaced and closed the area of the sinking to search for survivors.
0613 A/31 - Picked up three survivors.
0625 A/31 - Dived and set course for the north end of our patrol area.
1851 A/31 - Surfaced and patrolled in the vicinity of Ras Dimass and Kuriat Island. (5)

2 Jan 1943 At 0500 A/2, when patrolling near Ras Dimass, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to 085° to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1650 A/31 in which she was ordered to leave patrol so as to return to Malta on 3 January. (5)
3 Jan 1943 At 1105 A/3, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by a minesweeper (unidentified) which had joined at 0825 A/3. (5)
14 Jan 1943 At 1015 A/14, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean).
P 45 was to proceed through position 36°03'N, 14°00'E thence direct to the patrol area west of line 340° for Kerkennah No.4 Buoy and north of latitude 35°50'N.

Between 1255 A/14 and 1520 A/14, A/S exercises were carried out with HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR), HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS Beryl (T/S.Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR) on which also practice attacks were made.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (23)

15 Jan 1943 At 1847 A/15, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) surfaced and set course 305° to comply with the S.10's signal timed 1221 A/15 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 36°29'N, 11°01'E from midnight. (5)

16 Jan 1943 At 0215 A/16, having been delayed by the weather conditions, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) commenced to patrol north and south through the ordered patrol position (36°29'N, 11°01'E) until 0430 A/16 when course was set co close the land.
At 0621 A/16, P 45 dived and patrolled to the south-west of El Maâmoura in the Gulf of Hammamet.
At 1842 A/16, P 45 surfaced and set course 138° for Kuriat Island. Nothing had been seen all day. (5)

17 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) damaged the Italian tug R 97 / Genova (96 GRT) with gunfire near Hergla, Tunisia. Two German armoured vehicles (under Sub.Lt. Heine) of Combat Group Hergla opened fire on the submarine with their 75mm cannon, claiming two hits on conning tower and bow, and forced her to dive.
0610 A/17 - Dived and set course to inspect Sousse and the coast.
0715 A/17 - Heard HE. Tried to close but was unable to do so. The mast of a small northbound coastal craft was visible for a short time. This vessel was bound for Sousse.
0740 A/17 - Heard HE, closed at full submerged speed.

0800 A/17 - Identified the source of the HE as a tug with one funnel, one mast and a small gun forward. This vessel was bound for Sousse at 8 knots. No gun attack was made as P 45 was only 3 to 4 miles from the Sousse breakwater and within range of shore batteries.

1345 A/17 - Sighted a northbound tug between Sousse and Hammamet. Closed to 2000 yards. The tug was lightly armed with only one machine gun forward. It was the same tug that had been seen in the morning.

1455 A/17 - Surfaced in position 36°03'N, 10°33'E and opened fire from 2000 yards. The tug turned away and was beached. Scored two hits, possibly three out of eighteen rounds. Shore batteries had by now opened fire so the action had to be broken off and P 45 dived and withdrew to seaward.
1845 A/17 - Surfaced and set course to patrol to the south of Kuriat Island in the vicinity of Cape Africa. (5)

18 Jan 1943 At 1500 A/18, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), which was patrolling between Ras Kapudia and Cape Africa, received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1226 A/18 in which she was ordered to keep north of 36°00'N until dawn/19. Course was set to run out from land before surfacing.
At 1840 A/18, HMS P 45 surfaced and set course to pass to the north of Kuriat Island.
At 2116 A/18, course was set for El Maâmoura.

HMS P 45 was ordered northwards as an anti-shipping sweep would be carried out in the area between Kuriat and Kerkennah from about 2300 A/18 to 0100 A/19 by HMS Pakenham (Capt. E.B.K. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and Vasilissa Olga (Lt.Cdr. G. Blessas). (23)

19 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked and damaged what is thought to be a small auxiliary off Korba, Tunisia. The gun action had to be broken off when a round jammed in the gun. This may have been the Italian tug Z 11 / Maurizio (62 GRT, built 1897) from Marsala which disappeared at that time in this area while on passage from Marsala to Sousse.
0350 A/19 - Sighted smoke of a small vessel bearing 320°.
0400 A/19 - Dived and closed to identify. Unable to close submerged. Surfaced and gave chase.

0455 A/19 - Engaged the enemy with the gun in position 36°32'N, 10°56'E from a range of 1500 yards and closing. The 6th round however jammed in the breach and we were unable to clear the gun so the action had to be broken off. One hit had been obtained. Dived and retired to seaward.
0534 A/19 - Surfaced and cleared the gun of the projectile. Unable to see the enemy.

0717 A/19 - Sighted three heavily laden small coastal steamers proceeding southwards. Closed at full speed but was unable to close within 3 miles. Smoke was also sighted to the southward and it was thought that a larger, northbound, target might be on its way. The smoke was however lost from sight and not seen again.

0830 A/19 - Went to 70 feet to make good a defect to the forward periscope High Power / Low Power fitting. It proved not possible to remedy the defect and the forward periscope could only be used in the High Power setting.

1845 A/19 - Surfaced and set course for Kuriat Island to intercept a 5000-tons merchant vessel reported by aircraft. It was also thought that the three small merchant vessels sighted in the morning may have put into Sousse and would sail again to make for for the Kerkennah Channel by dawn. (5)

19 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1059 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); From dusk/20 keep south of latitude 35°48'N. HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) will be operating to the north of this latitude. (24)

20 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) intercepts a small Italian naval auxiliary towing a schooner. She sank the naval auxiliary with gunfire, most likely this was the Italian (former French) auxiliary minesweeper Grondin seized in Bizerta. The schooner apparently beached herself on the Tunisian coast south of Mahdia. This was most probably Maggio (also a former French vessel) which was reported beached near Ras Hercle but was later refloated.
1120 A/20 - Sighted smoke bearing 215°. Turned to close but the smoke disappeared. No HE was picked up and nothing developed.
1459 A/20 - Sighted smoke bearing 215°. Turned to close.

1538 A/20 - In position 35°27'N, 11°06'E fired two torpedoes at a small northbound auxiliary wearing the Italian Ensign. Range was 1350 yards. She was towing another small vessel. A torpedo boat, not thought to be escorting, was also in the area. Went deep after firing. The torpedo wakes could clearly be seen in the water though. One explosion was heard after the correct interval.

1545 A/20 - Returned to periscope depth. The torpedo boat was seen to the northward, almost out of sight. There was no sign of the towing vessel. The vessel that had been under tow was seen making smoke and had altered course towards the shore. P 45 followed but after a while the vessel was not seen anymore. It is thought she foundered while attempting to beach this ship.
1917 A/20 - Surfaced and patrolled to the northwards towards Kuriat Island. (5)

20 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1103 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Establish patrol forthwith west of line 340° from Kerkennah as follows; 1) P 42 south of latitude 35°30'N. 2) P 45 north of P 42 and south of latitude 36°00'N. 3) P 46 north of P 45. (24)

21 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sank the Italian sailing vessel Margherita (140 GRT, built 1893) towed by the auxiliary minesweeper R 220 / Ardito (120 GRT, built 1937) with gunfire and a demolition charge about 5 nautical miles east-north-east of Kuriat Island, Tunisia.

0035 A/21 - Dived to close a ship sighted to the southeast of Kuriat Island. The vessel was northbound. Closed at full submerged speed. The ship was seen to alter course towards Sousse and P 45 was unable to close further submerged. The ships had high engine revolutions.
0057 A/21 - Surfaced and closed at full speed on the surface. Still unable to close. The vessel is thought to have been a torpedo boat.
0112 A/21 - Dived.
0137 A/21 - Surfaced and patrolled east and westwards to the north of Kuriat Island.
0621 A/21 - Dived and set course to close Monastir.

0737 A/21 - Heard HE. Visibility had closed down to about 3 cables. Two distant sources of HE were picked up. One was high speed and the other 180 revolutions on the same bearing. The HE drew rapidly ahead across the bow. Enemy course was approximately 280° bound for Sousse. Lt. Turner did not surface as he suspected one of the vessels to be a torpedo boat or motor torpedo boat.
0830 A/21 - Sighted two 200-ton topsail schooners. One was towing the other. Enemy course was 030°. Closed at full speed.

0916 A/21 - Surfaced in position 35°49'N, 10°56'E for gun action. Both vessels were soon abandoned by the crews. The towing vessel was sunk by gunfire. The towed vessel was also hit several times but was then boarded. She was then scuttled with a demolition charge.
0953 A/21 - Dived and withdrew towards Kuriat Island.
1405 A/21 - Altered course to 360° to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1116 A/21. [For the text of this signal see one of the other events for 21 Janaury 1943.]
1530 A/21 - Sighted an enemy torpedo boat proceeding northwards near Kuriat Island. It was later seen to proceed towards Sousse.
1843 A/21 - Surfaced and patrolled on east and west courses during the night. (5)

21 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 2257 A/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); From dawn/22, P 45 operate between 35°30'N and 36°00'N. P 46 operate north of 36°00'N. (24)

21 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1116 A/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); P 42 leave patrol after dark tonight and return to Malta through positions 35°01'N, 12°20'E and 35°02'N, 14°10'E to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0830 A/23. P 45 move forthwith north of latitude 36°00'N, keeping south of 36°15'N. P 46 keep north of 36°15'N. Three motor torpedo boats from Malta will be operating off Tunisian the coast between 35°00'N and 36°00'N. They are routed north of Linosa and have orders not to attack submarines.
The motor torpedo boats referred to in this signal were HMMTB 264 and HMMTB 307 (only two sailed instead of three). (24)

22 Jan 1943 At 0400 A/22, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) set course for Kuriat Island to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2257 A/21. [For the text of this signal see one of the events for 21 January 1943.]
At 0636 A/22, HMS P 45 dived and patrolled to the south of Kuriat Island towards Cape Africa.
At 1855 A/22, HMS P 45 surfaced. Nothing had been sighted all day. Patrolled north and south in the vicinity of Ras Dimass hoping to intercept a northbound schooner reported by aircraft.

At 2023 A/23, reveived the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1015 A/22 which contained the following orders; Keep south of 35°30'N during the hours of darkness during the night of 22/23. Motor torpedo boats from Malta will be operating off Kuriat and have orders not to attack submarines. You may extend your patrol to 36°00'N tomorrow.

The motor torpedo boats which were to carry out this anti-shipping sweep were HMMTB 307 and HMMTB 313. However they returned to Malta a few hours after having departed and the operation was apparently cancelled. (23)
23 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked and damaged the Italian auxiliary minesweeper B 487 / Michelino (37 GRT, built 1927) with gunfire off Sousse, Tunisia.
Also, near Mahdia, two torpedoes were fired against a vessel thought to be a minelayer but both missed.
0047 A/23 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0013 A/23 which gave P 45 freedom of action south of 36°00'N. Set course for Kuriat Island.
0639 A/23 - Dived. Patrolled off Monastir and Sousse.
1059 A/23 - Heard a distant explosion.

1415 A/23 - Sighted three tank landing craft on the port beam steering a course of 095°. These landing craft were all armed and P 45 had no High Explosive ammunition for the deck gun remaining. Torpedoes could not be used against these shallow draft vessels.
This must have been the Italian MZ 702, MZ 725 and MZ 760 which had left Sousse around 1300 A/23 to proceed southwards.
1430 A/23 - Heard two distant explosions.
1531 A/23 - Sighted a small merchant vessel with an auxiliary sail set. Enemy course was 260° and she was bound for Sousse.

1550 A/23 - Surfaced in position 35°52'N, 10°43'E for gun action. The enemy was hit several times but the ships crew did not abandon ship. Shore batteries from Sousse opened up and the action was broken off, also P 45 was out of ammo for the deck gun.
1559 A/23 - Dived and withdrew to seaward.
1700 A/23 - Went to 70 feet to renew the periscope wire for the forward periscope.
1933 A/23 - Surfaced and set course for Kuriat Island.
2201 A/23 - Sighted smoke bearing 180°. Closed on the surface.
2215 A/23 - Dived.
2239 A/23 – In position 35°45’N, 11°07’E, fired two torpedoes from 800 yards at northbound enemy vessel thought to be a minelayer. The vessel had 2 masts and one funnel. Missed.
2345 A/23 - Surfaced after having completed the reload of the two torpedo tubes fired. Patrolled on course north and south in the vicinity of Cape Africa. (5)
24 Jan 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sank the small Tunisian sailing vessel Adjbi with demolition charges off Salakta, Tunisia. The Adjbi had been damaged earlier by Beaufighters.

1200 A/24 - Sighted a small northbound sailing vessel. Prepared for gun action with the Vickers machine gun and starshell for the 3" gun (the only 3" ammunition left). It was hoped that the enemy would abandon ship after fire was opened so we could board.
1230 A/24 - Four Beaufighter aircraft were seen. These attacked the sailing vessel and the crew abandoned ship. Unrivalled continued to close.

1345 A/24 - Surfaced in position 35°23'N, 11°05'E. The Tunisian ship had a full cargo off earthenware gourds. Two demolition charges were placed and the vessel was sunk to prevent her from being used by the enemy. Following the destruction of the vessel P 45 retired to seaward.
1835 A/24 - Surfaced and patrolled north and south off Cape Africa - Ras Kaboudia keeping south of Cape Africa in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1120 A/24.
These orders must have been issued as a group of four MTB's from Malta, HMMTB 315, HMMTB 260, HMMTB 264, HMMTB 307 were to operate off Monastir during the night of 24/25 January. (23)

26 Jan 1943 At 1911 A/26, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) surfaced and set course to leave patrol and return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's orders in his signal timed 1232 A/25 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol after dark/26 passing through positions 35°42'N, 12°02'E and 36°02'N, 12°21'E thence make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0830 A/28. (23)

27 Jan 1943 At 0247 A/27, north-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°52'N, 12°12'E, a ship was sighted bearing 310°. Visibility was varying considerably owing to rain showers. The ship was thought to be a destroyer / torpedo boat or a patrol vessel. HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived. Indistinct HE was heard intermittently. It was though to be probably turbine HE. The vessel could not be seen through the periscope. Course of the vessel was thought to be approximately 330°. (5)
28 Jan 1943 At 1100 A/28, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by a minesweeper (not identified) which had joined at 0845 A/28. (5)

8 Feb 1943 At 1300 A/8, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina. On board was a commando of one officer and two other ranks for train wrecking.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (5)
9 Feb 1943 At 0634 A/9, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived in position 35°48'N, 15°40'E. Continued to proceed northwards on course 005°.
Between 1430 A/9 and 1600 A/9, four distant explosions were heard. P 45 was proceeding at 70 feet except when up of W/T routines.
At 1832 A/9, HMS P 45 surfaced and continued on course 005° to make landfall off Cape Spartivento. (5)

10 Feb 1943 At 0600 A/10, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived. She continued to close the Italian mainland at 70 feet at 5 knots. Lt. Turner had decided to proceed northwards from Cape Spartivento to reconnoitre the coast.
At 0900 A/10, four distant explosions were heard followed after an interval by three more.

At 1304 A/10, HMS P 45 sighted four small armed trawlers. The enemy vessel were proceeding southwards close inshore. P 45 closed Cape Bruzzano and and carried out a preliminary periscope reconnaissance of the coastal railway line and tunnels.
At 1510 A/10, distant explosions were heard.

At 1645 A/10, smoke was sighted bearing 200°. Course was altered to investigate. The source of the smoke turned out to be a destroyer / torpedo boat which passed at a range of 1000 yards proceeding on a course of 020° at 19 knots.
At 1845 A/10, HMS P 45 surfaced and patrolled on courses north and south while keeping 5 miles from the coast. (5)

12 Feb 1943 At 0017 A/12, after an uneventful day, HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) closed the coast for a night reconnaissance of the shore near Cape Bruzzano. Weather conditions were deteriorating though.
By 0043 A/12, the visibility was deteriorating rapidly and the reconnaissance was abandoned when still 2 miles from the shore. The submarine withdrew to seaward.
At 0340 A/12, P 45 dived to 70 feet after RUGBY W/T routine.
Between 0410 A/12 and 0813 A/12 and also between 1430 A/12 and 1600 A/12, distant underwater explosions were heard.
At 1840 A/12, P 45 surfaced. The weather conditions were improving. The submarine patrolled on courses 020° - 200° along the coast with the intention to launch the Folbot party after midnight. (5)
13 Feb 1943 HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) landed a party of three to blow up a railway tunnel of Cape Bruzzano. The attempt failed due to the heavy swell close inshore.
0148 A/13 - Placed the Folbot on the casing.
0149 A/13 - Trimmed down and closed the coast near Cape Bruzzano.
0237 A/13 - Launched the Folbot in position 38°02'N, 16°09'E with three men to sabotage a railway tunnel off Cape Bruzzano.

0345 A/13 to 0415 A/13 - The Folbot was recovered. Unfortunately, the raiding party had been unable to land in the heavy swell close inshore. It took considerable time to recover the Folbot as it was in a sinking condition.
0436 A/13 - A southbound train was seen to enter the tunnel adding further to the disappointment.
0545 A/13 - Dived and set course up the coast to proceed towards Lorci.
1500 A/13 - Sighted a small two-masted schooner on the beach, apparently for maintenance, near Marina di Siderno.
1900 A/13 - Surfaced and set course to pass 5 miles south of Cape Spartivento and then make for Catania, Sicily. (5)

15 Feb 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) fired a total of three torpedoes at a tug an lighter off Acireale, Sicily. No hits were obtained. Unrivalled then surfaced for gun action but before a round could be fired she had to dive again when shore batteries opened fire on her.

The target was the Italian auxiliary minesweeper R 204 / Tea (115 GRT, built 1914) towing a barge. Three torpedoes were observed to explode on the shore. Axis documents show that the submarine opened fire but this was a mistake as, from a distance, the firing from Flak Regiment 131 (located about a mile away) which forced Unrivalled to withdraw, was mistaken for coming from the submarine.
0532 A/15 - Dived and closed Catania from the southward to inspect the harbour through the periscope.

0700 A/15 to 1000 A/15 - Sighted 40 to 50 small fishing boats in the vicinity of the submarine which prevented P 45 from closing within 2 miles from the harbour. Some of these fishing vessels passed very close.
1223 A/15 - Sighted three small two-masted schooners proceeding southwards at a range of 3000 yards.

1332 A/15 - Sighted three small schooners, two under sail and one under auxiliary motor and one small cargo vessel. All were southbound. Commenced a dummy attack on the small cargo vessel which was thought not to be worth a torpedo. Now a tug was sighted bound for Messina. She was towing a large heavily laden lighter. The dummy attack on the small cargo vessel was broken off and an attack was started on the tug and lighter.
1418 A/15 - In position 37°37'N, 15°12'E fired two torpedoes at the tug and lighter from 1000 yards. The torpedoes ran under and exploded on the rocks behind. The tug slipped the tow.
1428 A/15 - Fired one torpedo at the now almost stationary lighter. Range was 750 yards. This torpedo also passed under. The tug meanwhile took the lighter in tow again.
1432 A/15 - Surfaced for gun action. The tug turned inshore. Shore batteries now opened fire and Unrivalled had to dive again without having fired a single shell.
1532 A/15 to 1536 A/15 - Heard distant explosions.
1745 A/15 - After going deep for a patrolling aircraft, Unrivalled was unable to see her quarry on regaining periscope depth. It is possible that the tug and lighter had entered Riposto harbour.
1755 A/15 to 1758 A/15 - Heard underwater explosions, possibly off the entrance to Riposto Harbour.
1817 A/15 - Commenced to withdraw to seaward.
1830 A/15 - Heard four explosions.

1957 A/15 - Surfaced and set course for the Calabrian coast. The patrol area had been extended to the northward by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1126 A/15. It now also included the Bay of Squillace. (5)

16 Feb 1943 While operating south of Punta Stilo, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian sailing vessel (naval auxiliary) U 4 / Sparviero (498 GRT, built 1915) in position 38°18'N, 16°26'E.
Unrivalled also torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant vessel Pasubio (2216 GRT, built 1903) in position 38°18'N, 16°29'E.
0630 A/16 - Dived and close the shore in the vicinity of Lorci.
0700 A/16 - Patrolled along the coast to the northward. The schooner seen beached on the 13th was still there.
0837 A/16 - Sighted a large 1000-tons, 4-masted motor vessel, at anchor close inshore of Rocella Ionica. Closed.
0932 A/16 - Fired one torpedo from a range of 750 yards. It hit amidships. The ship broke in two and sank in shallow water. Withdrew to seaward.
1010 A/16 - Sighted a small southbound two-masted coastal schooner close inshore. The schooner was not attacked with the gun owing to the proximity of Rocella Ionica and patrolling aircraft.
1038 A/16 - Heard a distant explosion.

1111 A/16 - Sighted smoke bearing 020°. This turned out to be two medium sized merchant vessels escorted by two destroyers and two CANT flying boats. The vessels were southbound, zigzagging, with the destroyers screening ahead and on either bow.
1215 A/16 - Fired four torpedoes at the leading merchant vessel thought to be 6000 tons. The vessel was deep laden.
1217 A/16 - One torpedo was heard to hit. Unrivalled meanwhile had gone deep.
1223 A/16 - Five depth charges were dropped.
1228 A/16 - Six depth charges were dropped.
1230 A/16 - 1300 A/16 - Unrivalled managed to drew clear from the hunt.
1330 A/16 - Returned to periscope depth. Several aircraft were seen to be patrolling near the position of the attack. No ships in sight.
1414 A/16 - Went to 70 feet and, now out of torpedoes, set course for Malta via the reverse of the outward route.
1850 A/16 - Surfaced. (5)

17 Feb 1943 At 0450 A/17, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) passed her signal timed 0431 A/17, to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; Torpedoes expended. Returning by reverse of outward route arriving at rendezvous at 0830 A/19.
At 1600 A/17, HMS Unrivalled sighted an Italian hospital ship, thought to be the Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923), on course 355° making for the Straits of Messina.
This must indeed have been the Toscana, which was on passage from Sfax to Naples via the Straits of Messina so as to avoid the dangerous Sicilian Channel.

At 2010 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1045 A/17 was received which ordered HMS Unrivalled to proceed through positions p.45 through 35°22'N, 15°20'E and 35°22'N, 14°18'E on her way to Malta. (25)
19 Feb 1943 Around 1130 A/19, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

2 Mar 1943 At 1300 A/2, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (beach reconnaissance of beaches '13' and '14') for the upcoming operation Husky.
On board was a COPP party of three officers and one other rank.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (26)
6 Mar 1943 At 0347 A/6, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) altered course to close the land in the vicinity of Porri Island.
Between 0900 A/6 and 1330 A/6, a close periscope reconnaissance was carried out of No.14 beach from just outside the 10 fathom line.
At 1330 A/6, HMS Unrivalled set course to complete close periscope reconnaissance of No.13 beach but rain and bad visibility made this impossible.
At 1630 A/6, HMS Unrivalled withdrew from the coast prior to charging the batteries. The weather was, like the previous days, still unsuitable for Folbot operations.
At 2020 A/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled on courses 090° - 270°. (27)
7 Mar 1943 In the evening, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), launched a Folbot with a crew of two for a beach reconnaissance. The Folbot failed to return.
0500 A/7 - Dived and set course to close the land.
A.M. - Carried out a careful periscope reconnaissance of '13' beach. Took four photographs but the value of these is doubtful owing to the swell

P.M. - Passed close to the south of Porri Island and then carried out a close periscope reconnaissance of '14' beach. The swell was too high for further photographs. Sea and swell were noticed to decrease towards sunset. The wind veered to westward.
1900 A/7 - Unrivalled bottomed in position 36°39.8'N, 14°52.7'E in 17 fathoms of water.
2037 A/7 - Surfaced.
2123 A/7 - Launched a Folbot for the first operation with Lt. De Kock, SANF(V) and S.Lt. Crossley, SANF(V) in position 36°41'N, 14°53.2'E.
2250 A/7 - Unrivalled bottomed in position 36°39.8'N, 14°52.7'E in 17 fathoms of water.
0048 A/8 - Surfaced and closed the launching position.
0120 A/8 - Sighted a red light inshore flashing but the Folbot was not sighted.
0351 A/8 - Abandoned the search for the Folbot. Withdrew one mile to seaward.
0407 A/8 - Bottomed in position 36°39.8'N, 14°52.7'E in 17 fathoms of water.
0808 A/8 - Heard distant explosions. (26)

8 Mar 1943 In the evening, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), launched a Folbot with a crew of two to search for the crew of the Folbot that had been launched the previous evening. This Folbot and her crew also failed to return. The two men of this Folbot were captured by Italian coast guards.

1630 A/8 - Came to periscope depth and moved to position 200° - Porri Island - 2 miles to recover the Folbot at the alternative rendezvous position and the launch a second Folbot for a beach reconnaissance.
1740 A/8 - Bottomed in position 200° - Porri Island - 2 miles
1850 A/8 - Came to periscope depth.
1908 A/8 - Surfaced.
1953 A/8 - Launched a Folbot with Lt. Davies, RNVR and Able Seaman McGuire for a reconnaissance operation. Unrivalled remained on the surface on bearing 200° from Porri Island between 1 and 2 miles.
0025 A/9 - Dived when HE of a vessel was picked up to seaward. The Enemy course was proceeding eastwards and is thought to have a small destroyer / torpedo boat.
0040 A/9 - Surfaced.
0143 A/9 - Abandoned the search for the Folbots and withdrew to seaward.
0245 A/9 - Surfaced and charged the batteries.
0544 A/9 - Dived and set course to close the land near Porri Island. Wind and swell were increasing rapidly from the east-south-east.
1200 A/9 - Bottomed in position 36°39'N, 14°55'E.
1830 A/9 - Heard several distant underwater explosions.

1913 A/9 - Surfaced and remained in the vicinity of position 200° - Porri Island - 2 miles. The weather was unsuitable for Folbot operations but the submarine remained on the surface in the vicinity in case the Folbot Crews swam off.
2245 A/9 - Dived and withdrew to seaward to charge the batteries about 15 miles from the coast. (26)

10 Mar 1943 At 0530 A/10, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the land with the intention to conduct a periscope reconnaissance of '14' beach but the weather conditions made this impossible.

At 1400 A/10, the submarine bottomed in position 36°39'N, 14°54.9'E. It was intended to close the alternative rendezvous position at 1630 A/10 abd remain there for the first part of the night if the weather was suitable.
At 1630 A/10, HMS Unrivalled commenced to shift position.
At 1750 A/10, HMS Unrivalled bottomed in the alternative rendezvous position.

At 1900 A/10, the submarine surfaced and closed Porri Island and remained in that vicinity until 2300 A/10 when the search was abandoned. HMS Unrivalled dived and retired to seaward to charge the batteries. (27)
11 Mar 1943 At 0010 A/11, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) surfaced,
At 0054 A/11, course was set to 090° to proceed to the eastern edge of the area to make a report.
Between 0515 A/11 and 0545 A/11, it was attempted to pass a signal, timed 0515 A/11 to the Capt. S.10 in which the situation was reported.
At 0546 A/11, the submarine dived. Distant explosions were heard.
At 1250 A/11, doubtful HE was picked up on the starboard bow but nothing was sighted. It is considered the sounds picked up were from fish.
At 1820 A/11, HMS Unrivalled surfaced for a starsight and to to transmit the attempt to transmit the the signal timed 0515 A/11 again which was successful.
At 1830 A/11, HMS Unrivalled dived, surfacing again at 2200 A/11.
At 2222 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2145 A/11 was received ordering the return to Malta by the reverse of the outward route. (27)
13 Mar 1943 At 1045 A/13, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by a minesweeper (not identified), which had joined at 0825 A/13. (26)

23 Mar 1943 At 1500 A/23, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily between Cape San Vito and Cape Cefalu.
HMS Unrivalled was to pass through position 'P' at 1600 A/25. Patrol orders would be signalled by the Capt. S.8.
24 Mar 1943 At 1610 A/24, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1224 A/24 in which she was ordered to patrol in area 'B'. (5)
26 Mar 1943 At 1030 A/26, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) entered her patrol area.

29 Mar 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) torpedoed and sank the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2201 (1375 GRT, built 1940, former French Bois Rose) and UJ 2204 (1188 GRT, built 1927, former French Boréal) east of Palermo, Sicily near position 38°06'N, 13°26'E.

The two German A/S vessel had been ordered the previous day to conduct an A/S sweep near Cape Milazzo but due to the bad weather conditions this was not possible and they remained anchored three miles to the east of Palermo where they had arrived late in the morning of the 25th.

At 1515 B/29, (note the one hour time zone difference), UJ 2204 was hit. She capsized to port and sank almost immediately. UJ 2201 immediately powered up and commenced to turn round her anchor. When she had turned around 60 degrees a torpedo track was seen passing astern but a second torpedo hit the ship almost underneath the funnel. UJ 2201 immediately took a list to port and started sinking. Six Italian VAS boats and local fishing vessels started picking up survivors. UJ 2201 had three men missing presumed killed while from the crew of UJ 2204 there were five men missing presumed killed.
0509 A/29 - Dived and closed the land in the vicinity of Cape Gallo. Proceeded southwards to make a close periscipe inspection of Palermo harbour.
0850 A/29 - Heard distant explosions.

1134 A/29 - Sighted a large tug, one Siebel ferry and two small schooners, probably auxiliary A/S vessels, circling outside Palermo harbour. Identified one very large merchant vessel, one tanker and one Maestrale-class destroyer laying alongside the eastern mole with their bows pointing to the south.

1230 A/29 - The Siebel ferry, tug and schooners entered Palermo harbour. Sighted a 3000-tons and a 6000-tons merchant vessel anchored to the east of Palermo in positions 38°06'1"N, 13°26'5"E and 38°06'2"N, 13°26'1"E respectively. Later several F-lighters were seen to proceed to these ships. Also motor torpedo boats and more F-lighters were sighted well inshore with bows to seaward and to the southward of the two merchant vessels.
1250 A/29 - Manoeuvred to get into an attacking position.

1414 A/29 - Fired one torpedo at the smaller of the merchant vessels from 600 yards. It hit and the ship quickly sank. The second merchant vessel went ahead immediately and altered course to comb possible torpedoes hoping to drag his anchor.
1417 A/29 - Fired two torpedoes from 800 yards at the larger vessel. The first torpedo hit but the result was not seen. Unrivalled had gone deep and retired to seaward.
1430 A/29 - 1510 A/29 - A fairly heavy counter attack took place in which a total of 51 depth charges were dropped. It is thought the counter attack was carried out mainly by motor torpedo boats.
1530 A/29 - Drew clear to the north-eastward.

1730 A/29 - Returned to periscope depth. The distance to the attack position was now too great to see anything of what was going on down there. Three enemy motor torpedo boats were seen to be in the vicinity of the submarine but these could be easily avoided.
2012 A/29 - Surfaced. Set course to patrol 15 miles to to the north of Cape Gallo - Raisi Point during the night. (28)

30 Mar 1943 At 0510 A/30, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed Cape San Vito. Weather conditions were deteriorating and Lt. Turner decided to patrol at 70 feet only coming up for W/T routines and when under the lee of Cape San Vito.
At 0610 A/30, two explosions were heard.
At 0645 A/30, thirteen explosions were heard thought to be distant depth charges. Several more explosions were heard throughout the day.
At 1630 A/30, course was set to 035° to open up from Cape San Vito.

At 2006 A/30, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and continued on course 035° to comply with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1156 A/29 in which she was ordered to patrol in the southern half of zone 'E' during the night of 30/31 March after which she was to return to zone 'B'.
At 2122 A/30, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1623 A/30 was received which reported an enemy convoy in position 39°33'N, 12°00'E steering 142° at 1610 A/30.

This convoy had been reported earlier in a signal timed 1541 A/30 which had contained the following; Six merchant ships, three destroyers, two motor torpedo boats at 1420 A/30 in position 39°40'N, 12°22'E, course 220°, 10 knots.

Three enemy convoys were at sea this day; 1) The Italian tranports Bivona (former French Socombel, 1646 GRT, built 1930), Giacomo C. (4638 GRT, built 1920) [at 2320 B/29, she signalled she could not keep up with the convoy and was ordered to Palermo escorted by Dezza] and Aquila (former French Algérie, 3386 GRT, built 1910), the German transport Charles Le Borgne (former French, 1426 GRT, built 1920). They were escorted by the Italian destroyer Lubiana, the Italian torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Tifone and the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2205 and UJ 2208 sailed from Naples between 1630 B/29-1800 B/29 proceeding to Bizerte at 7 knots.

2) The Italian transport Belluno (former French Fort de France, 4279 GRT, built 1935) and the German Pierre-Claude (formrr French, 1794 GRT, built 1934) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sagittario, Fortunale (escort leader), Antares and Enrico Cosenz sailed from Naples between 1605 B/29-1730 B/29 for Tunis via Trapani and Favignana.

3) The Italian transports Nuoro (former French Saint Ambroise, 7305 GRT, built 1920), Crema (former French Hébé, 1684 GRT, built 1920), Chieti (former French Arizona, 5457 GRT, built 1925) and Benevento (former French Fort de Souville, 5229 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Cigno (escort leader) and Cassiopea (joined at 1520 B/30 by the corvette Cicogna from Trapani which escorted the steamer Chieti to Palermo) and the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2202 and UJ 2203 sailed from Naples/Pozzuoli/Baia at 1900 B/29-1915 B/29 for Bizerte. (5)

31 Mar 1943 At 0330 A/31, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) re-entered zone 'B'. Nothing had been seen of the reported convoy. Unrivalled now closed Ustica to examine St. Maria Bay but no shipping was seen there. Course was then set to close Raisi Point.
At 1455 A/31, three distant explosions were heard.
At 2055 A/31, HMS Unrivalled surfaced. Nothing of interest had been seen all day. The submarine then patrolled east and west during the night.

At 2058 A/31, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1134 A/31 was received with ordered HMS Unrivalled to patrol in zone 'A' after daylight and then leave patrol to return to Malta so as to pass position 'P' at 0545 A/2.
Lt. Turner decided to carry out a quick inspection of Castellammare Gulf and anchorage at dawn before proceeding to zone 'A'. (29)
1 Apr 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sank the Italian sailing vessel Triglav (former Yugoslav, 231 GRT, built 1920) 4 nautical miles south-east of Cape San Vito, Sicily.
Triglav had two killed, eight survivors were picked up. She was in company with the sailing vessel San Matteo which reported missed by a torpedo. The corvette Cicogna was sent to hunt the submarine.
0501 A/1 - Dived and closed Castellammare for a periscope reconnaissance.
0714 A/1 - The reconnaissance was completed. No ships were sighted. Set course to clear Cape San Vito and enter zone 'A'.
0815 A/1 - HE was picked up. Later sighted two westbound motor torpedo boats and two eastbound F-lighters passing Cape San Vito.

0850 A/1 - When the vessels sighted at 0815 hours were drawing clear, sighted a small auxiliary sailing cutter with a 300-tons three-masted auxiliary schooner. They were escorted by a flying boat. They were rounding Cape San Vito. Started attack on the schooner.
0920 A/1 - In position 38°09.5'N, 12°48'E, fired two torpedoes at the schooner from 1600 yards. One hit was obtained and the ship blew up.
0939 A/1 to 1045 A/1 - Motor torpedo boats counter attacked with a total of Thirty-one depth charges. Unrivalled managed to draw clear to seaward.
1115 A/1 - Came to periscope depth. Two motor torpedo boats were seen laying stopped about 3 miles to the south-eastward. Went to 70 feet to reload No.4 torpedo tube.
1200 A/1 - Nine depth charges were dropped and HE of a destroyer was heard. Set course to draw clear to the north-eastwards.
1345 A/1 - Returned to periscope depth. Set course for position 'P'. Returned to 70 feet as the weather conditions were unsuitable for periscope depth patrol.
1630 A/1 - Heard HE bearing 270°. Returned to periscope depth. Sighted an enemy destroyer proceeding eastwards. Drew clear to the northward. The enemy destroyer was lost from sight 20 minutes later.
1745 A/1 - Heard distant HE bearing 008° but sighted nothing and it soon faded.
2006 A/1 - Surfaced. The wind was decreasing. (5)

1 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1126 A/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); From position 'P' return Malta through positions 'Q' and 'R' to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 0830 A/4. (30)

2 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2346 A/2, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) were informed of the following by the Capt. S.10; 1) HMS Abdiel (Capt. D. Orr-Ewing, RN) will be operating along usual submarine route night of 3rd/4th. HMS Unrivalled is to keep 5 miles to northward of route until south of 36°20'N. 2) Waiting destroyers will be operating to the south of Pantellaria, HMS Unbending and HMS Unshaken are to keep west of 12°00'E and south of 36°35'N until daylight/4. 3) Surface forces are aware submarine dispositions and will observe sanctuary rules. (30)
4 Apr 1943 At 1230 B/4, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by a minesweeper (not identified) which had joined at 1030 B/4. (5)

16 Apr 1943 At 1500 B/16, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily in the Marettimo - Cape San Vito area.
After leaving the swept channel around 1645 A/16, independent exercises were carried out until 1930 A/16.
She was to pass position 'P' at 1600 B/18. Patrol orders would be signalled by the Capt. S.8.
17 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1158 A/17, the Capt. S.8 ordered HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to patrol in zone 'B'. (31)
18 Apr 1943 At 2254 B/18, north of Marettimo, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a white light bearing 080°. Course was altered to 120° to intercept and identify the source of the light.
At 2311 B/18, the source of the white light was identified as a properly illuminated hospital ship proceeding on a course of 215°.
At 2317 B/18, HMS Unrivalled dived in position 38°14'N, 12°00'E and carried out a long range dummy attack on the hospital ship.
[Principessa Giovanna (8389 GRT, built 1923) ???] (5)
19 Apr 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sank the Italian transport Mostaganem (former French, 1942 GRT, built 1921) north of Marettimo Island in position 38°11'N, 11°44'E.

The ship had been damaged by aircraft bombs earlier. She was was on passage from Bizerta to Palermo escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Tifone. Twenty-five survivors were picked up by Tifone, two men were missing.
Later on this day HMS Unrivalled sank the Italian tanker Bivona (former French Socombel, 1646 GRT, built 1930) west of Sicily in position 38°22'N, 12°36'E.

Bivona was escorted by the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2203 and UJ 2208. From a crew and passengers consisting in all of thirty-eight Italians and eighteen German soldiers, only three Italians and five German survivors were picked up by UJ 2208 while UJ 2203 dropped sixty-nine depth charges but without success.

0025 B/19 - Heard an underwater explosion. Sighted two ships thought to be destroyers bearing 220°. Received two enemy reports which identified the contact as a merchant vessel escorted by a torpedo boat. Started attack on the merchant vessel.

0120 B/19 - In position 38°15'N, 12°00'E fired three torpedoes at the almost stationary merchant ship. Range was 1400 yards. One hit was obtained. Unrivalled then took evasive action but the torpedo boat did not start a hunt. Most likely the Italians thought the ship had been attacked from the air as before.
0139 B/19 to 0400 B/19 - Avoided HE and Asdic transmissions that were picked up. At least one destroyer or torpedo boat was hunting but no counter attack developed.
0409 B/19 - Picked up faint HE on the starboard bow but sighted nothing through the persicope. Visibility was very good.
0430 B/19 - Surfaced. Proceeded on course 050° while charging.
0539 B/19 - Dived. Picked up HE on the starboard bow. Later sighted a destroyer / torpedo boat conducting an A/S sweep on course 220°. Drew clear.
0630 B/19 - Set course 090° for area 'B'. Distant explosions were heard during the forenoon.

1455 B/19 - Sighted a flying boat on the starboard bow. Later smoke of a merchant vessel was sighted and finally a convoy came into sight made up of a 5000 tons tanker with two armed merchant vessels on either bow. Three or four flying boats were patrolling overhead. Started attack on the tanker.

1547 B/19 - In position 38°22'N, 12°36'E fired four torpedoes from 1400 yards. At the correct intervals four explosions were heard. During the next observation through the periscope only a large cloud of smoke and fire on the water were seen. Unrivalled then withdrew to the eastward but was detected and depth charged by the enemy. Some were quite close and caused some minor damage.
1800 B/19 - The counter attack ceased. A total of 52 depth charges had been dropped.
1830 B/19 - Reloaded No.4 torpedo tube.
1945 B/19 - Set course 270° to vacate the area and proceed to seaward through zone 'A' to report the torpedo situation.
2050 B/19 - Surfaced. Lt. Turner decided not to remain on patrol in area's 'A' and 'B' with only one torpedo remaining and to return to Malta to reload.
2331 B/19 - Sighted an eastbound vessel bearing 180° at a range of 6 miles. This is thought to be a patrolling destroyer / torpedo boat. The vessel was lost from sight after half an hour. (5)

20 Apr 1943 At 0408 B/20, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) passed her signal timed 0401 B/20 to the Capt. S.8. which stated the following; One torpedo remaining. 1500-tons eastbound motor vessel and 5000-tons westbound tanker sunk. Intend returning to Malta through positions 'P', 'Q' and 'R'. Passing 'P' at 2100 A/20.
At 2145 B/20, HMS Unrivalled dived for an aircraft which flew out of the clouds and started machine gunning the submarine.
At 2215 B/20, position 'P' was reached. Course was altered in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1037 B/20 which ordered her to be at the Malta rendezvous at 0830 B/23. (32)

21 Apr 1943 At 0044 A/21, after diving for an aircraft, HE was picked up by HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN). Later two British destroyers were sighted and identified proceeding at speed on an easterly course. It is thought this were HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) and RHS Adrias (Cdr. I. Toumbas) proceeding to Malta which movements had been reported in the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1120 A/20. (32)
23 Apr 1943 At 1130 B/23, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

She and, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined HMS Unison around 0845 A/23. HMS Unrivalled had joined them half an hour later. (5)
1 May 1943 At 1430 B/1, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily.
Before proceeding on patrol independent exercises were carried out in the Malta submarine sanctuary from around 1730 B/1 to 1915 B/1.
HMS Unrivalled was routed through positions 'R', 'Q' and 'P' and was to pass this last position around 1600 B/3. Patrol orders would be signalled by the Capt. S.8.
No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. (5)

1 May 1943 In a signal timed 1759 B/1, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) of the following; Three destroyers from Malta will be sweeping towards Marittimo tonight routed through position 'R' and returning west of Panellaria. Submarines will not be attacked north of the line Cape Bon, 131°.
This referred to an anti-shipping sweep by HMS Jervis (Capt. A.F. Pugsley, DSO, RN), HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Petard (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Egan, RN). (33)

2 May 1943 In a signal timed 1148 A/2, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) leaving patrol P.M./6 through positions 41°40'N, 10°42'E, 39°25'N, 10°42'E, 38°39'N, 09°39'E at 0800 A/8, thence course 268°, speed of advance 120 miles a day. 2) HMS Trident (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN) is not to enter Tactician's area until after dark/6. 3) HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) after passing position 'P' at 1500 A/3 proceed through zone 'B' to operate in zones 'C', 'F' and 'E' east 14°00'E. 4) HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) operating in zone 'D' after dark/3 and leaving patrol through position 38°42'N, 11°30'E at 0001 A/5 thence course 268°, speed of advance 105 miles a day. (33)
3 May 1943 At 1600 B/3, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) passed through position 'P'. Course was altered to 000° followed by a course change to 063° two hours later. (33)
4 May 1943 At 1834 B/4, south-west of Ustica, in position 38°35'N, 12°57'E, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a vessel with a CANT seaplane overhead. An attack was started.

At 1850 B/4, the vessel was identified as a small patrol craft, similar to a French Chasseur. The vessel was wearing Italian colours, had a gun forward, a high square bridge structure with a funnel the same height at the bridge but abaft the mast. The vessel was camouglaged and had a large group of depth charges aft. An unsuccessful attempt mas made to obtain a periscope photograph at 1600 yards range. The vessel had three figures painted in red on the bow believed to by 210. The enemy was on a steady course of 210° and proceeding at 15 knots. No A/S transmissions or HE were heard. Unrivalled drew clear to the north-eastward.
[Discription of the target looks like this was a Gabbiano-class corvette.] (5)

5 May 1943 At 1312 B/5, north-north-west of Cape Orlando, in position 38°20'N, 14°39'E, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a torpedo boat which was subsequently identified as being of the Sirtori-class. Enemy course was 260°. (5)

6 May 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) attacked the Italian sailing vessel Albina (223 GRT, built 1915) with two torpedoes off Cape Vaticano, Calabria, Italy. The torpedoes however missed their target.
0444 B/6 - Dived and set course to 090° to close Cape Vaticano. Sighted masts bearing 140°.
1007 B/6 - Closed at full speed to identify and attack the vessels of which masts were seen on diving.

1100 B/6 - Identified the vessels as three small southbound coastal schooners. Broke off the attack. At the same time sighted the masts and funnels of two small vessels apparently northbound, bearing 180°. The vessels are thought to be A/S trawlers sweeping. Apparently they had turned round to return to the south towards the Straits of Messina as it was not possible to close the range. The coastal schooners sighted earlier turned to the northward after reaching Cape Vaticano.
1250 B/6 - Sighted a three-masted topsail schooner. She was northbound.

1336 B/6 - Fired two torpedoes from 1400 yards. No hits were obtained. The enemy did not notice the attack and continued on her way. Decided not to surface as it seemed very likely that in this location Unrivalled would be taken under fire from shore batteries before a good deal of damage could be done to the schooner.
1520 B/6 - A second schooner was now sighted. Set course to be in a good position to intercept the schooners further north the following morning.
1815 B/6 - Altered course to 180° to close and investigate smoke. Later sighted and identified a northbound small escort vessel with one black topped funnel and two masts. The vessel was camouflaged.
1900 B/6 - Drew clear to the westward.
2139 B/6 - Surfaced and commenced to patrol towards Stromboli and then to the north-eastward. (5)

7 May 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) destroyed the Italian sailing vessel Albina (223 GRT, built 1915) south of San Lucido, Italy. Only one man was wounded but the vessel was a constructive total loss.
0504 B/7 - Dived and set course to 090° to close the land in the vicinity of Mount Cocuzzo.
1025 B/7 - Sighted a three-masted topsail schooner. She was northbound. The vessel was identified as the schooner we had attacked yesterday.

1101 B/7 - In position 39°13'N, 16°02'E from 1000 yards. After about 400 yards a large splash was seen, this was probably caused by a torpedo collision. This was seen by the schooner. The vessel changed course. Two small machine guns were seen on the schooners stern.

1116 B/7 - Surfaced and engaged with the gun. The crew soon abandoned ship and the vessel was turned towards the shore. 17 rounds of HE (High Explosive) were fired for 7 or 8 hits before the vessel ran on the beach. Dived and drew clear.
1220 B/7 - Sighted a small southbound A/S trawler approaching the schooner.
1245 B/7 - An aircraft was searching in the area as was the A/S trawler. Went deep. One distant depth charge was dropped followed by another later.
Nothing was sighted or happened during the remainder of the day. (5)

9 May 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) torpedoed and sank the small Italian passenger ship Santa Marina Salina (763 GRT, built 1929) south-east off Lipari, Italy. There were forty-eight victims, eleven sailors, fourteen military and twenty-one civilian passengers were picked up by the motorboats M.3 and M.5 and two German patrol boats. The rescue boats were then missed by two torpedoes.
Later a schooner (not identified) towing two other vessels was attacked with one torpedo which missed.
0700 B/9 - Sighted a small 800-tons auxiliary. Closed and commenced attack. Did not fire torpedoes as it seemed unlikely these would run correctly set to 6 or 8 feet in the prevailing sea and swell.
1104 B/9 - Set course to investigate Lipari anchorage.
1524 B/9 - Heard HE and sighted the same 800-tons auxiliary we had seen during the forenoon. She was bound for Milazzo. Started attack.
1540 B/9 - In position 38°25'N, 15°03'E fired three torpedoes. Two hits were obtained and the ship sank. The third torpedo was heard to explode on Vulcano Island.
1618 B/9 - Sighted a two-masted schooner coming out of Lipari. Closed and commenced attack. The schooner was towing two other vessels. All three were flying the Italian Naval Ensign.
1642 B/9 - In position 38°25'N, 15°02'E fired one torpedo. The schooner saw the track and altered away to comb it. The torpedo missed. Unrivalled then drew clear to the northward.

2116 B/9 - Surfaced and set course to comply with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2150 A/8 which had contained the following orders; Leave zone 'E' at 0200 A/10 and proceed south of Ustica and through position 'P' at 0500 A/11 thence to Malta as ordered will be ordered by the Capt. S.10.
In a signal timed 1929 B/9, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unrivalled to arrive Malta 0850 B/13. (5)

10 May 1943 At 2114 A/10, on surfacing, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted two illuminated vessels proceeding eastwards. This were in all likelihood two Swedish relief vessels all submarines on patrol were informed about. (5)
11 May 1943 At 1032 B/11, north-east of Marettimo, in position 38°17'N, 12°18'E, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted a southbound hospital ship.
At 1213 B/11, HE was heard and a destroyer or torpedo boat was seen. The enemy vessel was northbound. Later this vessel altered course to the eastward and passed out of sight at speed.
At 1717 B/11, smoke was sighted of an eastbound vessel to the north of Favignana which is thought to have been another destroyer or torpedo boat. (5)

12 May 1943 At 0028 B/12, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) sighted the lights of a vessel which was later identified as a southbound hospital ship making for Cape Bon. Course was altered to evade.
At 0620 B/12, a small motor launch was seen proceeding close inshore. This was thought to be a rescue launch type of vessel.
During the day several distant explosions were heard. (5)
13 May 1943 Between 1000 B/13 and 1100 B/13, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

She, and HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), entered through the northern swept channel and were escorted in by three minesweepers [not identified in the patrol reports of both submarines] which had joined at 0830 B/13. (5)

31 May 1943 Shortly after 1700 B/31, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (beach reconnaissance for Operation Husky). For this purpose, Unrivalled had a Chariot and a Folbot on board.
She was to proceed through position 36°16'N, 14°17'E, thence on course 005°.
On return from the operation she was to steer direct to position 36°11'N, 14°20'E, thence to the rendezvous position.
On board were two officers, one petty officer and three other ranks to operate the Chariot and Folbot.
1 Jun 1943 At 2227 B/1, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), launched a folbot and a chariot in position 36°53'N, 14°23'E. The Folbot returned at 0034 B/2, the Chariot at 0132 B/2.
0417 B/1 - Dived, closed and identified Scalambri lighthouse. Several small fishing boats were seen off Scoglitti.
During the day a periscope reconnaissance was carried out of beaches '66(3)' and '66(4)' from a range of two to three miles.
2150 B/1 - Surfaced in the launching position for beach '66(3)'.
2227 B/1 - Launched the Chariot and Folbot in position 36°53'N, 14°23.2'E.
0034 B/2 - The Folbot returned.
0059 B/2 - Sighted the lights of the Chariot. The Folbot closed it to home it to the submarine.
0132 B/2 - The Folbot and Chariot were embarked. Both had successfully completed their reconnaissance.
0142 B/2 - Dived and withdrew to seaward. (5)
2 Jun 1943 At 0201 B/2, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) surfaced and set course for Malta.
At 0655 B/2, Unrivalled dived.
At 0710 B/2, the submarine sighted HMS Poole (Lt. W.L.G. Dutton, RNR) and and HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) sweeping to the northward.
At 0911 B/2, the submarine surfaced and joined the minesweepers which were now sweeping to the southward.
At 1036 B/2, HMS Unrivalled secured at the torpedo depot at Malta. (5)
3 Jun 1943 At 1830 B/3, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta to continue her special operation.
One of the other ranks of the party to operate the Chariot and Folbot had been replaced by another person. (5)

4 Jun 1943 At 0428 B/4, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the land in the vicinity of the Scalambri lighthouse. Later course was set to close the beaches for a periscope reconnaissance. The sea and swell increased from the west-north-west and visibility deteriorated and the periscope reconnaissance was abandoned. HMS Unrivalled remained in the vicinity of '66' beach until 1830 B/4 after which she retired to seaward.

At 2214 B/4, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled on courses 040° - 220° about 8 to 15 miles from the shore during the night. The weather conditions were unsuitable for the operation which had to be carried out. (5)

5 Jun 1943 In the evening, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), launched the Folbot and a few minutes later the Chariot but the craft bumped on the casing and was heavily damaged. The operation was abandoned and the Folbot and the Chariot returned to HMS Unrivalled shortly before midnight.
0421 B/5 - Dived and closed the coast in the vicinity of Scoglitti. Visibility was varying. The sea and swell were decreasing. A preliminary periscope reconnaissance was carried out of '36' beach.
Wind, sea and swell decreased further towards sunset and visibility improved.
At 2211 B/5, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and stopped in the launching position; 36°57'N, 14°20'E.
At 2238 B/5, the Folbot was launched.
At 2242 B/5, the Chariot was launched but the craft bumbed badly on the casing during launch and was rendered unserviceable.
At 2346 B/5, the Chariot and Folbot were re-embarked and the operation was abandoned.
At 2359 B/5, HMS Unrivalled dived and proceeded to seaward.
At 0100 B/5, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and set course to return to Malta. (5)
6 Jun 1943 At 1530 B/6, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) returned to Malta after having abandoned the beach reconnaissance operation. The party, Chariot and Folbot were disembarked. (5)
7 Jun 1943 At 1800 B/7, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta to continue her special operation.
An officer, from C.O.P.P. party 5, had been embarked. He was to conduct several periscope reconnaissances. (5)
8 Jun 1943 At 0409 B/8, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the coast near Cape Murro di Porco to conduct a periscope reconaissance of No. '44' beach.
Several small fishing boars were sighted throughout the day and an armed trawler was sene at anchor in Syracuse Bay.
At 2154 B/8, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and retired to seaward. (5)
9 Jun 1943 At 0407 B/9, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the coast for a periscope reconnaissance of '45' and '46' beaches.
At 1030 B/9, the periscope reconnaissance was completed. Course was set to close Syracuse harbour for a periscope reconnaissance.
At 1640 B/9, the inspection of the harbour was completed. Set course 005° to proceed to the approaches to Augusta.
At 1755 B/9, a small vessel was seen at a range of 7 miles. This may have been a destroyer or torpedo boat entering Augusta harbour.
At 1830 B/9, the sweep to the northward was abandoned in order to arrive off the Malta swept channel at 1300 B/10.
At 2207 B/9, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and continued her passage to Malta. (5)
10 Jun 1943 At 1610 B/10, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

24 Jun 1943 At 2030 B/24, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (beach reconnaissance for Operation Husky). For this purpose, HMS Unrivalled had a Chariot and a Folbot on board as well as a party of one Officer and three other ranks to operate these craft.
25 Jun 1943 At 0424 B/25, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) closed the Correnti lighthouse. Course was then set to close '57' beach for a periscope reconnaissance.
At 1445 B/25, having completed the periscope reconnaissance HMS Unrivalled in a position close south of the launching position.
At 1945 B/25, the submarine came to periscope depth to check her position and the weather conditions.
At 2015 B/25, HMS Unrivalled bottomed again.
At 2220 B/25, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and then launched the Folbot in position 36°39.9'N, 15°07'E.
At 2255 B/25, the Folbot was re-embarked as the sea conditions were unsuitable for a swimming reconnaissance. HMS Unrivalled retired to seaward to charge the batteries. (5)
26 Jun 1943 At 0423 B/26, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the Correnti lighthouse. Course was then set to carry out a periscope reconnaissance of Punta Castelluzzo.
At 1330 B/26, the submarine bottomed close south of the launching position.
At 1940 B/26, HMS Unrivalled came up to periscope depth to check her position and the weather conditions.
At 2000 B/26, the submarine bottomed again.
At 2150 B/26, HMS Unrivalled surfaced.
At 2200 B/26, the Folbot was lauched after which the submarine bottomed once more.
At 0047 B/27, HMS Unrivalled surfaced.
At 0135 B/27, the Folbot was re-embarked. HMS Unrivalled then withdrew to seaward and after charging set course to close Cape Scalambri at dawn. (5)
27 Jun 1943 At 0430 B/27, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived. She subsequently carried out a periscope reconnaissance of beaches '65B' and '65C'.
At 1520 B/27, the submarine turned to seaward to assess the current in the vicinity of the beaches.
At 1900 B/27, HMS Unrivalled bottomed in the vicinity of the launching position.

At 2158 B/27, the submarine surfaced and closed the land for half a mile to identify land marks. Visibility was low and varying with a slight sea mist. Shore leading marks were indistinguishable so the Folbot reconnaissance was postponed.
At 2235 B/27, HMS Unrivalled dived and retired to seaward.
At 2340 B/27, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and commenced to charge the battery. (5)
28 Jun 1943 At 0424 B/28, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) dived and closed the land.
At 0724 B/28, the submarine bottomed in the vicinity of the landing position after having identified some shore marks which were visible at night.
At 1800 B/28, HMS Unrivalled came to periscope depth the check the tidal set and the weather conditions.
At 2048 B/28, the submarine bottomed once more in the vicinity of the launching position.
At 2152 B/28, HMS Unrivalled surfaced.
At 2209 B/28, the Folbot was launched.
At 2220 B/28, HMS Unrivalled bottomed.
At 0045 B/29, HMS Unrivalled surfaced.
At 0135 B/29, the Folbot was re-embarked. HMS Unrivalled withdrew 5 miles to seaward and then set course to return to Malta. (5)
29 Jun 1943 At 1040 B/29, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0820 B/29. (5)
6 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta to act as a beacon for operation Husky.
[No log is available and also no report of this operation has so far been located. Therefore some details of this operation will be missing.]
9 Jul 1943 At 2211 B/9, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) launched two folbots to act as marking positions for operation Husky.
At 2300 B/9, from position 36°36.5'N, 14°56'E, HMS Unrivalled commenced to act as a beacon for the invasion convoys approaching the beaches in her section.
10 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) returned to Malta from operations.
17 Jul 1943 Around 1200 B/17, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of the Straits of Messina.
She departed Malta together with HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN). The submarines were escorted until around 0600 B/18 to the west of Pantelleria by HMS Swona (T/Lt. E.C. Davids, RNR).
HMS Unrivalled was to proceed through positions 37°03'N, 11°51'E, 37°09'N, 11°36'E, 38°00'N, 11°35'E, 38°23'N, 12°10'E and 38°24'N, 12°25'E which was to be reached by 2000 B/19.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (34)

19 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1210 B/19 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); Patrol areas north of Messina allocated as follows; Unsparing north of 38°33'N and west of 15°30'E. Unrivalled north of 38°33'N, and east of 15°30'E. Unison south of 38°33'N. Areas are bound by a circle with a radius of 17 miles from position 38°33'N, 15°30'E. (35)

20 Jul 1943 At 0320 B/20, well to the south of Ustica, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sighted a small westbound vessel at a range of 6 miles on bearing 180°. It is thought to be a destroyer / torpedo boat or a large motor torpedo boat. (5)
21 Jul 1943 At 0335 B/21, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) arrived in her patrol area.
