At 0440 B/21, the submarine dived and closed Cape Vaticano.
At 1400 B/21, HMS Unrivalled commenced to patrol on course north and south 3 to 4 miles from Cape Vaticano.
At 1530 B/21, a small sailing boat was seen close inshore.
At 2150 B/21, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled north and south and up and down moon's patch while charging the batteries.
At midnight course was set to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1205 B/21. [See the other event for 21 July 1943 for the text of this signal.] (5)

21 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1205 B/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); Take up following positions on the 22nd from the centre of the circle referred to in my 1210 B/19 at times indicated; Unrivalled 333° - 17 miles at 0200 B/22. Unsparing 270° - 9 miles at 0300 B/22. Unison 219° - 17 miles at 0400 B/22. [Corrupt] in a southerly direction 26 miles. To normal patrol after 1200 B/22, Unison keep to southward of her area. (36)

22 Jul 1943 At 0108 B/22, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received an aircraft report, timed 0035 B/22, that an enemy motor vessel (transport) and two destroyers had been sighted in position 40°00'N, 14°35'E, course 170°, speed 8 knots.
This referred to the Italian transport Alfieri (4573 GRT, built 1943) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Ardimentoso, which were on passage from Naples to Milazzo.
At 0200 B/22, HMS Unrivalled commenced to patrol in the ordered position.
At 0444 B/22, HMS Unrivalled dived.

At 0531 B/22, east of Stomboli, in position 38°46.3'N, 15°22'E, a merchant vessel was sighted. Shortly afterwards two destroyers were seen to be escorting this merchant vessel. They were zigging continuously on a mean course of 155°. Unrivalled seemed to be in a good position for attacking from the enemy's port side after a short run across the enemy's bow, but when the range was 5000 yards, the enemy made a broad alteration of course away. It was now not possible to obtain an attack position and eventually the attack was broken off.
At 0745 B/22, two explosions were heard which were the result of a failed torpedo attack by HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN).
At 0815 B/22, distant explosions were heard which were the counter attack on HMS Unison.
At 0825 B/22, more explosions were heard.
At 0830 B/22, course was altered to 106° to return to the patrol area near Cape Vaticano.
At 2224 B/22, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled north and south and up and down moon during the night. (34)

23 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) made two attacks this day off Cape Vaticano. In the first attack three torpedoes were fired against a tug, all missed. Unrivalled then surfaced to attack with the gun but the gun attack had to be broken off twice. This was the tug Boeo which returned fire and managed to escape.

Not much better luck in the second attack. Four torpedoes were fired at what is thought to be a German U-boat. This was actually the Italian submarine Argento (T.V. Leo Masina) which reported three torpedoes missing astern.
0444 B/23 - Dived.
0506 B/23 - Sighted a tug bearing 210°. Enemy course 040°, speed 11 knots. Started attack.

0521 B/23 - In position 38°38'N, 15°39'E fired three torpedoes. Due to an error in drill Unrivalled broke surface. She quickly dived again and on the next periscope observation the enemy was seen to have turned towards and combed the torpedo tracks.
0525 B/23 - Surfaced and engaged the enemy from 2000 yards with the 3" gun.
0535 B/23 - The enemy returned fire with machine guns. So far the enemy had not been hit by us. Broke off the action and dived.
0540 B/23 - When the range had opened up to 3000 yards surfaced and engaged the enemy again. A round of 3" jammed in the gun and again the action had to be broken off. Dived.
0725 B/23 - Sighted two masts which turned out to be a schooner to the southward of Cape Vaticano.
0730 B/23 - A southbound motor torpedo boat was seen. It is thought this vessel had been sent to search for the attacker of the tug.
0838 B/23 - Sighted an F-lighter to the southward proceeding on course 040°. The motor torpedo boat and schooner were by now no longer in sight.
1459 B/23 - Sighted what is thought to be a German U-boat with an Italian patrol vessel as escort. Range was 4000-5000 yards. Started attack.
1506 B/23 - In position 38°37'N, 15°45'E fired four torpedoes from 4000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1512 B/23 - The U-boat and escort turned and carried out practice gunnery.

1516 B/23 - Three end of run explosions were heard. The U-boat resumed it's original course. The escort stopped to listen for the submarine that had attacked them. Unrivalled then retired to the west.
2150 B/23 - Surfaced and set course to clear the area passing to the north of Stromboli.
2359 B/23 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1529 B/23. [See the other event for 23 July 1943 for the text of this signal.] (5)

23 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1529 B/23, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); 1) Unsparing is to leave leave patrol routed through positions 38°45'N, 15°12'E at 0200 B/26, 38°17'N, 10°49'E at 1200 B/28, 37°42'N, 09°33'E at 0600 B/29 to make rendezvous with an escort vessel flying a black flag in position 37°26'N, 09°33'E at 1200 B/29. 2) Unison is to move to Unsparing's area at 0300 B/26. 3) Unrivalled and Unison are to leave patrol by the same route as above 24 hours later. Unrivalled is to keep 2 miles north and Unison 2 miles south of the route. An escort will be proviced at 1200 B/30. (35)

24 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary minesweeper R 172 / Impero (68 GRT) with gunfire off Amantea, Italy in position 38°59'N, 15°38'E. She was only armed with two 6.5mm machine guns and was unable to resist, four men were taken prisoners and sixteen were left in a lifeboat, two of them were wounded.

0230 B/24 - Passed a signal timed 0015 B/24 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; Your 1529 B/23. Have one torpedo remaining. Missed southbound U-Boat off Gape Vaticano at 1510 B/23. Have withdrawn to northward of area pending further orders.
0444 B/24 - Dived.
0557 B/24 - Sighted a two-masted schooner wearing the Italian Naval Ensign approaching from the Stromboli direction.

0620 B/24 - Surfaced in position 38°59'N, 15°38'E and engaged the target with the 3" and Vickers guns from a range of 1500 yards. After several hits by both guns the enemy started to abandon ship. Ceased fire. Unrivalled now went alongside and the schooner was boarded. Several items and documents were removed. Two demolition charges were then placed. The lifeboat was then approached and the wounded on board it were tended. Four Italians were taken prisoner.
0711 B/24 - Dived and proceeded clear.
0717 B/24 - Surfaced to ventilate the submarine.
0728 B/24 - Dived and set course for Vibo Valentina Marina.
0807 B/24 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0458 B/24 but unfortunately the signal was corrupt. The correct version was received after surfacing in the evening.
This signal contained the following; Acknowledge your 0015 B/24. Return to area and comply with my 1529 B/23.

Between 1600 B/24 and 1915 B/24 - Numerous motor torpedo boats, F-lighters, coastal schooners and landing craft were seen entering and leaving Vibo Valentina Marina. Several landing craft were also seen to the eastward and north-eastward on the beaches. Many trains were seen on the shore.
1915 B/24 - Commenced to withdraw to seaward.
2151 B/24 - Surfaced and continued to withdraw to the seaward.
2205 B/24 - Received the correct version of the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0458 B/24 which ordered HMS Unrivalled back to her patrol area until A.M./27. (5)
25 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) damaged the Italian naval tug Z 10 / Iseo (80 GRT, built 1912) and a schooner with gunfire off Cape Vaticano. Both vessels were beached.

0154 B/25 - Sighted the lights of a hospital ship. It was later seen to be a properly illuminated vessel which was approaching Cape Vaticano on a course of 045° apparently coming from the direction of Milazzo. The hospital ship later turned back on a course of 210°.
0422 B/25 - Sighted a vessel of Cape Vaticano. Closed at full speed.
0457 B/25 - Dived. The vessel seen at 0422 B/25 was identified as a southbound destroyer or torpedo boat.
0524 B/25 - Carried out a dummy attack on the enemy vessel.
0529 B/25 - Sighted one landing craft and one motor torpedo boat off Cape Vaticano. The enemy craft drew clear to the southward. Unrivalled commenced to close the land.
0900 B/25 - Closed a schooner which was seen to the northward and prepared for gun action.
0945 B/25 - Sighted a torpedo boat or destroyer proceeding northwards. Two motor torpedo boats were also seen to the northward.
1100 B/25 - All enemy ships were now no longer in sight.
1130 B/25 - Sighted a small tug towing a two-masted schooner. They were northbound.

1215 B/25 - Surfaced in position 38°36'N, 15°49'E on the enemy's port quarter and engaged both vessels with the gun at a range of 1800 yards and closing. Both ships were beached after being damaged. Unrivalled was then forced to dive due to gunfire from the shore. Retired to seaward.
1245 B/25 - Two enemy A/S vessels were seen closing from the southward and one from the northward. Unrivalled withdrew to seaward but returned to the vicinity of Cape Vaticano around 1530 B/25.
1900 B/25 - Heard bomb explosions. Commenced to withdraw to seaward.
1948 B/25 - Heard more explosions.
2204 B/25 - Surfaced and patrolled north and south and later up and down the moon's path. (5)

26 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) engaged a two-masted schooner with gunfire just to the north of Cape Vaticano. Three hits were claimed before the submarine was forced to break off the action due to gunfire of enemy shore batteries.

0644 B/26 - Sighted the masts of two vessels to the northward. HMS Unrivalled turned towards to close but 15 minutes later the vessels were out of sight. Presumably these vessels were northbound from Milazzo and routed to the westward of the Lipari Islands.
0855 B/26 - Sighted a three-masted schooner under sail proceeding eastwards towards Port San Venere.
0922 B/26 - Sighted smoke bearing 060°.

1035 B/26 to 1114 B/26 - Carried out a dummy attack on a large tug accompanied by a landing craft and a patrol vessel which resembled a motor launch. The one remaining torpedo was not fired as the submarine was unable to get within 1500 1500 yards. The enemy followed the coastline keeping about 3 cables from the shore. It was through the enemy might be attempting to assist the small tug and schooner beached the previous day. The tugs final alteration of course seemed to point that way but he then proceeded southwards.

1131 B/26 - Sighted a northbound two-masted schooner. Lt. Turner intended to engage with the gun off the beach to the immediate northward of Cape Vaticano. The schooner sighted the two beached vessels in the bay to the south of Cape Vaticano and turned in to investigate. Lt. Turned decided to wait until the schooner emerged again northbound.

1130 B/26 - Surfaced in position 38°39.3'N, 15°49.3'E and engaged the schooner with the gun from 2000 yards and closing. The schooner was hit three timed and turned towards the shore. Shore guns opened fire and HMS Unrivalled dived and retired to seaward. The schooner was last seen to resume her course and had not been beached.
1740 B/26 - Heard an explosion.
2200 B/26 - Surfaced and set course to leave the area passing to the north of Stromboli and then as routed in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1529 B/23. (5)
27 Jul 1943 At 0515 B/27, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sighted three motor torpedo boats on the starboard quarter. The submarine dived 5 minutes later.
Later the motor torpedo boats crossed the submarines stern, presumably returning to Palermo.
At 1447 B/27, a loud explosion was heard.
At 2200 B/27, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and continued her passage westwards.

At 2245 B/27, the C-in-C Mediterranean's signal timed 2202 B/27 was received which contained the following orders; Amend route as given in the Capt. S.10;s signal timed 1529 B/27 to pass 5 miles north of Ustica before proceeding to position 38°17'N, 10°49'E.
At 2258 B/27, after decoding the message course was altered to the northward to comply. (5)
28 Jul 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) damaged an Italian sailing vessel with gunfire off Cape Vaticano, Italy. This vessel has not yet (July 2016) been identified.
(All times are zone -2) 1131 hours - Sighted a northbound two-masted schooner.

1330 hours - Surfaced in position 38.39'N, 15.49'E and engaged the schooner with the 3" gun from 2000 yards range and closing. The schooner was hit three times and turned towards the shore. Shore guns opened fire. Unrivalled now dived and retired to seaward. The schooner was last seen to resume her course and was not beached. (5)
30 Jul 1943 At 1415 B/30, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) arrived at Bizerta.
She, and HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) were escorted in from shortly before 1200 B/30, by HMS Foula (T/Lt.Cdr. E.R.O.C. Greenstreet, RNVR). (5)

2 Aug 1943 Around 1500 B/2, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Bizerta together with HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) to join convoy KMS 21 for onward passage to Malta. Late in the evening, shortly after joining the convoy, both submarines were taken under fire by one of ships of the convoy, the US steam tanker Yankee Arrow (8046 GRT, built 1921) which had apparently not been informed about their presence.

HMS Unison was hit on the conning tower and the pressure hull. The officer of the watch, T/Lt. J.P. King, RNR was killed and the commanding officer, Lt. Daniell, DSC, RN was wounded as were the two lookouts, petty officer Day and leading seaman Halliday.
Both submarines then returned to Bizerta escorted by ORP Slazak (Lt.Cdr. R. Nalecz-Tyminski, ORP). (5)
3 Aug 1943 At 0330 B/3, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) arrived back at Bizerta. (5)

4 Aug 1943 After emergency repairs, HMS Unison (Lt. J.E.E.D. Haward, RN) departed Bizerta for diving trials. After these had been completed she and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Bizerta around 1100 B/4, for passage to Malta escorted by HMS Clacton (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) L.S. Shaw, RNR).
Around 0730 B/5, the armed yacht HMS Charles McIver (Lt. R.T. Patterson, RANVR) took over the escort duties. (37)
5 Aug 1943 At 1445 B/5, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

24 Aug 1943 At 1500 B/24, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform two special operations. The first was operation 'Bottom' in which Chariot human torpedoes were to be launched to attack an Italian battleship inside Taranto harbour.
She sailed in company with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) and were escorted out via the eastern swept channel by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR) until 1840 B/24.

HMS Unrivalled was to follow the same route to the Gulf of Taranto as HMS Ultor but then four hours behind her sistership. Routing was as follows; 35°56'N, 16°31'N at 1300 B/25, 37°37'N, 17°32'N at 2359 B/26, 38°38'N, 18°10'N at 1400 B/27.
On board were one officer, one petty officer and two other ranks to operate the Chariot HMS Unrivalled had embarked.

26 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1915 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN); Operation 'Bottom' postponed. 1) Ultor is to proceed to Alice Point and carry out instructions given in paragraph 7 of the sailing orders. Then pass through positions 39°25'N, 17°17'E at 0600 B/29, 38°56'N, 18°34'E at 0300 B/30, 35°45'N, 16°39'E at 1200 B/31 and rendezvous with a minesweeper at 0800 B/1 5 miles east of Q.B.B. 147 (mined area) 2) Unrivalled is to return Malta through positions 38°17'N, 18°10'E at 0600 B/27, 35°45'N, 16°39'E at 1200 B/28 and Rendezvous with a minesweeper at 0800 B/29, 5 miles east of Q.B.B. 147. Surface passage on the 27th. 3) Submarines are not to be attacked unless positive indentification as hostile. (5)

27 Aug 1943 At 0450 B/27, in position 38°03.7'N, 17°58'E, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) spotted a submarine on her port quarter. She was not in a good position for a gun action and the identity was uncertain so Lt. Turner ordered his submarine to dive but could not regain contact after having done so.
The submarine sighted was HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) on her way for a patrol off Cephalonia. She had observed HMS Unrivalled and had dived at the same time. (5)
29 Aug 1943 At 1200 B/29, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR) which had joined at 0830 B/29. (5)
2 Sep 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Brindisi.
She was escorted out until around 1745 B/2 by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR).

HMS Unrivalled was routed as follows; 35°56'N, 16°31'E at 0800 B/3, 36°24'N, 16°47'E at 2000 B/3, 39°10'N, 18°30'E at 2000 B/4, 39°45'N, 18°52'E, 40°20'N, 18°52'E at 1200 B/5, 41°10'N, 18°00'E at 0200 B/6 thence course 270° to proceed to patrol off Bari. Unrivalled was to proceed on the surface on the 4th.

4 Sep 1943 At 0550 B/4, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sighted another submarine which was southbound. She dived and so did the other submarine following which S.S./T. communication was established with ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski).
Dzik, which had sailed from Malta for patrol on 1 September was returning to Malta due to a defective muffler valve. (5)
6 Sep 1943 At 0800 B/6, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) made landfall off Bari. She then closed the harbour.

Later she sighted a large two-funnelled hospital ship, a tanker, a freighter and four other ships inside Bari harbour. All vessels had steam up and the hospital ship shifted billet during the forenoon.
At 1910 B/6, the hospital ship sailed. The tanker was then seen to shift billet as dusk fell and it was hoped she too would sail but apparently she secured to the west wall of the harbour.
At 2000 B/6, HMS Unrivalled commenced to withdraw to seaward.
At 2025 B/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled along the approach route to Bari during the night but nothing was seen. (5)
8 Sep 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) attacked a small merchant vessel off Bari with three torpedoes. No hits were obtained. This ship has not yet (July 2016) been identified.

0302 B/8 - While patrolling about 3 to 6 miles from the shore on the approach route to Bari, Unrivalled encountered two enemy destroyers / torpedo boats. The submarine dived to evade surfacing again when clear of the enemy.
0420 B/8 - Again two enemy destroyers / torpedo boats were encountered. Unrivalled dived once more and surfaced again when clear.
0454 B/8 - Yet again two enemy destroyers / torpedo boats were encountered and Unrivalled dived once more to avoid being sighted. As dawn was approached she remained down for the day.

1030 B/8 - Sighted three enemy destroyers / torpedo boats patrolling along the approach route from Bari. They were about two miles distance and were sweeping to seaward. Unrivalled tried to pass between them but when doing so the enemy apparently made contact as one of the enemy vessels turned towards and increased speed. Lt. Turner took his submarine deep and took avoiding action. A hunt was started by the enemy but they did not appear to remain in contact for any length of time. One pattern of depth charges was dropped fairly close and other depth charges were dropped singly or in pairs. It looked like the enemy was trying to keep the submarine down while a ship got clear of the harbour. All attempts to return to periscope depth were frustrated by the enemy closing or by the dropping of a depth charge.

1235 B/8 - Finally returned to periscope depth. Unrivalled had meanwhile withdrawn to the north-eastward. About 5 to 6 miles to the southward two destroyers / torpedo boats were sighted leading a small merchant vessel out on the reverse of the Bari approach route. Later the escorts were seen to turn to the southward while dropping more depth charges.

It was appreciated that the merchant vessel might turn to a 315° course at the end of the swept channel to clear the Vieste headland. Although there were other courses open to the enemy, this one was the only one which gave Unrivalled a chance to attack. A high speed approach to cut the enemy off was made. The enemy turned to the northward as had been hoped for and an attack was commenced.

1329 B/8 - In position 41°12'N, 16°59'E fired three torpedoes at a the merchant vessel, which was of about 800 to 1000 tons. Range was 1000 yards. No hits were obtained. The first torpedo was seen to pass ahead but the second torpedo is thought to have run under the target. The enemy had seen the torpedo tracks and increased to full speed and made off for Bari followed by Unrivalled which was meanwhile reloading the torpedo tubes which had been fired.

Between 1630 B/8 and 1730 B/8, the two destroyers / torpedo boats were seen to close Bari while conducting an A/S sweep. They circled round close to Unrivalled but were easily avoided after which the enemy withdrew to seaward.
They were not seen again and it is thought they had been recalled to harbour having been ordered to 'cease hostilities'.
HMS Unrivalled kept a close patrol off Bari until after dark. The tanker seen on the 6th was no longer seen inside the harbour.
2042 B/8 - Surfaced and patrolled on the approach route to Bari and to the northward.
2234 B/8 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1935 B/8. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 8 September 1943.] Course was set to proceed to seaward. (5)

8 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1932 B/8, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea on patrol in enemy waters. This were ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); Cease hostilities against Italy. Submarines identified as German are to be sunk. Maintain all normal precautions for your own safety. Sokol return to Malta by reverse of outward route E.T.A. 0800 B/11. Unruly conform with my signal timed 0955 B/8, 24 hours early. Unrivalled is to return, by same route 24 hours after . Unshaken conform with my signal timed 1115 B/7. (38)

9 Sep 1943 At 0340 B/9, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 090155 B/9, which contained the following; Patrol 'Weight' continues pending further instructions HMS Unshaken and HMS Unruly returning to Malta as ordered in my signals timed 1115 B/7 and my 0955 B/8. Only German vessels are to be attacked. Italian shipping has been directed to proceed to Allied ports.

At 1725 B/9, a small northbound escort vessel similar to the a French Chasseur-type was seen. The vessel appeared to be flying a pendent at the yard arm which was thought to be a blue on which had been ordered to be worn by all Italian naval vessels. The ship was approaching Bari.
Unrivalled closed Bari harbour and during the night of 9/10 September she close the breakwater twice to inspect the harbour. Several merchant vessels were still sighted in port. (5)

10 Sep 1943 After the Italian surrender, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN), entered Bari harbour. The Italians were ordered to form of convoy of all merchant ships in the harbour that were seaworthy. Unrivalled then left the harbour again and waited for the convoy to form up. Around 1800 B/10 the convoy of eight ships was outside the harbour and then set course for Malta escorted by HMS Unrivalled.

The ships in the convoy were the following; Lucrino (The master of this vessel was appointed as convoy Commodore) (4372 GRT, built 1917), Acilia (354 GRT, built 1912), Apuania (????), Costante C. (869 GRT, built 1901), Montebello (630 GRT, built 1918), Luana (1158 GRT, built 1892), Pelagosa (671 GRT, built 1921) and Rinucci (1217 GRT, built 1914).
At 0405 B/10, the convoy was joined by HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN).
0546 B/10 - Dived.
0605 B/10 - Sighted a southbound merchant vessel and escort vessel entering Bari harbour.

0743 B/10 - Sighted and closed a small southbound trawler intending to surface and order him to take one officer and armed guard with a message to the Italian Naval Commander at Bari. A lengthy stalk was broken off when two more promising and slightly larger trawlers, one of which wearing the Italian naval ensign appeared.

0913 B/10 - Surfaced and ordered one of the trawlers to proceed to Bari with S.Lt. J. Evans, RN and able seaman D. Lower on board. Both Commanding Officers of the trawlers were held onboard Unrivalled as hostages until Evans and Lower returned. The submarine then closed Bari harbour and patrolled outside with the gun and Vickers gun manned.

1024 B/10 - A Regolo-class cruiser and a destroyer / torpedo boat passed about 2000 yards across the bow of the submarine. They were southbound. All torpedo tubes were at the ready and the cruiser was seen to have her director and 'B'-turret trained on the submarine. Fortunately no 'incident' occurred.

Whilst of the harbour and waiting for the return of Evans and Lower, the submarine had always one and sometimes two 'escorts' varying from a naval tug to small naval patrol vessels / motor launches. A torpedo boat escorted a small merchant vessel into the harbour.

1145 B/10 - Evans and Lower returned in a motor boat and an Italian officer who took verbal orders from Lt. Turner and made arrangements for the submarine to berth in the harbour. Lt. Turner considered this a necessary though undesirable step in order to make more definite arrangements with the Italian Admiral and to hold a convoy conference. The Italian Admiral had apparently fallen in with Lt. Turner's demand that all available merchant shops should sail that evening in convoy for a British port under Lt. Turner's order and in compliance with the Armistice terms.
1330 B/10 - Passed a signal, timed 1158 B/10 to the Capt. S.10 reporting the situation at Bari. This was done before entering harbour.

1333 B/10 - Secured along the inner harbour mole. No precautions were relaxed. A curious, motley and dirty looking crowd in various uniforms formed. They were certainly not unfriendly but lacked all order, discipline and dignity. S.Lt. Evans was again sent to see the Admiral. While Lt. Turner was finishing his convoy orders, the First Lieutenant, Lt. C.H. Hammer, RN was left on the bridge in charge of the submarine.

Evans later returned with the Admiral and the officer who had boarded the submarine in the morning. The Admiral was a broken man but he was pleased that the war with the Allies was over and that he never had wanted it.

The Admiral promised that eight ships could sail at 1800 hours and that it possibly may be nine. Meanwhile there were reports that German forces were arrived but these were not true but as this was at that moment not known to be the case Lt. Turner ordered the Admiral to sail the ships at once and that Unrivalled would be waiting outside the harbour for them.
From 1510 B/10 to 1610 B/10, Unrivalled patrolled on the surfaced outside the harbour with the gun and Vicker's gun manned. There were no signs of the Germans entering the town or harbour.

It was decided that if at 1615 B/10, the ships for the convoy were not moving the submarine would re-entered the harbour in order to hold a convoy conference and make certain of the convoy. This however proved unnecessary as the Italian Admiral did not fail Lt. Turner. As Unrivalled moved to re-enter the harbour the first ship was seen to be manoeuvring to leave. Two small trawlers entered the harbour from seaward.

By 1745 B/10 - A motley assortment of eight old and small merchant vessels had assembled outside the harbour and most had been boarded. Some sent boats with representatives varying from master to cook and Lt. Turner wished more than ever that the opportunity of a conference had not been lost. S.Lt. Evans and telegraphist Bryne were transferred to the largest vessel, the Lucrino, which was to act as Commodore's ship. Copies of the sailing instructions were sent to most of the ships and the convoy sailed at 1800 B/10. All but two of the convoy's ships names were known. Some of the masters understood English but some did not. None had signalmen or signalling lamps. Even the Commodore's ship had to be given a lamp. Cargoes were not known though some vessels were obviously light and one or two were laden. (5)

12 Sep 1943 At 0437 B/12, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 0430 B/12 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; My 1158 B/10. Position, course and speed at 0600 B/12, 38°56'N, 18° 35'N, 206° at 6 knots. Expect to arrive at position 'B' of Q.B.B. 147 at 0800 B/14. HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) had joined the convoy.
At 0715 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0605 B/12 was received which contained the following; Your 0430 B/12. Stick it. Escorts will be sent when they can be spared.
At 1510 B/12, identity signals were exchanged with a Baltimore aircraft which was to form the air escort.

At 1825 B/12, the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1138 B/12 was received which contained instructions for HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR) which was to escort the convoy to Bengasi and relieve the submarines at 1800 B/13. (5)

13 Sep 1943 At 0722 B/13, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) passed a hand message to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) which contained instructions and notes on peculiarities of the convoy for HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR). As the convoy could not be at the rendezvous by 1800 B/13, HMS Unruly was sent ahead to contact the corvette and lead her back to meet the convoy.

At 0811 B/13, while HMS Unruly was still near the convoy and HMS Unrivalled was abeam of the second ship of the port columnm the latter complaining that she had no food, when a two-engined German bomber, at first thought to be a Beaufighter, fles out from up the sun and dive-bombed the convoy. A large bomb fell well clear of all ships. The aircraft was out of Vicker's gun range before it was identified as enemy. The 3" gun was manned but the enemy made iff befire any further action. HMS Unruly fired one found at the aircraft with her 3" gun.

At 0854 B/13, HMS Unrivalled passed her signal timed 0820 B/13 to the Capt. S.10. The signal contained the following; Attacked by enemy aircraft in position 36°52'N, 17°28'E, course 210°, speed of the convoy 6 knots. No damage but request fighter protection.
At 0930 B/13, 56 hours of provisions were passed to the mechant vessel which had requested those.
At 1400 B/13, S.Lt. Evens and telegraphist Bryne were transferred from the Commodore's ship to HMS Unrivalled.

At 1402 B/13, the C-in-C Mediterranean's signal timed 0924 B/13 was received which ordered the convoy to proceed to Malta for fuel if necessary before onward sailing to Algiers. It was now decided to remain with the convoy as their peculiarities were by now well known to Lt. Turner. It was decided to release HMS Unruly after she had brought HMS Bergamot to the convoy. It was also decided to return S.Lt. Evans and telegraphist Bryne to the Commodore's ship. This was accomplished at 1700 hours. They returned to HMS Unrivalled at 1745 B/14.
At 1840 B/13, HMS Bergamot and HMS Unruly were sighted.
At 1930 B/13, HMS Unrivalled turned over the convoy to HMS Bergamot and the submarine now took station astern. (5)
14 Sep 1943 At 1820 B/14, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
27 Sep 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (39)
2 Oct 1943 At 1600 A/2, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the south-west Aegean.
She was escorted out until 1850 A/2 by HMS Brixham (T/Lt. C.H. Walton, RNR).

HMS Unrivalled was routed as follows; 35°50'N, 15°17'E at 2030 A/1, 35°47'N, 17°30'E at 2000 A/3, 35°38'N, 23°01'E at 0500 A/5, thence to the west Aegean (zone 'SQ') for patrol. [Apparently HMS Unrivalled sailed late, or the times must have been adjusted.]

3 Oct 1943 At 1149 A/3, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1151 C/2, which contained the following orders for HMS Unrivalled; From last position mentioned (35°38'N, 23°01'E) dive to 200 feet through Antikithera Channel on course 066° by day 5. Then patrol patrol between the approaches to Souda Bay and south of Milos. (40)

4 Oct 1943 At 2103 A/4, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1121 C/4 which contained the following orders for her; HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) will be patrolling in the vicinity of Kos Island to attack shipping landing enemy troops. You have freedom of action in the western Aegean. Suggest you start north of Milos working down to Suda Bay thence Candia and close to coast east of Candia. (40)

6 Oct 1943 At 2115 B/6, when HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) was to the north of Milos smoke was sighted bearing 290°. From the smoke it was seen that the vessel was northbound and Lt. Turned altered course to 340° to close at full speed. The bearing remained constant and the form of the ship eventually became visible above the horizon.

Course was then altered to 330°. Estimated enemy course was 350°, speed 8 knots. It was intended to gain a position ahead of the target and then dive to attack as the moon and calm sea made a night surface attack almost impossible.

At 2142 B/6, HMS Unrivalled dived for an aircraft which flew overhead. It was thought to be a Wellington but the aircraft altered course when the submarine dived and the identity could not be established.
At 2152 B/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and continued the chase but five minutes later she again had to dive for an aircraft.
At 2201 B/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and once more continued the chase.

At 2215 B/6, Unrivalled was gaining bearing rapidly. The enemy had either altered course or was stopped. One explosion had been heard shortly before and was thought to have been a bomb from an aircraft. Course was altered to 280° to close. The enemy appeared to be end on and looked like a destroyer or small warship. Up to this time it was thought it may have been the small merchant vessel of about 500-tons that had been reported at Milos at 1535 Z/6 by aircraft.

At 2220 B/6, Unrivalled dived when the range to the enemy was about 5000 yards. No HE was heard although this had been heard distinctly previously. The bearing remained constant, right ahead. As it was thought possible that the enemy had been damaged by a bomb speed was increased to close submerged. Enemy HE was then again picked up and soon afterwards the enemy vessel resumed it's course of about 345° at 8 knots. The enemy was still too far away to be identified.
At 2229 B/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and commenced the chase the enemy on it's starboard quarter. The submarine was slowly gaining bearing and the silhouette gave the impression of a small warship.

At 2239 B/6, the enemy appeared to alter course towards and HMS Unrivalled dived. This was later thought to have been just a zig-zag and the submarine surfaced shortly afterwards and resumed the chase but it was soon realised that an attack position could not be reached before the latitude of the St. Georgio Island minefields. Also the enemy had increased speed slightly and his speed was now estimated as being 8.5 knots so at 2304 B/6, the chase was reluctantly broken off and course was set to the southward.

The ship chased by HMS Unrivalled was most likely the German transport Sifnos (315 GRT, built 1891) which had departed Milos at 1900 B/6 and arrived at Piraeus at 0520 B/7. This vessel was originally constructed as yacht. (41)

8 Oct 1943 Off Serifos, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) attacked the German auxiliary minelayer Bulgaria (1188 GRT, built 1894) with three torpedoes. All missed. Bulgaria was transporting troops from Piraeus to Kos together with German minelayer Drache. [No exact position is given in the patrol report but a monthly report of the Capt. S.1 gives the position of the attacks as 37°04'N, 24°22.5'E.]
0542 B/8 - Dived an closed Serifos Island to patrol on the inshore route and to inspect harbour inlets.

0832 B/8 - Sighted two small merchant vessels on the port beam. They were escorted by three CANT aircraft. Commenced attack on the larger vessel. The patrol report does not give a position for this attack and there is no log available for this month.

0905 B/8 - Fired three torpedoes at the target from 2000 yards. All torpedoes missed, most likely astern. The enemy saw the tracks and turned towards, transmitting on Asdics but he soon re-joined his consort after having fired 17 rounds with a stern gun thought to be 4". Unrivalled meanwhile remained at periscope depth as the aircraft were not near.

1545 B/8 - Whilst carrying a reload of No.2 torpedo tube the torpedo commenced running and the 'hot run cock' had to be opened when close to the land. A stream of bubbles must have been visible on the surface. Course was set away from the land. It was not necessary to jettison the torpedo but on retracting it, it was found to be defective. Also No.2 tube was out of action for repairs for a period of 14 hours.

1923 B/8 - Surfaced and set course to pass between Anti-Milos and Falconera Island and then between Milos and the Anoes group to patrol in a southerly direction on the main shipping route towards Suda Bay.

At 2322 B/8, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2217 C/8 was received which contained the following orders; 1) Proceed with all despatch to patrol between Stampalia and Sophrana keeping west of 26°20'E to intercept possible attack on Leros. 2). HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is to patrol between Stampalia and Amorgos. 3) Own surface forces may be operating east of your boundary line.
Course was changed immediately to comply with the orders received. (42)

9 Oct 1943 At 1046 B/9, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0754 C/9 was received which ordered HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) to patrol in the billet of HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) as this submarine has now left patrol. Course was set to 037° to comply with these orders, passing between Santorin and Anaphi Islands to proceed to the area between Stampalia and Amorgos. (5)

10 Oct 1943 At 0350 B/10, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0126 C/10 which contained the following orders; 1) After 0500 C/10 close in to cover the western approaches to Leros. The 26°20'E boundary line is cancelled. 2) Kalimno channel may be newly mined.
At 0400 B/10, course was set to comply with these orders passing between Amorgos and Liadhi.
At 1840 B/10, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled inside Q.B.B. 163 (?) (mined area) between Patmos and Leros.

During the night of 10/11 October, HMS Unrivalled dived three times to evade small patrolling vessels at first thought to be enemy but later considered to be our own motor launches or patrol vessels from Leros. This vessel was also seen to patrol up and down the coast of Leros. (5)

12 Oct 1943 At 0442 B/12, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2308 C/11 to HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) and which was also repeated to HMS Unrivalled, which contained the following; Enemy report from Leros at 1750 C/11. Kalimno Island (A) Palaio Bay north east coast two merchant ships (B) Atsipa Bay two merchant ships.

This was most disappointing to HMS Unrivalled who had been in the western bay at that time. It was felt the delay in receipt of this signal (nearly 12 hours after Leros had originated it) would give HMS Trooper little chance to take succesful action. [Unbeknown to the British at that time was that HMS Trooper had already been lost before she was ordered to patrol off Leros.]

At 0517 B/12, HMS Unrivalled dived and closed Kalimnos Island. Lt. Turner decided to close and inspect all bays and possible anchorages off the west coast down to and including the port of Kalymnos itself. All bays and inlets were closely inspected but nothing was sighted.
At 1415 B/12, a small fishing caique was seen.
At 1517 B/12, HMS Unrivalled dived to 120 feet to cross the suspected minefield between Kos and Cagios Georgio, Kalimnos.

At 1612 B/12, HMS Unrivalled returned to periscope depth and closed the port of Kalimnos. A small merchant vessel and one large schooner were sighted with several smaller craft. All were behind the breakwater. The vessels were seen to fly swastika flags. Unrivalled closed to try to fire a torpedo at the stern of the merchant vessel. A Cant seaplane arrived, landed and later took off again while Unrivalled was closing the harbour. When close in it was seen that only a tiny portion of the stern was open from the breakwater, an impossible shot. It was thus decided to abandon the attempt to fire through the breakwater and creep back out to seaward. While doing so a red Verey light was fired from a building in the harbour and a crowd assembled on the wharf. The bridge and guns on the merchant vessel were manned at it was suspected the submarine had been detected. Also the Cant aircraft commenced to patrol off the harbour entrance. A large two-masted schooner left harbour and approached the submarine while transmitting on Asdic.

At 1728 B/12, HMS Unrivalled went to 130 feet. Six depth charges were dropped in pairs of two, the first of which were very close and caused some minor damage. The schooner was shaken off when HMS Unrivalled changed course to pass the Cape Agios Georgios minefield.

According to German records the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2101 sighted a periscope at 1604 B/12. The schooner which was hunting and which dropped depth charges was the GA 44. The aircraft sighted and patrolling was a German Arado which was carrying General Müller (offsite link).

At 1855 B/12, HMS Unrivalled returned to periscope depth. A small eastbound vessel was thought to have been sighted against the land on the starboard beam and others on the port quarter. An attack was commenced but trim was lost and when this was regained 15 minutes later there were no more vessels in sight.
At 1940 B/12, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and commenced to patrol to seaward.
At 2119 B/12, two enemy reports, timed 1725 Z/12 and 1815 Z/12 were received of westbound ships near Levitha Island so patrolled towards this island.

At 2241 B/12, HMS Unrivalled dived on sighting the light cruiser HMS Sirius (Capt. P.B.W. Brooking, DSO, RN) and two destroyers [this were HMS Pathfinder (Cdr. E.A. Gibbs, DSO and 3 Bars, RN) and HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, DSO, RN)] proceeding on course 035° at 28 knots.
At 2320 B/12, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and resumed patrol.

At 0002 B/13, HMS Sirius, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Penn were seen again and HMS Unrivalled dived and carried out a dummy attack on HMS Sirius which was proceeding on course 160° at 27 knots. HMS Unrivalled surfaced again at 0029 B/13 and continued her patrol. (43)

13 Oct 1943 At 05192 B/13, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled four miles to the south of Levitha Island. Visibility was low and the sea and swell were unpleasant. Towards sunset the weather conditions improved.
At 1835 B/13, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and continued to patrol to the south of Levitha Island until 2100 B/13 when course was set to close Kalimnos and Leros. (5)

14 Oct 1943 At 0540 B/14, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) dived. Gunfire from Leros shore batteries was noticed during the forenoon. Later enemy air raids on gun positions on southern Leros and Port Lakki were seen to take place.
At 1300 B/14, HMS Unrivalled altered course to the southwards to leave the area.
At 1500 B/14, a small sailing vessel was seen to enter Argynonta Bay on Kalymnos.

At 1834 B/14, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and set course to comply with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1758 C/12 which had contained the following orders; HMS Surf (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN) passes Kaso Straits during daylight 14th to relieve you. You are to adjust patrol so as to pass position 'A' 35°16'N, 26°36'E at 2305 C/15th thence on the surface through position 'B' 34°28'N, 27°28'E at 0658 C/16 to arrive at Beirut at 0702 C/18.

Lt. Turner also expected that the light cruiser HMS Sirius (Capt. P.B.W. Brooking, DSO, RN) and the destroyers HMS Pathfinder (Cdr. E.A. Gibbs, DSO and 3 Bars, RN) and HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, DSO, RN) would make another sweep during the night of 14/15 October. [This was not the case as they had returned to Alexandria.]
Lt. Turner therefore decided to proceed west of Stampalia and carry out a brief submerged daylight sweep of the coast of Crete between Cape Saint John and Sitia Bay before leaving the Aegean. (44)
15 Oct 1943 At 0556 B/15, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) dived and closed Cape Saint John, Crete at speed.

At 1106 B/15, the masts of a small caique were seen near Cape Saint John and later 5 miles on the starboard quarter. The caique at first appeared to be on an easterly course and closing. Five minutes later the caique altered course towards the west and was soon lost from sight. HMS Unrivalled then inspected Mirabello Bay and later Sitia Bay but nothing was sighted in either bay.
At 1846 B/15, HMS Unrivalled surfaced to pass to the north of the Dionysades Islands and comply with the route given in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1758 C/12. (5)
18 Oct 1943 At 0700 B/18, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (5)
29 Oct 1943 At 1030 C/29, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Beirut for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean.
At 1730 C/29, HMS Unrivalled returned to Beirut to make good a defect to the packing of the starboard stern gland.
At 2030 C/29, with the repairs completed, HMS Unrivalled departed again to resume her patrol.
At 2115 C/29, a trial dive to 100 feet was made to check the repaired stern gland. All was found to be good and at twenty minutes later the submarine surfaced and proceeded as routed. (5)

2 Nov 1943 At 0105 B/2, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2246 B/2 which contained the following orders; Shift patrol to between Armorgos and Anaphi. (45)

4 Nov 1943 In a signal timed 1456 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) Unruly moving to patrol between Amorgos and Anaphi. Unrivalled moving to patrol in the west Aegean at the Commanding Officers discretion except that disturbance required in Milos possibly 5th/6th. 2) Unrivalled leave patrol diving to 200 feet through Antikithera Channel on 8th on course 246° from position 35°52'N, 23°41'E for 36 miles. 3) Capt. S.10 is requested arrange onward routeing to Malta.

In a signal timed 2206 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled and HMS Unruly; 1) Cancel paragraph 1 of my signal timed 1456 B/4 and resume previous patrols. Reports indicate enemy movements from westward shortly. 2) Unruly cancel my signal timed 1811 B/2 as destroyers will shoot up Amorgos during the night of 5/6 November. (46)
4 Nov 1943 At 2122 B/4, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1456 B/4 [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' listed for 4 November 1943.]
At 2130 B/4, course was set to comply with the orders in the above signal passing between Anaphi and Santorini before proceeding west towards Milos.
At 2322 B/4, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2206 B/4 was received. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' listed for 4 November 1943.]
At 2333 B/4, course was set to comply with the latest singal, returning to patrol to the south of Amorgos. (5)

5 Nov 1943 At 0745 B/5, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sighted a motor launch-type vessel passing down the port side on course 300°. Two men were seen amidships and one aft manning a light machine gun. No attack was made so as not to disclose the submarines position as eastbound enemy traffic was expected. The vessel seemed to be on passage from Stampalia to either Naxos or Paros.
At 1832 B/5, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and patrolled between Amorgos and Anydros.
At 2230 B/5, HMS Unrivalled altered course to patrol to the south of Anydros to keep clear of the destroyer patrol referred to in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1235 B/5.
At 2358 B/5, HMS Unrivalled sighted two destroyers off the South-Amorgos on course 125° proceeding at 25 knots. The submarine dived to avoid detection.
This must have been HMS Faulknor (Capt. M.S. Thomas, DSO, RN) and HMS Echo (Lt.Cdr. R.H.C. Wyld, RN) which carried out an anti-shipping sweep near Amorgos during the night of 5/6 June. (5)
6 Nov 1943 At 0018 B/6, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) surfaced and resumed patrol between Amorgos and Anydros.
At 0535 B/6, the submarine dived and continued her patrol in the same area.

At 1440 B/6, in position 36°40'N, 25°47'E a floating object was sighted on the port quarter which was later identified as a small boat containing two men. The sea and swell were flat calm. The boat was not approached closely until 1600 hours when a slight breeze broke the glassy surface. The boat was kept in sight and its movements were kept under observation.
At 1730 B/6, the boat was closed and recognised as an RAF rubber dinghy.
At 1803 B/6, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and embarked two RAAF airmen of 603 Beaufighter Squadron. The dinghy was also embarked.

At 2125 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1853 B/6 was received which was addressed to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC). Given the orders that were transmitted to these two submarines, Lt. Turner decided to leave this area to comply with the onward routing instructions received earlier. He decided to sweep round Iraklia, Ios and Santorin the folling day. At 2332 B/6, 2335 B/6 and 2350 B/6, aircraft reports timed 2240 Z/6, 2105 Z/6 and 2230 Z/6 were received which reported one merchant vessel, twelve barges and two motor torpedo boats southbound between Paros and Naxos.
At 2345 B/6, HMS Unrivalled set course to patrol to the south-east of Iraklia to intercept the enemy if they took the route Iraklia - Amorgos.
The enemy vessels reported were from a large landing force made up of mostly smaller vessels which were proceeding in two sections from Paros to Amorgos. (5)
7 Nov 1943 At 0001 B/7, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) sighted flares bearing 334° to the northward of Iraklia.
At 0110 B/7, the submarine arrived in the selected patrol position, to the south-east of Iraklia.
At 0124 B/7, flares and bomb flashes were seen to the north-west of the Island. It is thought the enemy was being attack by aircraft.

At 0133 B/7, two more flares were sighted. It was expected the enemy would come in sight round Avelas Rock at any time but nothing furher was sighted until 0425 B/7 when distant flares were sighted in a northerly and east-south-easterly direction.

[The destroyers HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, DSO, RN) and HMS Pathfinder (Lt.Cdr. C.W. Malins, DSC and Bar, RN) had conducted an anti-shipping sweep in the Paros / Naxos area during the night and shortly after midnight they had sunk an enemy caique. This was the German patrol vessel GA 45.]
At 0543 B/7, HMS Unrivalled dived and set course to close Ios.
At 0930 B/7, a small sailing boat was sighted and closed.

At 1026 B/7, HMS Unrivalled surfaced next to the boat and hailed it alongside. The tree occupants were Greek fishermen and the boat carried nothing but two lines and fishing tackles. They were allowed to proceed on their way. They were cheerful and grateful.
At 1033 B/7, HMS Unrivalled dived and set course for Santorini.

At 1640 B/7, the submarine entered Santorini harbour through the northern entrance. Nothing was sighted though and the submarine left the arbour at 1845 B/7 passing through the channel to the north of Aspronisi Island.
At 1930 B/7, HMS Unrivalled surfaced and set course for the Antikithera Channel. (5)
10 Nov 1943 At 2030 B/10, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR) which had joined at 1640 B/10. (5)
This convoy departed Port Said at 1130 A on 12 November 1943.

On departure from Port Said this convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Buteshire (British, 6590 GRT, built 1912), City of Derby (British, 6616 GRT, built 1921), Comedian (British, 5122 GRT, built 1929), Coulgorm (British, 6997 GRT, built 1942), Empire Voice (British, 6828 GRT, built 1940), Jean L.D. (French, 5795 GRT, built 1935), Magician (British, 5105 GRT, built 1925), Rodsley (British, 5000 GRT, built 1939), Sampa (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Samshire (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).
On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by the corvette HMS Alisma (Lt. G. Lanning, RANVR) and the A/S whaler HMS Falk (T/Lt. W. Metcalfe, RNVR).

At 0830 A/13, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Alexandria; Defender (British, 8078 GRT, built 1915), Director (British, 5107 GRT, built 1926), Empire Falstaff (British, 7067 GRT, built 1943), Empire Farmer (British, 7049 GRT, built 1943), Fort Yale (British, 7132 GRT, built 1942), Magdala (Dutch (tanker), 8248 GRT, built 1931), Samarina (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Samphire (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).
The Defender returned to Alexandria with engine defects though.
They were escorted by the frigate HMS Aire (A/Lt.Cdr. N.K. Boyd, DSC and Bar, RNR), escort destroyer Miaoulis (?) and the corvette HMS Spiraea (Lt. A.H. Pierce, OBE, RNR).
At 1640 A/14, the minesweeper HMS Whitehaven (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) G.W.A.T. Irvine, DSC, RNR) joined the convoy coming from Benghazi.
[We have been unable to find out when HMS Falk parted company with the convoy and returned to Port Said. Most likely she was with the convoy only rather briefly.]
[We have also been unable to find out when HMS Whitehaven parted company with the convoy.]

At 1200 A/17, in position 36°29'N, 15°43'E, the following merchant vessels were detached to Augusta; Comedian, Director, Empire Falstaff, Empire Farmer, Fort Yale, Samarina, Sampa, Samphire and Samshire.

At 1330 A/17, in position 36°31'N, 15°26'E, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Augusta; Amos Kendall (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6404 GRT, built 1943), Cap Padaran (British (former French), 8009 GRT, built 1922), Empire Darwin (British, 6765 GRT, built 1941), Empire Zephyr (British, 6327 GRT, built 1941), Fort Ash (British, 7131 GRT, built 1943), Fort Carillon (British, 7129 GRT, built 1943), Fort Gibraltar (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Gloucester (British, 7127 GRT, built 1943), Fort Grahame (British, 7133 GRT, built 1943), Fort Souris (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Wellington (British, 7131 GRT, built 1943), Francis Marion (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Glaisdale (British, 3777 GRT, built 1929), Ile de Brehat (French, 6176 GRT, built 1919), Indiana (Panamanian (former French), 5751 GRT, built 1917), James G. Birney (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), James J. Hill (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), James J. Pettigrew (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), James Whitcomb Riley (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John Rutledge (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), John W. Brown (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), M. M. Guhin (American, 7180 GRT, built 1943), Marigot (French, 4047 GRT, built 1932), Ocean Faith (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Pocahontas (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Shickshinny (American, 5103 GRT, built 1919), Thaddeus Kosciuszko (American, 7200 GRT, built 1942), Thorshov (Norwegian (tanker), 9955 GRT, built 1935) and Toltec (Honduran, 5527 GRT, built 1929).

At 1630 A/17, in position 36°28'N, 14°56'E, the following merchant vessels were detached to Malta; Cap Padaran and Rodsley while the following vessels joined the convoy coming from Malta; The submarine HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN), corvettes HMS Honeysuckle (Lt. H.H.D. MacKillican, DSC and Bar, RNR), HMS Oxlip (Lt. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and the A/S trawler HMS Retriever (T/A/Lt.Cdr. G.E. Geeve, RNR). The corvette HMS Jonquil (T/Lt. R.W. Tretheway, RNR) had also come with these ships from Malta but she did not join the convoy but escorted the two merchant vessels that parted company with the convoy to Malta.

At 2200 A/18, off Bizerta the following merchant vessels parted company with the convoy; Amos Kendall, Empire Zephyr. Fort Carillon, Fort Souris, Francis Marion, James G. Birney, James J. Hill, James Whitcomb Riley, John Rutledge, Shickshinny, Thaddeus Kosciuszko, Thorshov and Toltec while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy; Atlantic Coast (British, 890 GRT, built 1934), Benjamin Tay (British, 1814 GRT, built 1943), Chloris British, 1171 GRT, built 1910), Empire Gareth (British, 2847 GRT, built 1942), Empire Newton (British, 7037 GRT, built 1942), Lochee (British, 964 GRT, built 1937), Ocean Vengeance (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942) and Pilar de Larrinaga (British, 7352 GRT, built 1918). Also the AA ship HMS Palomares (A/Capt.(Retd.) J.H. Jauncey, RN) reinforced the escort of the convoy.

At 1200 A/19, in position 37°17'N, 07°59'E, the following merchant vessels parted company with the convoy to proceed to Bone; Fort Ash and while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bone; Danio (Norwegian, 3496 GRT, built 1924), Fort Grant (British, 7133 GRT, built 1943), Kelbergen (Dutch, 4823 GRT, built 1914) and Ocean Fame (British, 7173 GRT, built 1942). Also the escort destroyer HMS Calpe (Lt.Cdr. H. Kirkwood, DSC, RN) joined the convoy as additional escort.

At 1700 A/19, off Philippeville, the merchant vessels Chloris, Fort Grant and Ocean Fame parted company with the convoy while the merchant vessel George C. Childress (American, 7245 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy. The Atlantic Coast also put into Philippeville, apparently having straggled from the convoy.

At 0400 A/20, off Bougie, the merchant vessels Benjamin Tay, Empire Newton, Glaisdale, Ocean Vengeance and Ocean Faith parted company with the convoy. [According to the Commodore report only two merchant vessels parted company with the convoy off Bougie but the merchant vessel shipping cards of these six ship indicated that they all went to Bougie.]

At 0700 A/21, off Algiers, the merchant vessels Andrea Gritti, Fort Gibraltar, Fort Gloucester, George C. Childress, Lochee, Magdala and Marigot parted company with the convoy as did the escort destroyer RHS Miaoulis. The merchant vessel Indiana apparently also put into Algiers after having straggled from the convoy. The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Cochrane (British, 7203 GRT, built 1923), Empire Scout (British, 2185 GRT, built 1936), Empire Snipe (British, 2497 GRT, built 1919), Ninella (British (tanker), 8134 GRT, built 1943), Thraki (Greek, 7460 GRT, built 1941) and Topsdalfjord (Norwegian, 4271 GRT, built 1921). The Empire Scout however returned to Algiers with engine trouble.

At 0900 A/22, off Oran the following merchant vessels parted company with the convoy; Ile de Brehat, James J. Pettigrew, John W. Brown, M. M. Guhin and Pocahontas while the merchant vessels Eskdalegate (British, 4250 GRT, built 1930), Pendeen (British, 4174 GRT, built 1923) and Slemmestad (Norwegian, 4258 GRT, built 1928) joined the convoy coming from Oran.

Early in the afternoon twelve USN LST's also joined the convoy, this were the following; USS LST-2 (T/Lt. W.G. Newhard, USN), USS LST-308 (Lt.Cdr. S.B. Purdie, USNR), USS LST-311 (Lt.Cdr. R.L. Coleman, USNR), USS LST-312 (T/Lt. C.L. Haslup, USN), USS LST-344 (T/Lt. R.B. Hensley, USN), USS LST-345 (Lt.Cdr. D.H. Adams, USNR), USS LST-346 (Lt. J.Z. Howell, USNR), USS LST-370 (Lt.Cdr. D.M. Hicks, USNR), USS LST-371 (T/Lt. A.W. Melohusky, USN), USS LST-380 (Lt. M.A. Goodrich, USNR), USS LST-382 (Lt.(jg) B.E. Toon, USNR) and USS LST-400 (Lt. C.J. Lyden, USNR).

Around 1445 A/23, the following merchant vessel parted company with the convoy; Cochrane, Empire Darwin, Fort Grahame, Fort Wellington, Ninella, Thraki and Topsdalfjord. Also parting company were HMS Unrivalled and HMS Palomares.
HMS Honeysuckle and HMS Oxlip also parted company to refuel at Gibraltar on completion of which they were to rejoin the convoy which after passing Gibraltar would be known as convoy MKS 31(G).

The remainder of the convoy, now known as convoy MKS 31(G), continued into the Atlantic, with the addition of the Gibraltar section of convoy MKS 31(G), to make rendezvous with convoy SL 141 coming from Freetown after which the convoy would continue to the UK as the combined convoy SL 141 / MKS 31(G). (47)
17 Nov 1943 At 1045 A/17, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. She was to proceed to the U.K. to refit. Passage to Gibraltar was made in convoy MKS 31.
[For more info on convoy MKS 31 see the event ' Convoy MKS 31 ' for 12 November 1943.]
23 Nov 1943 Around 1630 A/23 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. On entering harbour D/G trials were carried out. (5)
24 Nov 1943 At 1300 A/24, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Blyth.

She was routed as follows; Position 'P' (35°49'N, 06°06'30"W) at 1830 A/24. Position 'Q' (35°53'N, 06°15'W) at 1930 A/24. Position 'R' (36°37'N, 07°24'W) at 0540 A/25. Position 'S' (36°51'N, 09°13'W) at 0300 A/26. Position 'T' (42°00'N, 13°10'W) at 0700 A/27. Position 'G' (43°00'N, 12°54'W at 0010 A/30. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (48)

26 Nov 1943 At 1640 A/26, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1155 A/26, which ordered her to proceed by route 'Green' and make rendezvous with the escort 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles at 1100 A/5. (48)

3 Dec 1943 At 2040 A/3, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2001 A/3 to the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which she reported she would be 24 hours late at the rendezvous position.
The receipt of this signal was acknowledged by the F.O.S. in his signal timed 2324 A/3 in which also the bombing restrictions were amended. (5)

At 0745 A/6, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 0745 A/6 to the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.), HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) C.M. Norman, RN), HMS Trident (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN), in which HMS Unrivalled reported that she would be two hours late at the rendezvous, 1300 A/6 instead of 1100 A/6.
At 1247 A/6, in a signal timed 1218 A/6, HMS Unrivalled reported that she would only be at the rendezvous at 1600 A/6. [This was due to a gyro compass failure.]
At 1805 A/6, HMS Unrivalled sighted HMS La Caprieuse and rendezvous was effected 20 minutes later. The three submarines and their escort then proceeded northwards through the Irish Sea. (5)
9 Dec 1943 Around 0230 A/9, HMS Trident (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) all arrived at Tobermory.
The submarines departed around 1000 A/9 for the east coast of the U.K. now escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (49)

11 Dec 1943 Off Dundee HMS ML 229 (T/Lt. W.C.R. Walsh, RNVR) took over the escort. HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS Trident (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN) and ML 229 then continued on to Blyth.
12 Dec 1943 At 0945 A/12, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) arrived at Blyth where she was to refit. (5)
23 Dec 1943 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) is taken in hand for refit at Blyth. (50)
20 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted post refit trials off Blyth. (51)
21 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted post refit trials off Blyth. Upon completion of these she proceeded to South Shields where she ran over the D/G range. (51)
22 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) proceeded from South Shields to Blyth. On departure she ran over the D/G range twice. En-route to Blyth full power trials were carried out. (51)
24 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (51)
25 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Blyth. These included a practice attack on HMS Tribune (Lt.Cdr. W.N. Eade, RNR). (51)
27 Apr 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Blyth. These included a practice attack on HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN). (51)
28 Apr 1944 At 0900 B/28, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Blyth for Rothesay. She was escorted by HMS Castlenau (Skr. W.G. Mickleburgh, RNR). (51)

29 Apr 1944 In the Pentland Firth, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR), made rendezvous with FFS Junon (Lt. E. Schlumberger). HMS Portchester Castle (Lt. A.G. Scott, RNR) then took over the escort. (51)
1 May 1944 Around 0900 A/1, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) arrived at Rothesay from Blyth. She was now assigned to training duties being part of the 7th Submarine Flotilla. (52)
3 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted gunnery exercises and speed trials in the Clyde area. (52)
4 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted practice attacks in the Clyde area during which HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. E.M. Mackay, RD, RNR) acted as the target. (52)
5 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (52)
7 May 1944 At 1000 B/7, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Rothesay for Heysham. She was escorted by HMS Mistral (Lt. J.H.M. Sowry, RNVR). (52)
8 May 1944 At 1000 B/8, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) arrived at Heysham from Rothesay.
HMS Mistral (Lt. J.H.M. Sowry, RNVR) had parted company at 0736 B/8, when HMS ML 149 (T/Lt. W.H. Mitchell, RNVR) took over the escort.
At 1300 B/8, HMS Unrivalled departed Heysham for exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
9 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
10 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
11 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
12 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
13 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)
14 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises with aircraft off Morecambe Bay. (52)

15 May 1944 At 0600 B/15, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) was joined off Morecambe Bay by her escort towards Rothesay, HMS Brontes (T/Skr. B. Truefitt, RNR). They arrived at Rothesay later the same day around 2345 B/15. (52)
25 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Rothesay for Stornoway. She was escorted by HMS Blade (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR). (52)
26 May 1944 At 1645 B/26, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) arrived at Stornoway from Rothesay. (52)
30 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with a trawler (not identified) on which two practice attacks were made. (52)

31 May 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Stornoway with aircraft. Before these exercises a practice attack was made on HMS Bellwort (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.V. Banks, RNVR). (52)

1 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with a trawler (not identified) on which two practice attacks were made. Also gunnery exercises were carried out. (53)
2 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with a trawler (not identified) on which three practice attacks were made. (53)
5 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway. (53)
7 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with a trawler (not identified) on which two practice attacks were made. (53)
8 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway. These included a long range practice attack on an escort carrier.
This must have been HMS Searcher (Capt. G.O.C. Davies, RN) which was on passage from Scapa Flow to Greenock. (53)

11 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) briefly conducted exercises off Stornoway with a trawler but the exercises were broken off due to bad visibility making practice attacks not possible. (53)
12 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway. These included a practice attack on HMS Kingston Peridot (A/Skr.Lt. J.T. Denoon, RNR). (53)

13 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with HMS Kingston Peridot (A/Skr.Lt. J.T. Denoon, RNR). The exercises included a practice attack on the trawler and also A/S exercises. (53)

14 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with HMS Kingston Peridot (A/Skr.Lt. J.T. Denoon, RNR). These included a practice attack on the trawler, A/S exercises and an exercise by the boarding party of the trawler. Later on this day the submarine carried out A/S exercises with HMS Neil Mackay (Skr. T. Normandale, RNR). (53)

15 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with HMS Kingston Peridot (A/Skr.Lt. J.T. Denoon, RNR) and HMS Neil Mackay (Skr. T. Normandale, RNR). These included attack and A/S exercises. (53)
16 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with HNoMS Farsund (?). (53)

17 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Stornoway with HNoMS Farsund (?) and HMS Kingston Onyx (A/Skr.Lt. H.L. Gladwell, RNR). These exercises included A/S exercises and also a boarding exercise by the boarding parties of the two trawlers. (53)
18 Jun 1944 At 0750 B/18, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Stornoway for Tobermory where she arrived at 2315 B/18. She was escorted by HMS Clorinde (T/Lt. M.L. Patterson, RNVR). (53)

21 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Mermaid (Lt.Cdr. J.P. Mosse, RN). Also a practice attack was carried out by the submarine on HMS Mermaid. (53)

22 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Mermaid (Lt.Cdr. J.P. Mosse, RN). Also a practice attack was carried out by the submarine on HMS Pevensey Castle. (53)

29 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Bamborough Castle (T/Lt. M.S. Work, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS Peacock (Lt.Cdr. R.B. Stannard, VC, DSO, RD, RNR). (53)

30 Jun 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Bamborough Castle (T/Lt. M.S. Work, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS Peacock (Lt.Cdr. R.B. Stannard, VC, DSO, RD, RNR). Also a practice attack was carried out by the submarine on HMS Peacock. (53)

2 Jul 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Hurst Castle (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS Bamborough Castle (T/Lt. M.S. Work, DSC and Bar, RNR). Also a practice attack was made on HMS Bamborough Castle. (54)

3 Jul 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Bamborough Castle (T/Lt. M.S. Work, DSC and Bar, RNR). Also the submarine made a practice attack on the corvette. (54)

4 Jul 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR) and HMS Cowslip (T/Lt. R. Thompson, RNR). Also a practice attack was made on HMS Cowslip. (54)

5 Jul 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted gunnery exercises off Tobermory. She also made a practice attack on HMS Varangian (Lt. G.J. Gellie, RANVR) which was conducting independent exercises in the same area. (54)
7 Jul 1944 At 1810 B/7, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Tobermory for Rothesay. She was initially escorted by HMS ML 453 (T/Lt. L.V. Horne, RNVR).
At 1830 B/7, HMS Blade (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) took over the escort. (54)
8 Jul 1944 At 1050 B/8, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) arrived at Rothesay from Tobermory. (54)
18 Jul 1944 At 1000 B/18, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Rothesay for Barrow. She was escorted by HMS Istria (A/Skr.Lt. A.E. Larner, RNR). (54)
19 Jul 1944 At 1230 B/19, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) arrived at Barrow to repair a defective main motor at her builders yard. (54)
29 Aug 1944 At 0730 B/29, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Barrow for Rothesay where she arrived at 2245 B/29. She was escorted by HMS Kihna (Cdr.(Retd.) T.J.T.C. Jenks, RN). (55)
8 Sep 1944 In the evening, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted gunnery exercises in the Clyde area on a target that was towed by HMS Blade (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR). (56)
9 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted practice attacks in the Clyde area on HMS H 50 (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) which was en-route from Campbeltown to Rothesay. (56)
10 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. These included a practice attack on a large merchant vessel. (56)
11 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (56)
12 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted D/G trials on the degaussing range off Helensburgh. (56)
13 Sep 1944 At 0830 B/13, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) departed Rothesay for Larne where she arrived at 1630 B/13. (56)
15 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. Presumably these were with an escort group that was to protect a 'convoy'. (56)
16 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Larne with, at least, HMS Peacock (Lt.Cdr. R.B. Stannard, VC, DSO, RD, RNR). (56)
17 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (56)
19 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft. (56)
20 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. Presumably these were with an escort group that was to protect a 'convoy'. (56)

23 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (56)
25 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft from HMS Tracker (A/Capt. J.H. Huntley, RN). (56)
26 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft. (56)
28 Sep 1944 At Larne, HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) was fitted with a dummy snorkel. (56)
29 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S (snorkel) exercises off Larne with ships from the 2nd Escort Group and also with aircraft.

The ships of the 2nd Escort Group at that time were the sloops HMS Wild Goose (A/Cdr. D.E.G. Wemyss, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Starling (Cdr.(Retd.) G.W.E. Castens, DSO, RN), HMS Wren (Lt.Cdr. S.R.J. Woods, DSC, RD, RNR) and the frigate HMS Loch Fada (Lt.Cdr. B.A. Rogers, RD, RNR). (56)
30 Sep 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (56)
4 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. Presumably these were with an escort group that was to protect a 'convoy'. (57)
5 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Larne with ships from the 10th Escort Group.

The 10th Escort Group was made up of the sloop HMS Whimbrel (Cdr. E. Hewitt, RD, RNR) and the frigates HMS Bayntun (Lt.Cdr. L.P. Bourke, RNZNR), HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. P.J. Stoner, RN), HMS Foley (Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR), HMS Helmsdale (Cdr. P.W. Burnett, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Loch Dunvegan (Cdr. E. Wheeler, RD, RNR). (57)
6 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted exercises off Larne with ships from the 10th Escort Group.
7 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (57)
9 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (57)
10 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft from HMS Vindex (Capt. H.T.T. Bayliss, RN). (57)

12 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (57)
13 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) conducted A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft from HMS Premier (A/Capt. R.J. Gardner, RN). (57)
14 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (57)
15 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne with aircraft. (57)
16 Oct 1944 HMS Unrivalled (Lt. D.S. Brown, RNVR) participated in A/S exercises off Larne. (57)
